topics.title: Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals - Elementary Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Time and Discrete Time Signals - Elementary Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is a function that conveys information about the state or behavior of a physical system. Mathematically, a signal is represented as a function of one or more independent variables (most commonly time).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A signal is a mathematical description of a phenomenon (e.g., voltage, current, temperature, pressure, sound) as a function of an independent variable (typically time $t$ or $n$).

### 1.1.1 Two Fundamental Classes of Signals

| Type | Independent Variable | Notation | Example Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Continuous-Time (CT) Signal** | Time $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (real, continuous) | $x(t)$ | Speech waveform, ECG |
| **Discrete-Time (DT) Signal** | Time $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integer indices) | $x[n]$ | Stock price daily, digital audio |

## 1.2 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time — The Core Distinction

A signal is **continuous-time** if its value is defined for **every instant** of time, while a signal is **discrete-time** if its value is defined only at **discrete instants** (usually integer multiples of a sampling interval).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** "Continuous-time" refers to the **continuity of the independent variable** (time), NOT the continuity of the amplitude. A signal can have continuous time with discrete amplitude (e.g., a digital step) or discrete time with continuous amplitude (e.g., sampled analog audio).

### Conceptual Analogy: The Thermometer vs. The Camera

Imagine two devices recording the outside temperature:

- **A mercury thermometer (CT signal):** It continuously responds to temperature changes. You can walk up at **any** moment and read the value. The reading evolves smoothly over time. This is a continuous-time signal $x(t)$.
- **A security camera taking snapshots every hour (DT signal):** It only records the temperature at specific instants: 9:00 AM, 10:00 AM, 11:00 AM, ... You cannot ask "what was the temperature at 9:37 AM?" — that information simply does not exist. This is a discrete-time signal $x[n]$.

> [!NOTE]
> The independent variable brackets matter in KTU exams: use **parentheses ( )** for continuous-time $x(t)$ and **square brackets [ ]** for discrete-time $x[n]$. Examiners deduct marks for incorrect notation.

## 1.3 What is an "Elementary Signal"?

**Elementary signals** are the fundamental building blocks from which more complex signals can be constructed using operations like shifting, scaling, and superposition. They serve as the "alphabet" of signal processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Listing — The Eight Elementary Signals (Most Frequently Asked):**
> 1. Unit Step Signal — $u(t)$, $u[n]$
> 2. Unit Impulse Signal — $\delta(t)$, $\delta[n]$
> 3. Unit Ramp Signal — $r(t)$, $r[n]$
> 4. Exponential Signal — $e^{at}$, $e^{an}$
> 5. Sinusoidal Signal — $\sin(\omega t)$, $\cos(\omega t)$, $\sin[\Omega n]$, $\cos[\Omega n]$
> 6. Complex Exponential — $e^{j\omega t}$, $e^{j\Omega n}$
> 7. Signum Function — $\text{sgn}(t)$
> 8. Sinc Function — $\text{sinc}(t) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unit Step Signal

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time Unit Step $u(t)$

The CT unit step signal is defined as:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

At $t = 0$, the value is technically undefined (or taken as 1/2 by some conventions, or 1 by others). In KTU problems, the default is **$u(0) = 1$**.

**Why does this matter?** The unit step acts as a "switch-on" function. Multiplying any signal $x(t)$ by $u(t)$ makes it zero for all $t < 0$ and leaves it unchanged for $t \geq 0$. It is the foundation for studying **causal systems** (those that respond only to present/past inputs).

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time Unit Step $u[n]$

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

Unlike the continuous case, $u[0]$ is **unambiguously defined as 1** in discrete-time.

### 2.1.3 Relationship Between Step and Ramp

The unit step is the **derivative** of the unit ramp, and the ramp is the **integral** of the step:

$$r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau)\,d\tau \quad ; \quad u(t) = \frac{dr(t)}{dt}$$

## 2.2 The Unit Impulse Signal

### 2.2.1 Continuous-Time Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ — The Dirac Delta

The CT unit impulse is a **generalized function** (distribution), not a regular function. It has zero width, infinite height, and a total area of one. It is formally defined by two properties:

$$\delta(t) = 0 \text{ for } t \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$$

**Equivalent construction (limit of a rectangle):**
$$\delta(t) = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{1}{\epsilon}\,\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{\epsilon}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Sifting Property** (most important impulse identity for KTU):
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$$
> This property allows the impulse to "pick out" or "sift" the value of a signal at any instant $t_0$. It is the cornerstone of convolution and LTI system analysis.

### 2.2.2 Discrete-Time Unit Impulse $\delta[n]$

The DT unit impulse is far more elementary — it is a perfectly well-defined ordinary sequence:

$$\delta[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sampling Property:**
$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\,\delta[n - n_0] = x[n_0]$$

### 2.2.3 Relationship Between Step and Impulse

$$u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau \quad ; \quad \delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}$$

In discrete-time, the analogous relationship is a **first difference**, not a derivative:

$$u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta[k] \quad ; \quad \delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$$

## 2.3 The Unit Ramp Signal

$$r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases} \quad ; \quad r[n] = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** The ramp can be expressed in terms of the step:
> $$r(t) = t \cdot u(t) \quad ; \quad r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$$

## 2.4 Exponential Signals

### 2.4.1 Continuous-Time Real Exponential $x(t) = e^{at}$

| Value of $a$ | Behavior | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $a > 0$ | Growing exponential | $e^{at}$ grows without bound |
| $a < 0$ | Decaying exponential | $e^{at}$ decays to zero (energy signal) |
| $a = 0$ | Constant $x(t) = 1$ | DC signal |

### 2.4.2 Discrete-Time Real Exponential $x[n] = a^n$

Unlike CT, DT exponentials exhibit fundamentally different behavior depending on $|a|$:

| Value of $a$ | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\vert a \vert > 1$ | Growing |
| $\vert a \vert < 1$ | Decaying |
| $\vert a \vert = 1$ | Constant magnitude |
| $a = -1$ | Alternates $\pm 1$ |
| $a = 0$ | Zero everywhere except $n=0$ |

## 2.5 Sinusoidal Signals

### 2.5.1 Continuous-Time Sinusoid

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

- $A$ = amplitude (in volts, amperes, etc.)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency in **rad/s** (continuous)
- $\phi$ = phase in **radians**
- Period $T_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ in **seconds**

### 2.5.2 Discrete-Time Sinusoid

$$x[n] = A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$$

- $\Omega$ = **discrete** angular frequency in **radians/sample**
- Period $N_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega}$ in **samples** (must be an integer for periodicity)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A discrete-time sinusoid is **periodic only if** $\dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega}$ is a **rational multiple of $\pi$**. Otherwise it is aperiodic. Example: $\cos(0.5n)$ is aperiodic because $2\pi/0.5 = 4\pi$ is irrational.

## 2.6 The Complex Exponential Signal (Euler's Form)

The **single most important signal** in signals and systems:

$$x(t) = e^{j\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Using Euler's identity, **any** real sinusoid can be reconstructed from two complex exponentials:
> $$A\cos(\omega t + \phi) = \frac{A}{2}\,e^{j\phi}\,e^{j\omega t} + \frac{A}{2}\,e^{-j\phi}\,e^{-j\omega t}$$
> This decomposition is the gateway to the **Fourier Series** and **Fourier Transform**.

## 2.7 The Signum Function $\text{sgn}(t)$

$$\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ -1, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Relationship to step:** $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$ (with $u(0) = 1$).

## 2.8 The Sinc Function

$$\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$$

- Peak value: $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$
- Zeros at all nonzero integers: $\text{sinc}(k) = 0$ for $k \in \mathbb{Z} \setminus \{0\}$
- Central role in **interpolation** and the **sampling theorem**.

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Signal | CT Definition | DT Definition | Key Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Unit Step** | $u(t) = 1, t>0$; $0, t<0$ | $u[n] = 1, n\geq 0$; $0, n<0$ | "Switch-on" function |
| 2 | **Unit Impulse** | $\delta(t)$: area=1, zero elsewhere | $\delta[n] = 1$ at $n=0$ | Sifting property |
| 3 | **Unit Ramp** | $r(t) = t\cdot u(t)$ | $r[n] = n\cdot u[n]$ | Integral of step |
| 4 | **Exponential** | $e^{at}$ | $a^n$ | Energy/Power class |
| 5 | **Sinusoid** | $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | $A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ | Period $= 2\pi/\omega$ |
| 6 | **Complex Exp** | $e^{j\omega t}$ | $e^{j\Omega n}$ | Eigenfunction of LTI |
| 7 | **Signum** | $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$ | — | Odd function |
| 8 | **Sinc** | $\text{sinc}(t) = \sin(\pi t)/(\pi t)$ | — | Sampling/reconstruction |

### Critical Derivative / Difference Identities

$$u(t) \xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} \delta(t) \quad ; \quad r(t) \xrightarrow{\frac{d}{dt}} u(t)$$

$$u[n] - u[n-1] = \delta[n] \quad ; \quad u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n}\delta[k]$$

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Signal | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Unit step $u(t)$ | Modeling switch activation, system turn-on, causality tests |
| Unit impulse $\delta(t)$ | Shock testing, impulse response, sampling theory |
| Exponential $e^{at}$ | RC/RL circuit discharge, radioactive decay, growth models |
| Sinusoid | AC power systems, audio signals, vibration analysis |
| Complex exponential $e^{j\omega t}$ | Phasor analysis, Fourier analysis, communication carriers |
| Sinc function | Ideal reconstruction filter in digital-to-analog conversion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Unit Impulse as a Limiting Rectangle

The CT unit impulse cannot be defined as an ordinary function (its value is "infinite" at one point). Instead, it is defined as a **limit** of a family of well-behaved functions. The classic construction uses a rectangle of area 1.

**Step 1: Define a rectangular pulse $p_\epsilon(t)$ of width $\epsilon$ and height $1/\epsilon$**

$$p_\epsilon(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{\epsilon}, & -\frac{\epsilon}{2} < t < \frac{\epsilon}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Verify the area is unity (this is the defining property of an impulse)**

$$\text{Area} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p_\epsilon(t)\,dt = \int_{-\epsilon/2}^{+\epsilon/2} \frac{1}{\epsilon}\,dt = \frac{1}{\epsilon}\cdot\epsilon = 1$$

**Step 3: Take the limit as $\epsilon \to 0$ (width collapses, height explodes, area stays 1)**

$$\delta(t) = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} p_\epsilon(t)$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

$$\delta(t) = 0 \text{ for } t \neq 0 \quad ; \quad \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, you may also be asked to derive the impulse as the **limit of a triangle** or **limit of a Gaussian**, but the rectangular derivation is the most commonly expected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Sifting Property of the Impulse

We want to prove:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$$

**Step 1: Use the defining property.** $\delta(t - t_0) = 0$ everywhere except at $t = t_0$. Therefore, the integrand $x(t)\delta(t - t_0)$ is zero everywhere except in an infinitesimally small neighborhood around $t = t_0$.

**Step 2: Use the fact that $x(t)$ is continuous (assumed).** For sufficiently small $\epsilon$ around $t_0$, $x(t)$ is essentially constant at $x(t_0)$. Pull it out of the integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)\int_{t_0-\epsilon}^{t_0+\epsilon}\delta(t - t_0)\,dt$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the remaining integral.** Since the integral of the impulse over any interval containing $t_0$ is 1:

$$= x(t_0) \cdot 1 = x(t_0)$$

**Conclusion:** The impulse "sifts out" the value of $x(t)$ at $t = t_0$. This is the foundation for the convolution integral and for representing arbitrary signals as weighted sums of impulses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DT Impulse as a First Difference of DT Step

We want to show:
$$\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$$

**Step 1: Write out the definition of $u[n]$ explicitly.**

$$u[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Shift the index by 1 to get $u[n-1]$.**

$$u[n-1] = \begin{cases} 1, & n - 1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow n \geq 1 \\ 0, & n - 1 < 0 \Rightarrow n < 1 \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 1 \\ 0, & n \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Subtract the two sequences term-by-term.**

$$u[n] - u[n-1] = \begin{cases} 1 - 0, & n = 0 \\ 1 - 1, & n \geq 1 \\ 0 - 0, & n < 0 \end{cases} = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases} = \delta[n]$$

**Conclusion:** Verified. The first difference of the step gives the impulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** In CT, the relationship is a **derivative** $\frac{du}{dt} = \delta(t)$. In DT, the analogous operation is a **first difference** $\Delta u[n] = u[n] - u[n-1] = \delta[n]$. This asymmetry is a frequent KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Period of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

Given: $x[n] = A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$. We want the smallest positive integer $N$ such that $x[n+N] = x[n]$.

**Step 1: Set up the periodicity condition.**

$$A\cos(\Omega(n+N) + \phi) = A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$$

**Step 2: Use the property that $\cos(\theta) = \cos(\theta + 2\pi k)$ for any integer $k$.**

$$\Omega(n+N) + \phi = \Omega n + \phi + 2\pi k$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$\Omega N = 2\pi k \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega}$$

**Step 4: Find the smallest positive integer $N$.**

$$N_0 = \min\left\{k \in \mathbb{Z}^+ : \frac{2\pi k}{\Omega} \in \mathbb{Z}^+\right\}$$

This is an integer if and only if $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi}$ is a **rational number**, i.e., $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{p}{q}$ with $p, q \in \mathbb{Z}$. In that case, $N_0 = q$.

**Conclusion:**
- If $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{p}{q}$ (rational) → $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N_0 = q$.
- If $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi}$ is irrational → $x[n]$ is **aperiodic**.

**Example:** $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$. Here $\Omega = 0.5\pi$, so $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.5\pi}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{4}$. This is rational ($p=1, q=4$). Therefore, $N_0 = 4$ samples. Verify: $\cos(0.5\pi(0)) = 1$, $\cos(0.5\pi(4)) = \cos(2\pi) = 1$. ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Expressing a Rectangular Pulse Using Step Functions

A rectangular pulse of width 1, centered at $t = 0$:

$$p(t) = u\!\left(t + \frac{1}{2}\right) - u\!\left(t - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step-by-step logical construction:**

**Step 1:** $u(t + 1/2)$ switches ON at $t = -1/2$.
**Step 2:** $u(t - 1/2)$ switches ON at $t = +1/2$.
**Step 3:** Subtracting turns the second step OFF at $t = +1/2$, so the result is 1 in $[-1/2, +1/2)$ and 0 elsewhere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized KTU Result:** Any rectangular pulse from $t_1$ to $t_2$ can be written as:
> $$p(t) = u(t - t_1) - u(t - t_2)$$
> This is the **most frequently asked construction** in KTU Module 1 — be ready to draw the result.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Quick Identification of Elementary Signals

| If you see this... | It is a... | Key identifier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A signal that is 1 for $t \geq 0$, 0 elsewhere | **Unit step** $u(t)$ | Sudden jump at $t=0$ |
| An infinitely tall, infinitesimally thin spike | **Impulse** $\delta(t)$ | Area under curve = 1 |
| A signal that grows linearly for $t \geq 0$ | **Ramp** $r(t)$ | Slope = 1 |
| A signal $\propto e^{at}$ | **Exponential** | Rate parameter $a$ |
| A signal $\propto A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | **Sinusoid** | Oscillates periodically |
| A signal $\propto e^{j\omega t}$ | **Complex exponential** | Real part = cosine, imag = sine |
| A signal that is +1 for $t>0$ and -1 for $t<0$ | **Signum** | Odd symmetry about origin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Elementary Signals

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Elementary Signals"] --> nodeB["Continuous Time x of t"]
    nodeA["Elementary Signals"] --> nodeC["Discrete Time x of n"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Unit Step u t"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Unit Impulse delta t"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Unit Ramp r t"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Exponential e to a t"]
    nodeB --> nodeB5["Sinusoid cos wt"]
    nodeB --> nodeB6["Complex Exp e to j w t"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Unit Step u of n"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Unit Impulse delta of n"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Unit Ramp r of n"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Exponential a to n"]
    nodeC --> nodeC5["Sinusoid cos Omega n"]
    nodeC --> nodeC6["Complex Exp e to j Omega n"]
    nodeB2["Unit Impulse delta t"] -.Derivative of.-> nodeB1
    nodeB1["Unit Step u t"] -.Integral of.-> nodeB3
    nodeB1["Unit Step u t"] -.2u-1.-> nodeSgn["Signum sgn t"]
    nodeB6["Complex Exp"] -.Euler.-> nodeB5
```

**Reading the diagram:** The eight elementary signals split into two parallel families (CT and DT). Within each family, the step, impulse, and ramp are linked by calculus/difference operations. The complex exponential is the "parent" of the real sinusoid via Euler's formula.

---

## 4.2 Waveform Schematic: Building a Rectangular Pulse from Step Functions

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeS1["u t plus 0.5"] --> nodeSum["Sum point: minus"]
    nodeS2["u t minus 0.5"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeOut["Rectangular Pulse p t"]
    nodeS1["u t plus 0.5"] -.Step ON at t = -0.5.-> nodeS1
    nodeS2["u t minus 0.5"] -.Step ON at t = +0.5.-> nodeS2
    nodeOut["Rectangular Pulse p t"] -.Equals 1 between minus 0.5 and plus 0.5.-> nodeOut
```

**Interpretation:** A rectangular pulse of width 1 centered at the origin is constructed by turning a step ON at $t = -0.5$ and OFF at $t = +0.5$ via subtraction.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Step, Impulse, Ramp Inter-relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRamp["Unit Ramp r t equals t u t"] --> nodeDer["Differentiate d by dt"]
    nodeRamp["Unit Ramp r t equals t u t"] --> nodeInt["Integrate from minus infinity"]
    nodeDer["Differentiate d by dt"] --> nodeStep["Unit Step u t"]
    nodeInt["Integrate from minus infinity"] --> nodeStep["Unit Step u t"]
    nodeStep["Unit Step u t"] --> nodeDer2["Differentiate d by dt"]
    nodeStep["Unit Step u t"] --> nodeInt2["Integrate from minus infinity"]
    nodeDer2["Differentiate d by dt"] --> nodeImp["Unit Impulse delta t"]
    nodeInt2["Integrate from minus infinity"] --> nodeImp["Unit Impulse delta t"]
```

**Interpretation:** This functional architecture shows that the three calculus operations (differentiate, integrate) form a closed loop among the ramp, step, and impulse signals. In discrete-time, the same loop exists but with "first difference" and "running sum" replacing the derivative and integral.

---

## 4.4 Information Flow Diagram: Decomposition of a Real Sinusoid

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Amplitude A"] --> nodeMul1["Multiply by e to j phi over 2"]
    nodeMul1["Multiply by e to j phi over 2"] --> nodeMul2["Multiply by e to j omega t"]
    nodeMul2["Multiply by e to j omega t"] --> nodeAdd["Add to conjugate term"]
    nodeB["Amplitude A"] --> nodeMul3["Multiply by e to minus j phi over 2"]
    nodeMul3["Multiply by e to minus j phi over 2"] --> nodeMul4["Multiply by e to minus j omega t"]
    nodeMul4["Multiply by e to minus j omega t"] --> nodeAdd["Add to conjugate term"]
    nodeAdd["Add to conjugate term"] --> nodeOut["Real Sinusoid A cos omega t plus phi"]
```

**Interpretation:** A real sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ is decomposed into **two complex exponentials** rotating in opposite directions. This is the conceptual heart of the Fourier Series.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define unit step and unit impulse functions. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

The continuous-time unit step signal is defined as $u(t) = 1$ for $t > 0$ and $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. The CT unit impulse $\delta(t)$ is a signal that is zero everywhere except at $t = 0$, with the property that $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$.

The relationship is that the unit step is the **running integral** of the unit impulse, and the unit impulse is the **derivative** of the unit step:

$$u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau \quad ; \quad \delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}$$

In discrete-time, the analogous relationship is a first difference: $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Determine whether the signal $x[n] = \cos(0.3\pi n)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.**

**Model Answer:**

For periodicity, we need the smallest positive integer $N$ such that $\dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega} \cdot \frac{k}{N}$ reduces to an integer. Here $\Omega = 0.3\pi$.

Compute $\dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.3\pi} = \dfrac{2}{0.3} = \dfrac{20}{3}$.

This is a rational number ($p/q$ form with $p = 20$, $q = 3$). So the signal **is periodic** with fundamental period $N_0 = 3$ samples.

**Verification:** $x[0] = \cos(0) = 1$; $x[3] = \cos(0.3\pi \cdot 3) = \cos(0.9\pi) \neq 1$. 

Let us recompute: $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.3\pi}{2\pi} = \dfrac{0.3}{2} = \dfrac{3}{20}$. The denominator is $q = 20$. So the fundamental period is $N_0 = 20$ samples.

**Verification:** $x[0] = \cos(0) = 1$; $x[20] = \cos(0.3\pi \cdot 20) = \cos(6\pi) = 1$. ✓

**Final Answer:** Periodic with $N_0 = 20$ samples. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Sketch the following continuous-time signals and express each as a combination of ramp and/or step functions:
- (i) $x_1(t) = u(t) - 2u(t-1) + u(t-2)$
- (ii) $x_2(t) = r(t) - 2r(t-1) + r(t-2)$ where $r(t) = t\cdot u(t)$

**(b)** Find whether the following signals are energy signals, power signals, or neither:
- (i) $x(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$
- (ii) $x[n] = (-1)^n$

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(a)(i) Signal $x_1(t) = u(t) - 2u(t-1) + u(t-2)$**

**Step 1: Analyze each step term.**

- $u(t)$: jumps from 0 to 1 at $t = 0$
- $-2u(t-1)$: drops by 2 at $t = 1$ (jumps from 0 to -2)
- $u(t-2)$: jumps from 0 to 1 at $t = 2$

**Step 2: Piecewise form.**

$$x_1(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 1 - 2 = -1, & 1 \leq t < 2 \\ -1 + 1 = 0, & t \geq 2 \end{cases}$$

**Step 3: Sketch.** A rectangular pulse: amplitude +1 from $t=0$ to $t=1$, then amplitude -1 from $t=1$ to $t=2$, then 0.

**(a)(ii) Signal $x_2(t) = r(t) - 2r(t-1) + r(t-2)$**

**Step 1: Use $r(t-a) = (t-a)u(t-a)$ and analyze piece by piece.**

- $r(t) = t$ for $t \geq 0$, else 0
- $-2r(t-1) = -2(t-1)$ for $t \geq 1$, else 0
- $r(t-2) = (t-2)$ for $t \geq 2$, else 0

**Step 2: Piecewise form.**

For $0 \leq t < 1$: $x_2(t) = t$

For $1 \leq t < 2$: $x_2(t) = t - 2(t-1) = t - 2t + 2 = -t + 2$

For $t \geq 2$: $x_2(t) = t - 2(t-1) + (t-2) = t - 2t + 2 + t - 2 = 0$

**Step 3: Sketch.** A triangular pulse: rises linearly from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$, then falls linearly from $(1,1)$ to $(2,0)$, then 0 for $t > 2$. This is a triangle of height 1 and base width 2. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**(b)(i) $x(t) = e^{-2t}\,u(t)$ — Energy or Power?**

**Step 1: Compute total energy.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\,dt = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-4t}\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{4}e^{-4t}\right]_0^{+\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 2: Since $0 < E < \infty$, it is an **energy signal** (and NOT a power signal).** **(3 marks)**

**(b)(ii) $x[n] = (-1)^n$ — Energy or Power?**

**Step 1: Compute total energy.**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} 1 = \infty$$

**Step 2: Compute average power.**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert (-1)^n \vert^2 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{2N+1}{2N+1} = 1$$

**Step 3: Since $E = \infty$ but $0 < P < \infty$, it is a **power signal**.** **(4 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define the CT and DT unit ramp signals. Express each in terms of the corresponding unit step signal. Show that the DT unit impulse can be obtained as a first difference of the DT unit step.

**(b)** For each of the following signals, determine whether it is periodic, and if so, find the fundamental period:
- (i) $x(t) = 2\cos(3t + \pi/4)$
- (ii) $x[n] = \cos(0.6\pi n) + \sin(0.5\pi n)$

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**Definition of CT and DT Unit Ramp:**

The CT unit ramp is $r(t) = t$ for $t \geq 0$ and $r(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. In terms of the unit step: $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$.

The DT unit ramp is $r[n] = n$ for $n \geq 0$ and $r[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. In terms of the DT step: $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$.

**Step-by-step proof that $\delta[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$:**

From the definition of $u[n]$:

$$u[n] = 1 \text{ for } n \geq 0, \quad 0 \text{ for } n < 0$$
$$u[n-1] = 1 \text{ for } n \geq 1, \quad 0 \text{ for } n < 1$$

Subtracting term-by-term:

$$u[n] - u[n-1] = \begin{cases} 1 - 0 = 1, & n = 0 \\ 1 - 1 = 0, & n \geq 1 \\ 0 - 0 = 0, & n < 0 \end{cases} = \delta[n]$$

Hence proved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Solution

**(b)(i) $x(t) = 2\cos(3t + \pi/4)$**

For CT sinusoids, $T_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ seconds.

**Periodic with period $T_0 = 2\pi/3$ seconds.** **(3 marks)**

**(b)(ii) $x[n] = \cos(0.6\pi n) + \sin(0.5\pi n)$**

**Step 1: Find period of the cosine term.**

$\Omega_1 = 0.6\pi$. Compute $\dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega_1} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.6\pi} = \dfrac{2}{0.6} = \dfrac{10}{3}$.

So $\dfrac{\Omega_1}{2\pi} = \dfrac{3}{10}$, giving $N_1 = 10$ samples.

**Step 2: Find period of the sine term.**

$\Omega_2 = 0.5\pi$. Compute $\dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega_2} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.5\pi} = 4$.

So $\dfrac{\Omega_2}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{4}$, giving $N_2 = 4$ samples.

**Step 3: Find the common period (LCM of 10 and 4).**

$\text{LCM}(10, 4) = 20$ samples.

**Periodic with fundamental period $N_0 = 20$ samples.** **(4 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Wrong bracket notation:** Using $x(t)$ for a DT signal or $x[n]$ for a CT signal — **lose 0.5 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing the period formula:** Writing $T = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ instead of $\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ — **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify periodicity for DT sinusoids:** Always check that $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi}$ is rational. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if you blindly apply $N = 2\pi/\Omega$ without checking that the result is an integer.
> 4. **Confusing derivative vs. first difference:** In CT, $u(t)$ and $\delta(t)$ are linked by derivative; in DT, $u[n]$ and $\delta[n]$ are linked by first difference. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping piecewise construction:** When asked to express a signal using steps, you must write out the piecewise form explicitly. A sketch alone gets partial credit at best.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Elementary Signals**

- [ ] **Notation discipline:** Always use $x(t)$ for CT and $x[n]$ for DT. KTU strictly enforces this.
- [ ] **Eight elementary signals** to memorize with definitions: step, impulse, ramp, exponential, sinusoid, complex exponential, signum, sinc.
- [ ] **The sifting property** is the single most important impulse identity:
  $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\delta(t-t_0)\,dt = x(t_0)$$
- [ ] **Step–Impulse–Ramp loop:**
  - CT: $r(t) \xrightarrow{d/dt} u(t) \xrightarrow{d/dt} \delta(t)$
  - DT: $r[n] \xleftarrow{\text{running sum}} u[n] \xrightarrow{\Delta} \delta[n]$
- [ ] **CT period formula:** $T_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ (in seconds).
- [ ] **DT period formula:** $N_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\Omega}$ in samples, **only if** $\dfrac{\Omega}{2\pi}$ is rational.
- [ ] **Euler's identity** is the bridge: $e^{j\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)$.
- [ ] **Energy vs. Power classification:**
  - Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$
  - Power signal: $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$
- [ ] **Rectangular pulse construction:** $p_{[a,b]}(t) = u(t-a) - u(t-b)$.
- [ ] **Signum-step relationship:** $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$.
- [ ] **Ramp-step relationship:** $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ and $r[n] = n \cdot u[n]$.
- [ ] **The sinc function** $\text{sinc}(t) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi t)}{\pi t}$ is the impulse response of the ideal low-pass filter and appears in sampling theory.
- [ ] **Sampling property (DT):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\delta[n-n_0] = x[n_0]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable(s) and conveys information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon. Formally, a signal is a **function of one or more independent variables** that carries information.

Mathematically, a signal $x$ is represented as a function:

$$x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x[n]$$

where $t$ denotes continuous (real-valued) time and $n$ denotes discrete (integer-valued) time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Strict):** A signal is a mathematical description of a physical phenomenon expressed as a function of one or more independent variables (typically time). Signals are the **carriers of information** in any communication, control, or processing system.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy
Think of a signal like the **temperature reading on a digital thermometer** 📈.
- As time passes (the independent variable), the temperature value (the signal) keeps changing.
- The thermometer *conveys information* — just like a signal does in an electrical circuit.
- If you sample the temperature every hour instead of every second, you get a *discrete* version of the same information — that's analogous to converting a continuous-time signal into a discrete-time signal.

## 1.2 What Do We Mean by "Classification of Signals"?

Signals can be categorized based on different properties:
1. **Nature of the independent variable** → Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time
2. **Nature of amplitude values** → Analog vs Digital
3. **Symmetry about the vertical axis** → Even vs Odd
4. **Repetition pattern** → Periodic vs Aperiodic
5. **Predictability of values** → Deterministic vs Random
6. **Energy/Power content** → Energy vs Power
7. **Causality (dependence on past/future)** → Causal, Anti-causal, Non-causal

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classification matters in KTU exams:** Most problems in Module 1 require you to *identify the type* of a given signal **before** you can analyze it. Examiners frequently test whether you can spot even/odd symmetry, periodicity, or energy/power nature in 2–3 mark questions.

---

## 1.3 Classification Based on the Independent Variable

### 1.3.1 Continuous-Time Signal $x(t)$
Defined for **every** instant of time. The independent variable $t$ is **continuous** and takes any real value in an interval.

**Example:** $x(t) = \sin(2\pi t)$, $x(t) = e^{-t}$, $x(t) = t^2$

### 1.3.2 Discrete-Time Signal $x[n]$
Defined only at **specific (discrete)** instants of time. The independent variable $n$ is an **integer** ($\mathbb{Z}$).

**Example:** $x[n] = \sin(0.2\pi n)$, $x[n] = (-0.5)^n$, $x[n] = n^2$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU commonly asks *"Given $x(t) = \sin(t)$, is it continuous-time or discrete-time?"* — the presence of the variable $t$ (real) vs $n$ (integer) decides this. Remember: the **variable symbol** matters, not just the function form.

---

## 1.4 Even and Odd Signals

### Even Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is **even** if it satisfies:

$$x(-t) = x(t) \quad \forall\, t$$

**Intuition:** The signal is **symmetric about the vertical axis** (the y-axis). Like a mirror reflection.

**Examples:** $\cos(t)$, $t^2$, $|t|$, constant signals

### Odd Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is **odd** if it satisfies:

$$x(-t) = -x(t) \quad \forall\, t$$

**Intuition:** The signal is **anti-symmetric** about the vertical axis. Like a $180^\circ$ rotation about the origin.

**Examples:** $\sin(t)$, $t$, $t^3$, signum function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every signal can be decomposed** into its even and odd parts:
> - **Even part:** $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$
> - **Odd part:** $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$
> - And $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$ always holds.

---

## 1.5 Periodic and Aperiodic Signals

### Periodic Signal
A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if there exists a positive constant $T$ (called the **fundamental period**) such that:

$$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \forall\, t$$

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is periodic if there exists a positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x[n + N] = x[n] \quad \forall\, n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A discrete-time signal $x[n] = \sin(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **if and only if** $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$ (i.e., $\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{p}{q}$ where $p, q$ are integers).

### Aperiodic (Non-periodic) Signal
A signal that does **not** repeat its values after any finite interval. It is also called a **non-periodic** signal.

**Examples of aperiodic signals:** $e^{-t}u(t)$, $e^{-|t|}$, $\sin(t)/t$ (sinc function)

---

## 1.6 Deterministic and Random Signals

### Deterministic Signal
A signal whose values are **completely known** at any time $t$ (or $n$) — no uncertainty.

**Example:** $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi t + \pi/4)$ — the value is known for any $t$.

### Random Signal
A signal whose values are **not known a priori** and are characterized using **statistical properties** (mean, variance, probability distribution).

**Example:** Thermal noise voltage in a resistor, speech signal, ECG signal.

---

## 1.7 Energy and Power Signals

### Energy Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is an **energy signal** if its total energy is **finite and non-zero**:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$

### Power Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is a **power signal** if its average power is **finite and non-zero**:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt > 0$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **A signal CANNOT be both an energy signal AND a power signal simultaneously.** A signal can be one, the other, or neither — but never both.

---

## 1.8 Causal, Anti-causal, and Non-causal Signals

### Causal Signal
A signal that is **zero for $t < 0$** (i.e., it does not depend on future values):

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad t < 0$$

**Example:** $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$

### Anti-causal Signal
A signal that is **zero for $t > 0$**:

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad t > 0$$

**Example:** $x(t) = e^{t} u(-t)$

### Non-causal (Two-sided) Signal
A signal that is **non-zero for both past and future times**.

**Example:** $x(t) = e^{-\vert t \vert}$ (defined for both positive and negative $t$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟨 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Each Classification Matters

| Classification | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|
| Continuous vs Discrete | Determines whether to use calculus or summation-based tools |
| Even vs Odd | Enables use of Fourier series (only cosines for even, sines for odd) |
| Periodic vs Aperiodic | Decides between Fourier series and Fourier transform |
| Deterministic vs Random | Picks deterministic or statistical analysis methods |
| Energy vs Power | Determines the right metric (energy for transient, power for steady-state) |
| Causal vs Non-causal | Critical for real-time system design (you can't access the future!) |

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Conditions for Each Type

### Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time

| Property | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| Notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| Operations use | Integrals, derivatives | Summations, differences |

### Even/Odd Conditions

| Type | Condition | Symmetry |
|---|---|---|
| Even | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | Mirror about y-axis |
| Odd | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | Origin anti-symmetry |

### Periodic Conditions

| Type | Condition |
|---|---|
| Continuous periodic | $\exists\, T > 0$ such that $x(t+T) = x(t)\, \forall t$ |
| Discrete periodic | $\exists\, N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ such that $x[n+N] = x[n]\, \forall n$ |

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Formulas — Memorize for KTU ESE 2024**

| # | Property | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Even signal | $x(-t) = x(t)$ | Symmetric about y-axis |
| 2 | Odd signal | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | Origin symmetric |
| 3 | Even-Odd decomposition | $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2}$ | Always valid |
| 4 | Even-Odd decomposition | $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2}$ | Always valid |
| 5 | Continuous periodic | $x(t+T) = x(t)$ | $T > 0$ — fundamental period |
| 6 | Discrete periodic | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 7 | Period of $\sin(\omega_0 t)$ | $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | radians/second |
| 8 | Period of $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $N = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}\cdot m$ | Need $\omega_0/2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| 9 | Energy of $x(t)$ | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$ | Joules (in 1 Ω) |
| 10 | Power of $x(t)$ | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$ | Watts (in 1 Ω) |
| 11 | Causal | $x(t) = 0,\, t < 0$ | Real-time realizable |
| 12 | Anti-causal | $x(t) = 0,\, t > 0$ | Past-only signal |
| 13 | Deterministic | Known $\forall\, t$ | No uncertainty |
| 14 | Random | Statistical description | Mean, variance, PDF |

> [!WARNING]
> **Watch out for the absolute value symbol** — in the formulas above, I used `\vert` (a LaTeX-friendly vertical bar) instead of `|` to keep the table rendering safe in markdown. In your exam paper, just write it normally as $|x(t)|^2$.

---

## 2.4 Periodicity of Sum of Periodic Signals

If $x_1(t)$ has period $T_1$ and $x_2(t)$ has period $T_2$, then $x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is periodic **if and only if** the ratio $T_1/T_2$ is a **rational number** $\dfrac{p}{q}$ where $p, q$ are positive integers.

The fundamental period is then:

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = m \cdot T_1 = n \cdot T_2$$

where $m, n$ are the smallest integers such that $mT_1 = nT_2$.

**Example:** If $T_1 = 2$ and $T_2 = 3$, then $T_1/T_2 = 2/3$ (rational), and the LCM is $T_0 = 6$.

---

## 2.5 Energy vs Power — Critical Distinctions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem:** A signal cannot be both an energy signal and a power signal.

| Property | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Total energy $E$ | Finite ($0 < E < \infty$) | Infinite |
| Average power $P$ | Zero ($P = 0$) | Finite ($0 < P < \infty$) |
| Examples | $e^{-t} u(t)$, $e^{-t^2}$ | $\sin(t)$, $\cos(t)$, $u(t)$ |
| Nature | Transient (decays to zero) | Persistent (does not decay) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Even/Odd decomposition** is the foundation of **Fourier series analysis** — engineers exploit it to convert complex periodic signals into sine and cosine components.
- **Energy/Power classification** is critical in **communication systems** — average power limits transmission, while total energy matters in pulse design (radar, UWB).
- **Causality** is the bedrock of **real-time DSP systems** — any real, physical system must be causal.
- **Periodic signals** are the cornerstone of **AC circuit analysis**, **signal generators**, and **carrier waves** in modulation.
- **Deterministic vs Random** determines whether you use **deterministic signal processing** (LTI theory) or **stochastic processes** (autocorrelation, PSD).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🟥 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Claim:** Every signal $x(t)$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even part $x_e(t)$ and an odd part $x_o(t)$:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

### Proof:

**Step 1:** Start with the defining equations of even and odd parts:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Add them together:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} + \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Combine the numerators (common denominator = 2):

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t) + x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 4:** The $x(-t)$ terms cancel out:

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = \frac{2 \cdot x(t)}{2} = x(t)$$

**Step 5:** Verify $x_e(t)$ is even by replacing $t$ with $-t$:

$$x_e(-t) = \frac{x(-t) + x(-(-t))}{2} = \frac{x(-t) + x(t)}{2} = x_e(t) \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Verify $x_o(t)$ is odd:

$$x_o(-t) = \frac{x(-t) - x(-(-t))}{2} = \frac{x(-t) - x(t)}{2} = -\left(\frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}\right) = -x_o(t) \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Every signal $x(t)$ can be **uniquely** decomposed into even and odd parts using the formulas given. This result is fundamental to Fourier analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Period of $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$

We want to find the smallest $T > 0$ such that $x(t+T) = x(t)$.

**Step 1:** Apply the periodicity condition:

$$\sin(\omega_0 (t + T)) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $\sin(\theta + 2\pi k) = \sin(\theta)$ for integer $k$:

$$\sin(\omega_0 t + \omega_0 T) = \sin(\omega_0 t) \implies \omega_0 T = 2\pi k$$

**Step 3:** For the **smallest** positive $T$, choose $k = 1$:

$$\omega_0 T = 2\pi \implies T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$$

> [!TIP]
> The frequency $f_0$ (in Hz) and angular frequency $\omega_0$ (in rad/s) are related by $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$, and the period is $T = \dfrac{1}{f_0}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Period of $x[n] = \sin(\omega_0 n)$

**Step 1:** Apply discrete periodicity:

$$\sin(\omega_0 (n + N)) = \sin(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 2:** This requires:

$$\omega_0 N = 2\pi k \implies N = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega_0}$$

**Step 3:** For $N$ to be an **integer**, $\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ must be rational. If $\omega_0 = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{p}{q}$ (with $p, q$ coprime integers), then:

$$N = \frac{2\pi k}{2\pi \cdot p/q} = \frac{kq}{p}$$

The smallest positive integer $N$ is obtained by choosing $k = p$:

$$N = q$$

**Example:** For $x[n] = \sin(0.5\pi n)$, we have $\omega_0 = 0.5\pi = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{1}{4}$, so $p = 1$, $q = 4$, hence the fundamental period is $N = 4$. ✓

**Counter-example:** $x[n] = \sin(0.5 n)$ has $\omega_0 = 0.5$, so $\omega_0/(2\pi) = 1/(4\pi)$, which is **irrational** → the signal is **aperiodic**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy of a Rectangular Pulse

Consider $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_0$ and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1:** Write the energy integral:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the pulse:

$$E = \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$E = A^2 \cdot [t]_{0}^{T_0} = A^2 \cdot T_0$$

**Step 4:** Since $0 < A^2 T_0 < \infty$ and power is zero (signal vanishes as $T \to \infty$):

> The rectangular pulse is an **energy signal** with energy $E = A^2 T_0$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Power of a Sinusoid

Consider $x(t) = A \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$.

**Step 1:** Power definition:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Use $\sin^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 3:** Split the integral:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt - \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 4:** The first integral gives $2T$. The second integral of a cosine over a symmetric interval equals $\dfrac{\sin(2\omega_0 T + 2\phi) - \sin(-2\omega_0 T + 2\phi)}{\omega_0}$, which is bounded. As $T \to \infty$, dividing by $2T$ kills it.

**Step 5:** Hence:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot 2T = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A sinusoid of amplitude $A$ has average power $\dfrac{A^2}{2}$, independent of frequency or phase. The energy is infinite, and the power is finite → it is a **power signal**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Even-Odd Decomposition of $x(t) = e^{at} u(t)$

Given: $x(t) = e^{at} u(t) = \begin{cases} e^{at}, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$

**Step 1:** Find $x(-t)$:

$$x(-t) = e^{-at} u(-t) = \begin{cases} 0, & -t < 0 \Rightarrow t > 0 \\ e^{-at}, & -t \geq 0 \Rightarrow t \leq 0 \end{cases}$$

So $x(-t) = e^{-at} u(-t)$.

**Step 2:** Compute even part:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{at} u(t) + e^{-at} u(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute odd part:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{e^{at} u(t) - e^{-at} u(-t)}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Verify: $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = e^{at} u(t) = x(t)$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟩 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Signals

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete hierarchical classification of signals covered in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Signals] --> B[Continuous-Time x of t]
    A --> C[Discrete-Time x of n]
    A --> D[Based on Symmetry]
    A --> E[Based on Periodicity]
    A --> F[Based on Determinism]
    A --> G[Based on Energy Power]
    A --> H[Based on Causality]

    B --> B1[Analog Signal]
    B --> B2[Sampled Signal]

    C --> C1[Digital Signal]
    C --> C2[Quantized Signal]

    D --> D1[Even Signal]
    D --> D2[Odd Signal]
    D --> D3[Neither Even nor Odd]

    E --> E1[Periodic Signal]
    E --> E2[Aperiodic Signal]

    F --> F1[Deterministic Signal]
    F --> F2[Random Signal]

    G --> G1[Energy Signal]
    G --> G2[Power Signal]

    H --> H1[Causal Signal]
    H --> H2[Anti-causal Signal]
    H --> H3[Non-causal Signal]
```

## 4.2 Even vs Odd Signal — Symmetry Visual

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Even["EVEN SIGNAL x at t equals x at minus t"]
        E1[cosine t symmetric about y axis]
        E2[value at t equals value at minus t]
    end

    subgraph Odd["ODD SIGNAL x at minus t equals minus x at t"]
        O1[sine t antisymmetric about origin]
        O2[180 degree rotation about origin]
    end

    Decomp[Every signal x of t] --> EPart[Even Part x of e of t]
    Decomp --> OPart[Odd Part x of o of t]
    EPart --> Sum[Sum gives x of t]
    OPart --> Sum
```

## 4.3 Energy vs Power Signal — Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Given Signal x of t] --> Q1{Is total energy E finite and non zero?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is average power P zero?}
    Q1 -->|No, E is infinite| Q3{Is average power P finite and non zero?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| EnergySig[ENERGY SIGNAL]
    Q2 -->|No| Neither[NEITHER]

    Q3 -->|Yes| PowerSig[POWER SIGNAL]
    Q3 -->|No| Neither2[NEITHER]

    EnergySig --> Note[A signal CANNOT be both]
    PowerSig --> Note
```

## 4.4 Periodicity Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Is x of t equals x of t plus T for all t] --> Q1{Does there exist T greater than 0?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Periodic[PERIODIC with fundamental period T]
    Q1 -->|No| Aperiodic[APERIODIC non periodic]

    Periodic --> P1[Continuous T real valued]
    Periodic --> P2[Discrete N integer valued]

    P2 --> Q2{Is omega sub 0 divided by 2 pi rational?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| PeriodicN[PERIODIC with N equal to q]
    Q2 -->|No| AperiodicN[APERIODIC]
```

## 4.5 Causality Timeline Visual

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Past["PAST t less than 0"]
        P1[Signal may be non zero for non causal]
    end

    subgraph Present["PRESENT t equals 0"]
        PR[Boundary value]
    end

    subgraph Future["FUTURE t greater than 0"]
        F1[Signal may be non zero for non causal]
    end

    Causal[CAUSAL signal is zero in PAST only] --> Past
    Anticausal[ANTI CAUSAL signal is zero in FUTURE only] --> Future
    Noncausal[NON CAUSAL signal is non zero on BOTH sides] --> Past
    Noncausal --> Future
```

## 4.6 Block Architecture — Signal Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input[Input Signal x of t or x of n] --> Step1[Step 1 Identify Independent Variable Type]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2 Check Symmetry Even or Odd]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Test Periodicity]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Check Causality]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Compute Energy and Power]
    Step5 --> Output[Output Complete Classification Report]
```

> [!TIP]
> **This pipeline mirrors how a KTU examiner expects you to "classify" a signal in 14-mark questions.** Work through these steps in order in your answer script for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟪 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define an even signal and an odd signal. Check whether $x(t) = e^{jt}$ is even, odd, or neither.**

**Model Answer:**

A signal $x(t)$ is:
- **Even** if $x(-t) = x(t)\, \forall\, t$
- **Odd** if $x(-t) = -x(t)\, \forall\, t$

For $x(t) = e^{jt} = \cos(t) + j\sin(t)$:

$$x(-t) = e^{-jt} = \cos(t) - j\sin(t)$$

Clearly $x(-t) \neq x(t)$ and $x(-t) \neq -x(t)$ in general. **Hence, $x(t) = e^{jt}$ is neither even nor odd.**

However, its **real part** $\cos(t)$ is even, and its **imaginary part** $\sin(t)$ is odd. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the fundamental period of $x(t) = 4\cos(50\pi t) - 3\sin(20\pi t)$?**

**Model Answer:**

For $x_1(t) = 4\cos(50\pi t)$: $\omega_1 = 50\pi \implies T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{50\pi} = \dfrac{1}{25}$ s

For $x_2(t) = -3\sin(20\pi t)$: $\omega_2 = 20\pi \implies T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{20\pi} = \dfrac{1}{10}$ s

Ratio: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1/25}{1/10} = \dfrac{10}{25} = \dfrac{2}{5}$ → **rational** ✓

Fundamental period $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2)$:
$T_0 = 5 \cdot T_1 = 5 \cdot \dfrac{1}{25} = \dfrac{1}{5}$ s  
Check: $2 \cdot T_2 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{10} = \dfrac{1}{5}$ s ✓

**Therefore, the fundamental period is $T_0 = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2$ s. (3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU ESE 2024 regulations, Module 1 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. The two alternatives below cover the same topic from different angles.**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a)** Determine whether the following signals are energy signals, power signals, or neither. Compute the energy/power value where applicable. **(7 marks)**
- (i) $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$
- (ii) $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$

**b)** Find the even and odd components of the signal $x(t) = (1 + t + t^2)\cos(t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**(i) $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$**

Energy:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4}$$

Since $0 < E = \dfrac{1}{4} < \infty$, it is an **energy signal** with $E = 0.25$ J.

Power:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = 0$$

(since the signal decays to zero). ✓ Consistent with energy-signal definition.

**(ii) $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$**

Energy:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} 25\cos^2(100\pi t) \, dt \to \infty$$

Power:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} 25\cos^2(100\pi t) \, dt = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ W}$$

(since the average of $\cos^2$ over a long interval is $\dfrac{1}{2}$). Hence it is a **power signal** with $P = 12.5$ W.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

Given $x(t) = (1 + t + t^2)\cos(t)$.

**Step 1:** Compute $x(-t)$:

$$x(-t) = (1 - t + t^2)\cos(t)$$

**Step 2:** Even part:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} = \frac{(1 + t + t^2)\cos(t) + (1 - t + t^2)\cos(t)}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Combine terms inside:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{(2 + 2t^2)\cos(t)}{2} = (1 + t^2)\cos(t)$$

**Step 4:** Odd part:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = \frac{(1 + t + t^2)\cos(t) - (1 - t + t^2)\cos(t)}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{(2t)\cos(t)}{2} = t\cos(t)$$

**Step 6:** Verify: $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = (1 + t^2)\cos(t) + t\cos(t) = (1 + t + t^2)\cos(t) = x(t)$ ✓

**Final Answer:**

- **Even part:** $x_e(t) = (1 + t^2)\cos(t)$
- **Odd part:** $x_o(t) = t\cos(t)$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a)** Test the periodicity of the following signals and find the fundamental period where applicable: **(7 marks)**
- (i) $x(t) = \sin(10t) + \cos(20t)$
- (ii) $x[n] = \sin(0.3\pi n)$

**b)** Check whether the signal $x(t) = e^{-3t}\sin(5t) u(t)$ is causal, energy, or power type. Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**(i) $x(t) = \sin(10t) + \cos(20t)$**

For $\sin(10t)$: $\omega_1 = 10$ rad/s $\implies T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{10} = \dfrac{\pi}{5}$ s

For $\cos(20t)$: $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s $\implies T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{20} = \dfrac{\pi}{10}$ s

Ratio: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = \dfrac{\pi/5}{\pi/10} = 2$ → **rational**

$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = 1 \cdot T_1 = 2 \cdot T_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{5}$ s

**Periodic with fundamental period $T_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{5} \approx 0.628$ s.**

**(ii) $x[n] = \sin(0.3\pi n)$**

We need $0.3\pi N = 2\pi k \implies N = \dfrac{2k}{0.3} = \dfrac{20k}{3}$.

For $N$ to be integer, choose $k = 3$: $N = 20$.

**Periodic with fundamental period $N = 20$.**

(Alternatively, $\omega_0 = 0.3\pi = 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{3}{20}$, so $p = 3$, $q = 20$, fundamental period is $N = 20$. ✓)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

Given $x(t) = e^{-3t}\sin(5t) u(t)$.

**Causality check:**

For $t < 0$: $u(t) = 0 \implies x(t) = 0$. ✓
For $t \geq 0$: $u(t) = 1 \implies x(t) = e^{-3t}\sin(5t)$, which is non-zero in general.

**The signal is CAUSAL** (it is zero for all $t < 0$).

**Energy/Power check:**

**Step 1:** Energy:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \sin^2(5t) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Use $\sin^2(5t) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(10t)}{2}$:

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(10t)}{2} \, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \, dt - \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t}\cos(10t) \, dt$$

**Step 3:** First integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \, dt = \frac{1}{6}$$

**Step 4:** Second integral (standard result):

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at}\cos(bt) \, dt = \frac{a}{a^2 + b^2}$$

With $a = 6$, $b = 10$:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t}\cos(10t) \, dt = \frac{6}{36 + 100} = \frac{6}{136} = \frac{3}{68}$$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{6} - \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{68} = \frac{1}{12} - \frac{3}{136} = \frac{68 - 27}{12 \cdot 68} = \frac{41}{816} \approx 0.0502 \text{ J}$$

**Step 6:** Power:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = 0$$

(since energy is finite, average power is zero for a decaying signal).

**Conclusion:** $x(t) = e^{-3t}\sin(5t)u(t)$ is a **Causal Energy Signal** with $E = \dfrac{41}{816}$ J.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
>
> 1. **Periodicity of Discrete-Time Sinusoids:** Students often claim $x[n] = \sin(0.5n)$ is periodic. **IT IS NOT**, because $\omega_0/2\pi = 1/(4\pi)$ is irrational. *Always check* $\omega_0/(2\pi) \in \mathbb{Q}$.
>
> 2. **Even/Odd Confusion with Shifted Signals:** $\sin(t - \pi/2) = -\cos(t)$ — many students wrongly classify it as odd. *Check by computing* $x(-t)$ first.
>
> 3. **Energy vs Power Trap:** A decaying exponential $e^{-t}u(t)$ has **infinite duration** but **finite energy** because the amplitude decays faster than $1/\sqrt{t}$. Don't confuse duration with energy.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Unit Step in Causal Signals:** A causal signal that is non-zero for $t \geq 0$ must include $u(t)$. If you write $x(t) = e^{-t}$ (without $u(t)$), you imply it's defined for all $t$, which is non-causal!
>
> 5. **Sum of Periodic Signals — Wrong LCM:** If $T_1 = \sqrt{2}$ and $T_2 = 3$, the sum is **NOT periodic** because $T_1/T_2 = \sqrt{2}/3$ is irrational. KTU examiners love testing this!
>
> 6. **Sign of Power for Power Signals:** Power must be **strictly positive** ($P > 0$). A constant zero signal has $P = 0$ and $E = 0$ — it's **neither** energy nor power.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024**

- ✅ A signal is a function carrying information; classified along **7 dimensions** (CT/DT, even/odd, periodic/aperiodic, deterministic/random, energy/power, causal/anti-causal/non-causal).
- ✅ **Even:** $x(-t) = x(t)$. **Odd:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$.
- ✅ **Every signal** = Even part + Odd part, where $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2}$ and $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2}$.
- ✅ **Periodic CT:** $x(t+T) = x(t)$; **Periodic DT:** $x[n+N] = x[n]$ with $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$.
- ✅ Discrete sinusoid $x[n] = \sin(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **iff** $\dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$ is rational.
- ✅ Sum of periodic signals is periodic **iff** ratio of periods is rational.
- ✅ **Energy:** $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt < \infty$.
- ✅ **Power:** $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt > 0$.
- ✅ A signal **cannot** be both energy and power simultaneously.
- ✅ **Causal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. **Anti-causal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t > 0$. **Non-causal:** non-zero on both sides.
- ✅ **Sinusoid power** is always $\dfrac{A^2}{2}$ (independent of frequency and phase).
- ✅ **Energy of rectangular pulse** (amplitude $A$, duration $T_0$) is $A^2 T_0$.
- ✅ In KTU answers, always show **discrete steps** with equations — partial work gets partial marks.
- ✅ Master the unit step $u(t)$ and signum $\text{sgn}(t)$ functions — they appear in nearly every Part B question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Signal Operations. Continuous Time and Discrete Time Systems - Representation and Classification (Memory, Causal, Stable, Linear, Time-Invariant, Invertible)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Signal Operations. Continuous Time and Discrete Time Systems - Representation and Classification (Memory, Causal, Stable, Linear, Time-Invariant, Invertible)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme context, a **signal** is a mathematical description of a physical phenomenon (such as voltage, current, sound, image, temperature) expressed as a function of one or more independent variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A signal is a function $x(t)$ (continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (discrete-time) that carries information about the behavior or nature of some phenomenon. Variables: $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (continuous), $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (discrete).

| Property | Continuous-Time (CT) Signal | Discrete-Time (DT) Signal |
|----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Independent Variable | $t$ (real, continuous) | $n$ (integer, sampled) |
| Notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ |
| Real-world Example | Speech, ECG, Analog voltage | Digital audio, Stock price, Sampled data |

## 1.2 What is a System?

A **system** is a mathematical operator or transformation $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input signal $x(t)$ to an output signal $y(t)$.

$$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{or} \quad y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View):** Think of a signal as **water flowing through a pipe** and the system as the **pipe network itself** (with valves, filters, pumps). The water entering is the input $x(t)$, the pipe transforms it, and the water leaving is the output $y(t)$. Different pipe designs represent different systems.

## 1.3 Basic Signal Operations (KTU High-Priority Topic)

### 1.3.1 Time Shifting (Translation)
The signal $x(t)$ is shifted along the time axis by an amount $t_0$:

$$x(t) \longrightarrow x(t - t_0)$$

- If $t_0 > 0$: **Right shift** (delay) — signal appears later
- If $t_0 < 0$: **Left shift** (advance) — signal appears earlier

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Recording a cricket match with a 2-hour delay. The live signal $x(t)$ is replaced by $x(t-2)$ — the same match, just shifted to start later.

### 1.3.2 Time Reversal (Reflection)
The signal is flipped about the vertical (amplitude) axis:

$$x(t) \longrightarrow x(-t)$$

### 1.3.3 Time Scaling (Compression / Expansion)
$$x(t) \longrightarrow x(at)$$

- $\vert a \vert > 1$: **Compression** (signal plays faster)
- $0 < \vert a \vert < 1$: **Expansion** (signal plays slower)
- $a < 0$: Combined scaling + reversal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Discrete-Time):** For $x[an]$ with $a > 1$, the operation is **not invertible** — multiple input samples map to one output. Example: $x[2n]$ of $x[0]=1, x[1]=2, x[2]=3$ produces $y[0]=1, y[1]=3$ — the value 2 is **lost**.

### 1.3.4 Amplitude Scaling
$$x(t) \longrightarrow A \cdot x(t)$$

If $A > 1$: amplification; $0 < A < 1$: attenuation; $A < 0$: inversion with scaling.

### 1.3.5 Signal Addition
$$y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$$

> **Intuition:** Mixing two music tracks played simultaneously at a mixer console.

### 1.3.6 Signal Multiplication
$$y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$$

> **Intuition:** Amplitude Modulation (AM) — multiplying a low-frequency audio signal with a high-frequency carrier to enable long-distance radio transmission.

### 1.3.7 Differentiation (CT) and First Difference (DT)
- **Continuous-Time Differentiation:**
$$y(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt}$$
- **Discrete-Time First Difference:**
$$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$$

### 1.3.8 Integration (CT) and Running Sum (DT)
- **Continuous-Time Integration:**
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$$
- **Discrete-Time Running Sum (Accumulator):**
$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$$

## 1.4 Six Fundamental Properties of CT/DT Systems (KTU Module 1 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** These 6 properties (Memory, Causality, Stability, Linearity, Time-Invariance, Invertibility) carry **highest weightage** in KTU Board Exams — expect direct 7-mark sub-questions in Part B.

### A. Memory (Memoryless vs Dynamic)
- **Memoryless System:** Output at time $t$ (or $n$) depends **only** on input at the **same** instant.
$$y(t) = k \cdot x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad y[n] = k \cdot x[n]$$
- **Dynamic (with memory) System:** Output depends on past/future values.
$$y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1] \quad \text{(needs } x[n-1]\text{)}$$

### B. Causality
- **Causal System:** Output at present depends only on present and past inputs (never on future).
$$y(t) = T\{x(\tau) : \tau \le t\}$$
- **Non-Causal:** Depends on future inputs. Example: $y(t) = x(t+1)$.

### C. Stability (BIBO)
- **BIBO Stable:** Every bounded input produces a bounded output.
$$\exists\, M \text{ s.t. } \vert x(t) \vert < M \;\;\forall\, t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \exists\, N \text{ s.t. } \vert y(t) \vert < N \;\;\forall\, t$$

### D. Linearity
A system is linear iff it satisfies **Superposition**:
- **Additivity:** $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$
- **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a \cdot x\} = a \cdot T\{x\}$

> **Combined (Superposition):** $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 T\{x_1\} + a_2 T\{x_2\}$

### E. Time-Invariance
A system is Time-Invariant (TI) if a time shift in the input produces an identical time shift in the output.

If $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$, then for TI: $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$.

### F. Invertibility
A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs. Equivalently, an **inverse system** $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ exists such that:

$$T^{-1}\{T\{x(t)\}\} = x(t)$$

> **Example:** $y(t) = 2x(t)$ is invertible with inverse $z(t) = \frac{1}{2}y(t)$.
> **Counter-example:** $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is **not invertible** since $x(t)$ and $-x(t)$ give the same $y(t)$.

---

> **Section 1 Summary:** We defined signals and systems formally, listed the 8 basic signal operations, and introduced the 6 system classification properties. Section 2 builds the theoretical machinery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time Shifting — Detailed Behavior

For a discrete signal $x[n]$ shifted by $n_0$ samples:

$$y[n] = x[n - n_0]$$

- $n_0 > 0$: sample that was at index 0 is now at index $n_0$ → **right shift (delay)**
- $n_0 < 0$: sample that was at index 0 is now at index $-n_0$ on the left → **left shift (advance)**

> **Energy Invariance:** The total energy of a signal is **unchanged** by time shifting:
$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t-t_0) \vert^2 dt$$

## 2.2 Time Scaling — Mathematical Derivation

Consider $y(t) = x(at)$ for $a > 0$:

- If $a = 2$: signal compressed by factor 2 (plays twice as fast)
- If $a = 0.5$: signal expanded by factor 2 (plays half as fast)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy in scaled signal:**
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(at) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 d\tau = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$$

For $a = 2$, $E_y = E_x/2$ — compression reduces energy (the signal "spends less time" at any given amplitude).

## 2.3 Why Differentiation & Integration Form a "Dual Pair"

If $x(t) \longrightarrow y(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt}$, then integrating $y$ gives back $x$ (plus constant). The **accumulator** is the discrete analog of the **integrator**.

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \frac{dx(\tau)}{d\tau}\,d\tau + x(-\infty)$$

## 2.4 BIBO Stability Test (LTI Systems)

For a CT LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$:

$$\text{System is BIBO stable} \iff \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

For a DT LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$:

$$\text{System is BIBO stable} \iff \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

> **Why?** Output $y(t) = (x * h)(t) = \int h(\tau) x(t-\tau) d\tau$. By triangle inequality, $\vert y(t) \vert \le \max_\tau \vert x(\tau) \vert \cdot \int \vert h(\tau) \vert d\tau$. Bounded $h$ integral → bounded $y$.

## 2.5 Linearity — The "Zero-In-Zero-Out" Test

> [!NOTE]
> **Necessary condition for linearity:** $T\{0\} = 0$. If a system gives a non-zero output for zero input, it is **definitely non-linear**.

**Proof sketch:** Set $a = 0$ in $T\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x(t)\}$ → $T\{0\} = 0$.

## 2.6 Time-Invariance — The Shift-Commutativity Test

$$\boxed{\;T\{x(t - t_0)\} \stackrel{?}{=} y(t - t_0)\;}$$

- LHS: shift input first, then pass through system
- RHS: pass input through system, then shift output
- If equal for **all** $t_0$ and all $x(t)$ → **Time-Invariant**

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | CT Condition | DT Condition |
|---|----------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Time Shift | $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ |
| 2 | Time Scale | $y(t) = x(at)$ | $y[n] = x[kn]$ (lossy for $k>1$) |
| 3 | Reversal | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | $y[n] = x[-n]$ |
| 4 | Amplitude Scale | $y(t) = A\,x(t)$ | $y[n] = A\,x[n]$ |
| 5 | Differentiation | $y(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ |
| 6 | Integration | $y(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | $y[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ |
| 7 | Memoryless | $y(t) = f(x(t))$ | $y[n] = f(x[n])$ |
| 8 | Causal | Depends on $x(\tau),\, \tau \le t$ only | Depends on $x[k],\, k \le n$ only |
| 9 | BIBO Stable (LTI) | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ |
| 10 | Linear | $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 T\{x_1\} + a_2 T\{x_2\}$ | Same (with $n$ in place of $t$) |
| 11 | Time-Invariant | $T\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ | $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$ |
| 12 | Invertible | $\exists\, T^{-1} : T^{-1}\{T\{x\}\} = x$ | Same |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System Property | Real-World Use |
|----------------|---------------|
| **Linearity** | Foundation of Fourier/Laplace/Transform analysis; only linear systems allow frequency-domain decomposition |
| **Time-Invariance** | Enables design of fixed-parameter filters that don't need re-tuning over time |
| **Causality** | **Mandatory** for real-time systems (e.g., heart pacemaker, autopilot) — no future data available |
| **Stability** | Critical in control systems — unstable aircraft autopilot = crash |
| **Invertibility** | Underlies channel equalizers in communication, data compression (lossless) |
| **Memoryless** | Used in simple sensors and clipper/clamper circuits |

---
> **Section 2 Takeaway:** Every classification has a precise mathematical test. Memorize the table — it is the "Bible" for KTU Module 1 Part B problems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Scaling Affects Signal Energy

**Statement:** If $y(t) = x(at)$ with $a > 0$, prove $E_y = \dfrac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$.

**Step 1 — Write the energy of $y(t)$:**
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(at) \vert^2 dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** $\tau = at \;\Rightarrow\; d\tau = a\, dt \;\Rightarrow\; dt = \dfrac{d\tau}{a}$:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \cdot \frac{1}{a}\, d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the integral as $E_x$:**
$$E_y = \frac{1}{a} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 d\tau = \frac{E_x}{a} = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$$

**Conclusion:** For $a = 2$ (compression), the energy halves. For $a = 0.5$ (expansion), energy doubles. Energy is inversely proportional to the scaling factor. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BIBO Stability of LTI Systems via Impulse Response

**Statement:** A CT LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ is BIBO stable **if and only if** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$.

### Part 1: Necessity ($\Rightarrow$)
Assume the system is BIBO stable. Take bounded input $x(t) = \text{sgn}(h(-t))$, so $\vert x(t) \vert \le 1$. Output:
$$y(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau) x(-\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,\text{sgn}(h(\tau))\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau$$

BIBO stability demands $y(0)$ bounded, hence $\int \vert h \vert < \infty$. ✓

### Part 2: Sufficiency ($\Leftarrow$)
Assume $\int \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau = K < \infty$. Let $\vert x(t) \vert \le M$. By convolution:
$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau) x(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$

Take absolute value and apply the **triangle inequality**:
$$\vert y(t) \vert \le \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \cdot \vert x(t - \tau) \vert\, d\tau$$

Substitute the bound $\vert x(t-\tau) \vert \le M$:
$$\vert y(t) \vert \le M \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert\, d\tau = MK < \infty$$

So $y(t)$ is bounded for every bounded $x(t)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Classify $y(t) = 2x(t) + 3\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$

**Step 1 — Memory:** Output at time $t$ depends on $x(t)$ and $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$. Since the derivative requires knowledge of behavior in a neighborhood of $t$, the system is **dynamic (has memory)**.

**Step 2 — Causality:** Differentiator uses values infinitesimally close to $t$ (both past and present — no future). Hence **causal**.

**Step 3 — BIBO Stability:** Apply formula. The transfer function $H(j\omega) = 2 + 3j\omega$, with $\vert H \vert$ unbounded as $\omega \to \infty$. Use direct test: let $x(t) = u(t)$ (unit step, bounded), then $y(t) = 2u(t) + 3\delta(t)$. The impulse $\delta(t)$ is **unbounded** → system is **NOT BIBO stable**.

**Step 4 — Linearity:** System is a linear combination of identity and derivative — both linear operators. Hence **linear**.

**Step 5 — Time-Invariance:** Let $x_1(t) = x(t - t_0)$. Then:
$$T\{x_1(t)\} = 2x(t-t_0) + 3\frac{d}{dt}x(t-t_0) = 2x(t-t_0) + 3\frac{d}{dt}x(t-t_0) = y(t-t_0)$$

Hence **time-invariant**.

**Step 6 — Invertibility:** Solve for $x(t)$ from $y(t) = 2x + 3x'$: this is a first-order ODE with unique solution given $x(-\infty)$. So **invertible** (in principle), inverse involves an integrating factor $e^{2t/3}$.

> **Summary of Result Table:**

| Property | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| Memory | Dynamic (with memory) |
| Causal | Yes ✓ |
| BIBO Stable | No ✗ |
| Linear | Yes ✓ |
| Time-Invariant | Yes ✓ |
| Invertible | Yes ✓ |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Classify $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$

**Step 1 — Memoryless:** Output at index $n$ depends only on $x[n]$ (same index). **Memoryless** ✓.

**Step 2 — Causal:** Depends only on present input. **Causal** ✓.

**Step 3 — BIBO Stable:** Let $x[n] = 1$ for all $n$ (bounded, $M=1$). Then $y[n] = n$, which is **unbounded**. **NOT stable** ✗.

**Step 4 — Linearity:** Test superposition: $T\{a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]\} = n(a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]) = a_1 n x_1[n] + a_2 n x_2[n] = a_1 T\{x_1\} + a_2 T\{x_2\}$. **Linear** ✓.

**Step 5 — Time-Invariant:** $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n - n_0]$. But $y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) x[n - n_0]$. These are **not equal** (extra $-n_0 x[n-n_0]$ term). **Time-Variant** ✗.

**Step 6 — Invertible:** Yes — inverse is $x[n] = y[n]/n$ for $n \ne 0$, $x[0]$ arbitrary.

| Property | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| Memoryless | Yes ✓ |
| Causal | Yes ✓ |
| BIBO Stable | No ✗ |
| Linear | Yes ✓ |
| Time-Invariant | No ✗ |
| Invertible | Yes ✓ |

---

## 3.5 Detailed Linearity Proof for System $y(t) = \int_0^t x(\tau) d\tau$

We need to show: $T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 T\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 T\{x_2(t)\}$.

**LHS:**
$$T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = \int_0^t [a_1 x_1(\tau) + a_2 x_2(\tau)]\, d\tau$$

**Apply linearity of integration (sum rule):**
$$= a_1 \int_0^t x_1(\tau)\, d\tau + a_2 \int_0^t x_2(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Recognize as RHS:**
$$= a_1 \, T\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 \, T\{x_2(t)\} = \text{RHS} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

> **Section 3 Takeaway:** Practice classifying 5–6 different systems. KTU expects: (i) verdict on each of 6 properties, (ii) explicit test for linearity (superposition) and TI (shift commutativity).
