### Step 2 — Introduce a substitution to eliminate the time shift in the integrand.

Let $\tau = t - t_0$, which means $t = \tau + t_0$ and $dt = d\tau$. The integration limits remain $-\infty$ to $\infty$.

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega(\tau + t_0)}\,d\tau$$

### Step 3 — Split the exponential using exponent laws.

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = e^{-j\Omega t_0} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,d\tau$$

### Step 4 — Identify the integral as the original Fourier Transform $X(j\Omega)$.

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,d\tau = X(j\Omega)$$

### Step 5 — Combine the result.

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = e^{-j\Omega t_0}\,X(j\Omega)$$

> **Conclusion:** A time shift of $t_0$ seconds multiplies the spectrum by $e^{-j\Omega t_0}$, a complex exponential that contributes a **linear phase** of $-\Omega t_0$ but **does not change the magnitude** $\vert X(j\Omega) \vert$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Convolution Property (Time Domain)

**Statement:** $x_1(t) * x_2(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X_1(j\Omega) \cdot X_2(j\Omega)$.

### Step 1 — Write the convolution integral explicitly.

$$y(t) = x_1(t) * x_2(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau)\,x_2(t - \tau)\,d\tau$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Fourier Transform to $y(t)$.

$$Y(j\Omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau)\,x_2(t - \tau)\,d\tau \right] e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt$$

### Step 3 — Interchange the order of integration (Fubini's theorem, valid under absolute integrability).

$$Y(j\Omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_2(t - \tau)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt \right] d\tau$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Time Shifting Property (derived above) to the inner integral.

For fixed $\tau$, the inner integral is the Fourier Transform of $x_2(t - \tau)$:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_2(t - \tau)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt = e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,X_2(j\Omega)$$

### Step 5 — Substitute back and factor out the constant $X_2(j\Omega)$.

$$Y(j\Omega) = X_2(j\Omega) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,d\tau$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the integral as $X_1(j\Omega)$.

$$Y(j\Omega) = X_1(j\Omega) \cdot X_2(j\Omega)$$

> **Conclusion:** Convolution in time corresponds to **multiplication** in frequency. This is the **central theorem** behind LTI system analysis: the output spectrum is the product of the input spectrum and the system's frequency response.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Duality Property

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\Omega)$, then $X(jt) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\,x(-\Omega)$.

### Step 1 — Start from the Inverse Fourier Transform equation.

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\Omega)\,e^{j\Omega t}\,d\Omega$$

### Step 2 — Swap the variable name $t$ with $\tau$ for clarity.

$$x(\tau) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\Omega)\,e^{j\Omega \tau}\,d\Omega$$

### Step 3 — Interchange the roles of time and frequency: replace $\tau$ with $-\Omega$ and $\Omega$ with $t$.

$$x(-\Omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j t)\,e^{j t (-\Omega)}\,dt$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange the integral to match the form of the Forward Fourier Transform.

$$x(-\Omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j t)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt$$

### Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $2\pi$.

$$2\pi\,x(-\Omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j t)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt$$

### Step 6 — Recognize the right-hand side as the Fourier Transform of $X(jt)$ with respect to the variable $t$, evaluated at $\Omega$.

$$\mathcal{F}\{X(jt)\} = 2\pi\,x(-\Omega)$$

> **Conclusion:** The duality property is the formal justification for transform pairs such as **rectangular pulse $\leftrightarrow$ sinc function** in either direction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

**Statement:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2 d\Omega$.

### Step 1 — Express the energy in terms of the inverse transform.

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,x^*(t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \left[ \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\Omega)\,e^{j\Omega t}\,d\Omega \right]^* dt$$

### Step 2 — Apply the complex conjugate to the bracketed expression.

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\Omega)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,d\Omega\,dt$$

### Step 3 — Swap the order of integration.

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\Omega) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt \right] d\Omega$$

### Step 4 — Identify the inner integral as $X(j\Omega)$.

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\Omega) \cdot X(j\Omega)\,d\Omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2\,d\Omega$$

> **Conclusion:** Total signal energy computed in time equals total energy computed in frequency (scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$). This is the foundation of **spectral energy density** analysis.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Property Selection Guide for KTU Problems

| Given Operation on $x(t)$ | Property to Apply | Resulting Operation on $X(j\Omega)$ |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Signal is delayed by $t_0$ | Time Shifting | Multiply by $e^{-j\Omega t_0}$ |
| Signal is multiplied by $e^{j\Omega_0 t}$ | Frequency Shifting | Shift spectrum to $\Omega - \Omega_0$ |
| Two signals are convolved | Convolution Theorem | Multiply spectra |
| Signal is differentiated | Differentiation | Multiply by $j\Omega$ |
| Signal is time-reversed | Time Reversal | Replace $\Omega$ with $-\Omega$ |
| Energy must be computed | Parseval's Theorem | Use $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2 d\Omega$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The diagrams below visualize the **logical flow** of the CTFT property framework. They are designed to show how a time-domain operation is mapped to a frequency-domain result and how properties interact in LTI system analysis.

---

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — CTFT Property Mapping Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input Time Domain Signal x of t] --> nodeB[Identify Operation on x of t]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Type of Operation}
    nodeC -- Shift in t --> nodeD[Time Shifting Property]
    nodeC -- Multiply by Complex Exp --> nodeE[Frequency Shifting Property]
    nodeC -- Scale t by constant a --> nodeF[Time Scaling Property]
    nodeC -- Differentiate --> nodeG[Differentiation Property]
    nodeC -- Convolve Two Signals --> nodeH[Convolution in Time Property]
    nodeC -- Multiply Two Signals --> nodeI[Multiplication in Time Property]
    nodeC -- Reverse t --> nodeJ[Time Reversal Property]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Output X of jOmega Phase Multiplied]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Output X of jOmega minus jOmega0]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Output Scaled and Compressed Spectrum]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Output jOmega Times X of jOmega]
    nodeH --> nodeO[Output Product of Spectra]
    nodeI --> nodeP[Output Scaled Convolution of Spectra]
    nodeJ --> nodeQ[Output X of minus jOmega]
    nodeK --> nodeR[LTI System Analysis or Filter Design]
    nodeL --> nodeR
    nodeM --> nodeR
    nodeN --> nodeR
    nodeO --> nodeR
    nodeP --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeR
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Convolution–Multiplication Duality Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    sigA[x1 of t] --> convNode[Convolution Operator in Time Domain]
    sigB[x2 of t] --> convNode
    convNode --> sigY[y of t Output Signal]
    sigA --> fwdA[Fourier Transform]
    sigB --> fwdB[Fourier Transform]
    fwdA --> mulNode[Multiplication in Frequency Domain]
    fwdB --> mulNode
    mulNode --> fwdY[Fourier Transform of Output]
    fwdY -.equivalent.- sigY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two paths from $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ — one through the convolution operator and the other through the multiplication of their Fourier transforms — lead to **equivalent outputs**. This is the **duality of convolution and multiplication** under the CTFT.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Conservation (Parseval's Theorem)

```mermaid
graph TD
    timeDomain[Time Domain Energy Integral] -- Parseval Equivalence --> freqDomain[Frequency Domain Energy Integral]
    timeDomain --> tFormula[Integral of absolute value of x of t squared dt]
    freqDomain --> fFormula[1 over 2 pi times Integral of absolute value of X of jOmega squared dOmega]
    tFormula --> result[Total Energy E in Joules]
    fFormula --> result
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct short-answer or one-step property application.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Module 2 questions on properties of CTFT often involve *deriving* a property and then *applying* it to a real signal.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the Continuous Time Fourier Transform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\Omega)$, then $x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\Omega t_0}\,X(j\Omega)$.

**Proof:**

By definition:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t - t_0)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt$$

Let $\tau = t - t_0 \Rightarrow dt = d\tau$:

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega(\tau + t_0)}\,d\tau = e^{-j\Omega t_0}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,d\tau = e^{-j\Omega t_0}\,X(j\Omega)$$

The magnitude spectrum $\vert e^{-j\Omega t_0} \vert = 1$, so the **magnitude is unchanged**; only the **phase** is shifted by $-\Omega t_0$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the duality property of the CTFT? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\Omega)$, then $X(jt) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\,x(-\Omega)$.

**Example:** The standard transform pair is:

$$\text{rect}(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2\pi}\right)$$

By duality:

$$\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{2\pi}\right) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} 2\pi\,\text{rect}(-\Omega) = 2\pi\,\text{rect}(\Omega)$$

since the rectangular function is even.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a) State and prove the convolution property of the Continuous Time Fourier Transform.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** $x_1(t) * x_2(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X_1(j\Omega) \cdot X_2(j\Omega)$.

**Proof:** Starting with the convolution integral and applying the Fourier Transform:

$$Y(j\Omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau)\,x_2(t - \tau)\,d\tau\right]e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt$$

Interchanging the integrals (justified by Fubini's theorem):

$$Y(j\Omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_2(t - \tau)\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt\right]d\tau$$

By the time-shifting property, the inner integral is $e^{-j\Omega \tau} X_2(j\Omega)$:

$$Y(j\Omega) = X_2(j\Omega)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x_1(\tau)\,e^{-j\Omega \tau}\,d\tau = X_1(j\Omega) \cdot X_2(j\Omega)$$

This completes the proof. **Engineering significance:** The output of an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ driven by input $x(t)$ is $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$, whose spectrum is $Y(j\Omega) = X(j\Omega) H(j\Omega)$. The product form is the basis of filtering.

---

**(b) Find the Fourier Transform of the triangular pulse** $\Delta(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ **using the convolution property.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recognize the triangular pulse as the convolution of two rectangular pulses.**

A rectangular pulse of width 1 and height 1:

$$p(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert t \vert \leq \frac{1}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Convolution of $p(t)$ with itself gives a triangular pulse of base 2 and peak 1 — which is exactly $\Delta(t)$ (after a horizontal shift of $\frac{1}{2}$, but for transform purposes the support matches):

$$\Delta(t) = p(t) * p(t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Fourier Transform of the rectangular pulse.**

$$P(j\Omega) = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt = \frac{2\sin(\Omega/2)}{\Omega} = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)$$

where the unnormalized sinc is $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the convolution property.**

$$\Delta(j\Omega) = P(j\Omega) \cdot P(j\Omega) = \left[\frac{2\sin(\Omega/2)}{\Omega}\right]^2 = \frac{4\sin^2(\Omega/2)}{\Omega^2}$$

**Step 4 — Express the final answer in closed form.**

$$\boxed{\Delta(j\Omega) = \left[\frac{\sin(\Omega/2)}{(\Omega/2)}\right]^2 = \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)}$$

This is the well-known **sinc-squared spectrum** of the triangular pulse.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern]**

**(a) State and prove Parseval's theorem for the Continuous Time Fourier Transform.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2 d\Omega$

**Proof:** Starting from $E = \int x(t)x^*(t)dt$ and substituting the inverse transform for $x^*(t)$:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \left[\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(j\Omega)\,e^{j\Omega t}d\Omega\right]^* dt$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\Omega)\left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{-j\Omega t}dt\right]d\Omega$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X^*(j\Omega) X(j\Omega)\,d\Omega = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2 d\Omega$$

**Engineering significance:** Parseval's theorem allows energy computation in whichever domain is more convenient. The quantity $\dfrac{\vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2}{2\pi}$ is called the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)**.

---

**(b) Given** $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$**, find its Fourier Transform** $X(j\Omega)$ **and verify Parseval's theorem.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Fourier Transform of $x(t)$.**

$$X(j\Omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t}\,e^{-j\Omega t}\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(2 + j\Omega)t}\,dt$$

$$= \left[\frac{e^{-(2 + j\Omega)t}}{-(2 + j\Omega)}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2 + j\Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the energy in the time domain.**

$$E_t = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t} \vert^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t}\,dt = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the energy in the frequency domain.**

$$\vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{(2 + j\Omega)(2 - j\Omega)} = \frac{1}{4 + \Omega^2}$$

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{4 + \Omega^2}\,d\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the frequency-domain integral using the standard result** $\int \frac{dx}{a^2 + x^2} = \frac{1}{a}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)$.

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \left[\frac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)\right]_{-\infty}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \pi = \frac{1}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Compare results.**

$$E_t = \frac{1}{4} = E_f \quad \checkmark$$

**Parseval's theorem is verified.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in CTFT Property Questions:**
> 1. **Missing the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor in the Multiplication (Modulation) Property.** Students often write $X_1(j\Omega) * X_2(j\Omega)$ and forget the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ scaling. **Always include the scalar.**
> 2. **Sign error in Time Shifting.** A shift of $+t_0$ in time corresponds to $e^{-j\Omega t_0}$ (negative sign in the exponent). Do not write $e^{+j\Omega t_0}$.
> 3. **Integration Property:** Forgetting the $\pi X(0)\delta(\Omega)$ term when $X(0) \neq 0$ (DC component present). If the signal is AC (zero average), this term vanishes.
> 4. **Parseval's Theorem:** Confusing the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor — it appears on the **frequency side**, not the time side.
> 5. **Convolution vs. Multiplication mix-up:** Convolution in time $\Rightarrow$ **multiplication** in frequency, and vice versa. Examiners specifically check this duality.
> 6. **Time Reversal with Time Shifting:** Students often forget that $x(-t)$ is equivalent to $x(at)$ with $a = -1$, and they apply the scaling formula incorrectly.
> 7. **Forgetting to state the property before proving it.** KTU examiners expect the **statement** first, then the **proof**. Marks are explicitly allocated for both.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of CTFT**

- **Linearity:** Linear combination in time $\Rightarrow$ same combination in frequency. Used to break signals into components.
- **Time Shifting:** $x(t - t_0) \Rightarrow e^{-j\Omega t_0} X(j\Omega)$. **Magnitude unchanged**, only phase shifted.
- **Frequency Shifting:** $e^{j\Omega_0 t}x(t) \Rightarrow X(j(\Omega - \Omega_0))$. Basis of **modulation**.
- **Time Scaling:** $x(at) \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} X\!\left(\dfrac{j\Omega}{a}\right)$. **Compression in time $\Rightarrow$ expansion in frequency.**
- **Time Reversal:** $x(-t) \Rightarrow X(-j\Omega)$. Equivalent to scaling with $a = -1$.
- **Differentiation:** $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} \Rightarrow j\Omega X(j\Omega)$. Emphasizes high frequencies.
- **Integration:** $\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)d\tau \Rightarrow \dfrac{X(j\Omega)}{j\Omega} + \pi X(0)\delta(\Omega)$.
- **Convolution in Time:** $x_1 * x_2 \Rightarrow X_1 X_2$. **Core of LTI system theory.**
- **Multiplication in Time:** $x_1 \cdot x_2 \Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2\pi} X_1 * X_2$. **Core of modulation theory.**
- **Duality:** $X(jt) \Rightarrow 2\pi x(-\Omega)$. Allows reverse engineering of transform pairs.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Energy in time = $\frac{1}{2\pi} \times$ Energy in frequency. Energy spectral density is $\dfrac{\vert X(j\Omega) \vert^2}{2\pi}$.
- **Engineering Hotspots:** AM radio, filter design, audio equalization, image processing, control systems — all rely on these properties.
- **KTU Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always state the property, derive it step-by-step, then apply it. Examiners award partial marks for **each** correct algebraic transition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Transform. Bilateral Laplace Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Transform. Bilateral Laplace Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Inverse Laplace Transform — Formal Definition

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is the mathematical operation that recovers a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ from its Laplace-domain representation $X(s)$. It is the reverse-engineering process of the Laplace Transform.

If the forward (unilateral) Laplace transform is defined as:

$$X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

Then the inverse transform is formally given by the **Bromwich Integral**:

$$x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{X(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) \, e^{st} \, ds$$

where the integration is performed along a vertical line $\text{Re}(s) = \sigma$ lying entirely within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(s)$, and $j = \sqrt{-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT402 Module 2):** Students are expected to compute the inverse Laplace transform using the **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) method** and understand the role of the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** in determining uniqueness.

### 1.2 The Bilateral (Two-Sided) Laplace Transform — Formal Definition

The **Bilateral Laplace Transform** extends the integration range to the entire real line $(-\infty, +\infty)$:

$$X_B(s) = \mathcal{L}_B\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bilateral vs. Unilateral:** The bilateral transform preserves information about the signal for **negative time** ($t < 0$), unlike the unilateral transform which implicitly assumes $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. The ROC of a bilateral Laplace transform is a **vertical strip** in the $s$-plane (rather than a right-half plane).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

#### Analogy for the Inverse Laplace Transform:
> Imagine you baked a cake (the signal $x(t)$) and put it into a "blender" (the forward Laplace transform) to get a smooth milkshake $X(s)$. The inverse Laplace transform is the **recipe reversal process** — figuring out the original cake ingredients and proportions from the blended shake. In engineering, we use **PFE** as a "de-blending" technique that breaks the complex $X(s)$ into simple, recognizable recipes (exponentials, sines, cosines).

#### Analogy for the Bilateral Laplace Transform:
> Think of a **two-way street**, whereas the unilateral Laplace transform is a **one-way street**. The one-way (unilateral) street only lets you analyze what happens from $t=0$ onwards (the **causal future**). The bilateral street lets you observe events from $t=-\infty$ to $t=+\infty$, capturing both the past and the future. This is essential for analyzing **non-causal** signals (like audio played in reverse, or anti-causal filters).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The standard complex frequency variable is $s = \sigma + j\omega$, measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- The real part $\sigma$ has units of **Neper per second (Np/s)** — the rate of exponential decay or growth.
- The imaginary part $j\omega$ represents **angular frequency** in **radians per second**.
- The contour integral constant is $\frac{1}{2\pi j}$, a normalization factor ensuring energy/amplitude preservation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Existence Condition:** A bilateral Laplace transform exists if and only if the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert \, e^{-\sigma t} \, dt$ is finite. The set of $\sigma$ values for which this holds is the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps for Computing the Inverse Laplace Transform (PFE Method)

The Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) method is the **primary computational tool** for KTU board examinations. The structured logic is as follows:

1. **Verify Rational Form**: Confirm $X(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ is a proper rational function ($\deg N(s) < \deg D(s)$). If improper, perform polynomial long division first.

2. **Factor the Denominator**: Express $D(s)$ as a product of linear and irreducible quadratic factors (i.e., real poles and complex conjugate pole pairs).

3. **Classify Pole Types**:
   - **Real distinct poles** (e.g., $s = -a, -b$)
   - **Real repeated poles** (e.g., $(s+a)^2$)
   - **Complex conjugate poles** (e.g., $(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2$)

4. **Construct the Expansion**: Write $X(s)$ as a sum of simple fractions matching each pole.

5. **Solve for Residues**: Use the cover-up method, Heaviside's formula, or derivative rules.

6. **Apply Standard Pairs**: Replace each term with its corresponding time-domain signal.

7. **Verify Causality/Anti-causality**: The ROC dictates whether the resulting time-domain signal is right-sided, left-sided, or two-sided.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **frequently tested formulas** for this topic. All absolute value bars use `\vert` notation to prevent markdown table corruption.

| # | $X(s)$ (Laplace Domain) | $x(t)$ (Time Domain, Causal ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > a$) | ROC Condition |
|---|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\frac{1}{s}$ | $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 2 | $\frac{1}{s+a}$ | $e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 3 | $\frac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ | $t e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{(s+a)^n}$ | $\frac{t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 5 | $\frac{\omega_0}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $\sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 6 | $\frac{s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $\cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 7 | $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $e^{-at} \sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 8 | $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 9 | $\frac{1}{s+a}$ (Anti-causal) | $-e^{-at} u(-t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) < -a$ |
| 10 | $\frac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\sinh(at) u(t)$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |

### 2.3 Bilateral Laplace Transform — Key Properties

The bilateral Laplace transform inherits all properties of the unilateral transform, but with a few important distinctions:

- **Linearity**: $\mathcal{L}_B\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a X_{B1}(s) + b X_{B2}(s)$, with ROC containing $\text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$.

- **Time Shifting**: $\mathcal{L}_B\{x(t - t_0)\} = e^{-s t_0} X_B(s)$, ROC unchanged.

- **Time Reversal**: $\mathcal{L}_B\{x(-t)\} = X_B(-s)$, ROC reversed about $\sigma = 0$.

- **Frequency Shifting**: $\mathcal{L}_B\{e^{s_0 t} x(t)\} = X_B(s - s_0)$, ROC shifted right by $\text{Re}(s_0)$.

- **Differentiation in Time**: $\mathcal{L}_B\left\{\frac{dx(t)}{dt}\right\} = s X_B(s)$ **(only if $x(t)$ has no impulses at infinity or zero that contribute boundary terms)**.

- **Convolution**: $\mathcal{L}_B\{x_1(t) * x_2(t)\} = X_{B1}(s) \cdot X_{B2}(s)$, ROC contains $\text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Bilateral / Inverse Laplace |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Control Systems** | Stability analysis via ROC; partial fraction for time-response derivation |
| **Signal Processing** | Filter design for non-causal systems (e.g., image processing, equalizers) |
| **Communications** | Analysis of two-sided signals in modulation/demodulation |
| **Circuit Analysis** | Solving RLC circuits with both pre-initial and post-initial conditions |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | Processing ECG/EEG signals which can be non-causal in nature |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Bromwich Inverse Laplace Transform Integral

Starting from the forward Laplace transform:

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-st} \, dt$$

Substitute $s = \sigma + j\omega$:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ x(t) e^{-\sigma t} \right] e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

This is recognized as the **Fourier Transform** of the signal $g(t) = x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$.

Applying the inverse Fourier Transform:

$$g(t) = x(t) e^{-\sigma t} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\sigma + j\omega) \, e^{j\omega t} \, d\omega$$

Multiplying both sides by $e^{\sigma t}$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\sigma + j\omega) \, e^{(\sigma + j\omega)t} \, d\omega$$

Since $s = \sigma + j\omega$, we have $ds = j \, d\omega \Rightarrow d\omega = \frac{ds}{j}$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) \, e^{st} \, \frac{ds}{j}$$

This simplifies to the **Bromwich Integral**:

$$\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) \, e^{st} \, ds}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — PFE for Distinct Real Poles (Unilateral)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Laplace transform of:

$$X(s) = \frac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$$

**Step 1:** Factor the denominator.

$$s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s+2)(s+3)$$

**Step 2:** Write the partial fraction expansion.

$$\frac{2s+5}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s+2} + \frac{B}{s+3}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for residues using the cover-up method.

For $A$ (set $s = -2$):

$$A = \left. \frac{2s+5}{s+3} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{2(-2)+5}{-2+3} = \frac{-4+5}{1} = 1$$

For $B$ (set $s = -3$):

$$B = \left. \frac{2s+5}{s+2} \right|_{s=-3} = \frac{2(-3)+5}{-3+2} = \frac{-6+5}{-1} = \frac{-1}{-1} = 1$$

**Step 4:** Rewrite and apply standard pairs.

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} + \frac{1}{s+3}$$

**Step 5:** Take the inverse transform (causal ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > -2$).

$$x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{-3t} u(t)$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Complex Conjugate Poles (Unilateral)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Laplace transform of:

$$X(s) = \frac{s + 3}{s^2 + 4s + 13}$$

**Step 1:** Complete the square in the denominator.

$$s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s+2)^2 + 9 = (s+2)^2 + 3^2$$

**Step 2:** Rewrite the numerator to match the standard form.

$$s + 3 = (s+2) + 1$$

Therefore:

$$X(s) = \frac{(s+2)}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} + \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply standard transform pairs.

From the formula sheet:
- $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \longleftrightarrow e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$
- $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \longleftrightarrow e^{-at} \sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$

**Step 4:** Final time-domain answer (causal ROC).

$$x(t) = e^{-2t} \cos(3t) u(t) + \frac{1}{3} e^{-2t} \sin(3t) u(t)$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Bilateral Laplace Transform with ROC Analysis

**Problem:** Find the bilateral Laplace transform of:

$$x(t) = e^{-a \vert t \vert}, \quad a > 0$$

**Step 1:** Split the signal into causal and anti-causal parts using $u(t)$ and $u(-t)$.

$$x(t) = e^{-at} u(t) + e^{at} u(-t)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the bilateral transform of each part.

For $x_1(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$:

$$X_1(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} e^{-st} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} dt = \frac{1}{s+a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -a$$

For $x_2(t) = e^{at} u(-t)$:

$$X_2(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{at} e^{-st} dt = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{-(s-a)t} dt = \frac{1}{a-s} = \frac{-1}{s-a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) < a$$

**Step 3:** Combine by linearity.

$$X_B(s) = \frac{1}{s+a} - \frac{1}{s-a} = \frac{(s-a) - (s+a)}{(s+a)(s-a)} = \frac{-2a}{s^2 - a^2}$$

**Step 4:** Determine the overall ROC.

$$\text{ROC: } -a < \text{Re}(s) < a$$

This is a **vertical strip** in the $s$-plane, characteristic of a two-sided signal.

### 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Inverse Bilateral Transform with ROC

**Problem:** Given $X(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 - 1}$ with ROC: $-1 < \text{Re}(s) < 1$, find $x(t)$.

**Step 1:** Factor and perform PFE.

$$\frac{1}{s^2 - 1} = \frac{1}{(s-1)(s+1)} = \frac{A}{s-1} + \frac{B}{s+1}$$

For $A$ (set $s = 1$):

$$A = \left. \frac{1}{s+1} \right|_{s=1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

For $B$ (set $s = -1$):

$$B = \left. \frac{1}{s-1} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{-1}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Write the expansion.

$$X(s) = \frac{1/2}{s-1} + \frac{-1/2}{s+1}$$

**Step 3:** Apply inverse bilateral transform pairs with ROC awareness.

- $\frac{1}{s-1}$: Pole at $s=+1$. Since ROC has $\text{Re}(s) < 1$, this is **anti-causal**: $\longleftrightarrow -e^{t} u(-t)$
- $\frac{1}{s+1}$: Pole at $s=-1$. Since ROC has $\text{Re}(s) > -1$, this is **causal**: $\longleftrightarrow e^{-t} u(t)$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$x(t) = -\frac{1}{2} e^{t} u(-t) - \frac{1}{2} e^{-t} u(t) = -\frac{1}{2} e^{-\vert t \vert}$$

### 3.6 Inverse Transform via Residue Theorem (Advanced)

The Bromwich integral can be evaluated by closing the contour in the left-half plane (LHP) for $t > 0$ or right-half plane (RHP) for $t < 0$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k} \text{Res}\left[ X(s) e^{st}, s = p_k \right]$$

where the sum is taken over poles enclosed by the closed contour. For a simple pole at $s = p_k$:

$$\text{Res} = \lim_{s \to p_k} (s - p_k) X(s) e^{st}$$

For a pole of order $m$ at $s = p_k$:

$$\text{Res} = \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{s \to p_k} \frac{d^{m-1}}{ds^{m-1}} \left[ (s - p_k)^m X(s) e^{st} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ROC matters:** The contour closure direction depends on the ROC. For ROC strictly to the right of all poles (right-sided signal), close LHP for $t > 0$ and RHP for $t < 0$ (giving zero). The reverse applies for left-sided signals.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Computing the Inverse Laplace Transform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given X of s] --> B{Is X s rational?}
    B -- No --> C[Use known transform pairs or convolution property]
    B -- Yes --> D{Check proper fraction: deg N less than deg D}
    D -- No --> E[Perform polynomial long division to get quotient and remainder]
    E --> F[Work with proper rational remainder]
    D -- Yes --> F
    F --> G[Factor denominator D of s into linear and quadratic factors]
    G --> H{Classify pole types}
    H -- Distinct real poles --> I1[Use cover up method for each residue]
    H -- Repeated real poles --> I2[Use derivative formula for repeated residues]
    H -- Complex conjugate poles --> I3[Complete the square and match standard pair]
    I1 --> J[Sum partial fractions]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    C --> J
    J --> K{Check ROC type}
    K -- Right sided --> L1[Append u of t to all terms]
    K -- Left sided --> L2[Append negative u of negative t with sign flip]
    K -- Two sided --> L3[Split into causal and anticausal parts]
    L1 --> M[Final answer: x of t]
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Bilateral Laplace Transform Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["Time Domain Signal"]
        A1["x of t defined for all real t"]
    end
    subgraph ForwardPath["Bilateral Laplace Engine"]
        B1[Multiply by e to the power negative s t] --> B2[Integrate from negative infinity to positive infinity]
    end
    subgraph ROCSpace["s Plane Analysis"]
        C1[Identify pole locations] --> C2[Determine ROC as vertical strip]
    end
    subgraph Output["Frequency Domain"]
        D1["X of s with bounded ROC"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B2 --> C1
    C2 --> D1
    D1 -.Inverse Path.-> E1[Bromwich Contour Integral] --> E2[Sum of Residues] --> A1
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix: ROC Type to Time-Domain Signal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Pole at s equals negative a] --> ROCQ{Where is ROC relative to pole?}
    ROCQ -- ROC to the RIGHT of pole --> Causal[Signal is RIGHT SIDED: multiply by u of t]
    ROCQ -- ROC to the LEFT of pole --> Anticausal[Signal is LEFT SIDED: multiply by negative u of negative t]
    ROCQ -- ROC excludes the pole --> Twosided[Signal is TWO SIDED: use both exponentials]
    Causal --> Out1[Example: e to the power negative a t times u of t]
    Anticausal --> Out2[Example: negative e to the power negative a t times u of negative t]
    Twosided --> Out3[Example: e to the power negative a times absolute t]
```

### 4.4 Pole-Zero and ROC Map for $X(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 - a^2}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPlane["s Plane: Vertical Strip between poles"]
        P1((Pole at s equals negative a)) --- ROC[ROC: negative a less than Re of s less than a]
        P2((Pole at s equals positive a))
    end
    ROC -.bounded by.-> P1
    ROC -.bounded by.-> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The ROC (shaded strip) is the region between the two poles (denoted by the `(( ))` circles). Any vertical line drawn within this strip can serve as the Bromwich contour for inverse transformation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the Inverse Laplace Transform. State the conditions under which the inverse Laplace transform exists and is unique.

**Model Answer:**

The Inverse Laplace Transform is the mathematical operation that recovers the time-domain signal $x(t)$ from its Laplace-domain representation $X(s)$. It is defined by the Bromwich integral:

$$x(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{X(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) e^{st} ds$$

**Conditions for existence and uniqueness:**
1. **Existence:** The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert e^{-\sigma t} dt$ must converge for some $\sigma$, defining a non-empty Region of Convergence (ROC).
2. **Uniqueness:** Two different signals can have the same $X(s)$ algebraic expression but different ROCs. Therefore, the **ROC must be specified** along with $X(s)$ to uniquely identify $x(t)$ (with the additional requirement that $x(t)$ has finite duration or satisfies Dirichlet conditions almost everywhere).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between the unilateral and bilateral Laplace transforms. Why is the bilateral transform essential for analyzing non-causal signals?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unilateral Laplace | Bilateral Laplace |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Limits of integration** | $0$ to $\infty$ | $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ |
| **Implied signal property** | $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (causal) | Signal defined for all $t$ |
| **ROC shape** | Right-half plane (vertical strip right of all poles) | Vertical strip between poles |
| **Information captured** | Only future / causal content | Both past and future |
| **Inverse uniqueness** | Unique if causal ROC specified | Requires ROC specification |

The bilateral transform is **essential for non-causal signals** because non-causal signals (like $x(t) = e^{at} u(-t)$ or $x(t) = \sin(-t)$) have energy/values for $t < 0$, which the unilateral transform completely ignores. The bilateral transform captures this two-sided information, making it the only correct tool for systems analysis where anti-causal components are present (e.g., image processing, two-sided filter design).

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Module 2 questions in Part B offer internal choice. Students must attempt **one** of the two options provided below. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — PFE Mastery + Complex Poles**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Find the inverse Laplace transform of:

$$X(s) = \frac{5s + 7}{s^2 + 3s + 2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -1$$

**(b)** Determine the inverse Laplace transform of:

$$X(s) = \frac{2s + 10}{s^2 + 4s + 8}$$

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Factor the denominator.

$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the PFE form.

$$\frac{5s+7}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve for residues using cover-up method.

$$A = \left. \frac{5s+7}{s+2} \right|_{s=-1} = \frac{5(-1)+7}{-1+2} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

$$B = \left. \frac{5s+7}{s+1} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{5(-2)+7}{-2+1} = \frac{-3}{-1} = 3$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Rewrite $X(s)$.

$$X(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} + \frac{3}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply standard pairs (causal ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > -1$).

$$\boxed{x(t) = 2 e^{-t} u(t) + 3 e^{-2t} u(t)}$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Complete the square in the denominator.

$$s^2 + 4s + 8 = (s+2)^2 + 4 = (s+2)^2 + 2^2$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Decompose the numerator to match standard forms.

$$2s + 10 = 2(s+2) + 6$$

$$X(s) = \frac{2(s+2)}{(s+2)^2 + 2^2} + \frac{6}{(s+2)^2 + 2^2} = \frac{2(s+2)}{(s+2)^2 + 2^2} + 3 \cdot \frac{2}{(s+2)^2 + 2^2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply standard transform pairs.

- $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \longleftrightarrow e^{-at} \cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$
- $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \longleftrightarrow e^{-at} \sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer.

$$\boxed{x(t) = 2 e^{-2t} \cos(2t) u(t) + 3 e^{-2t} \sin(2t) u(t)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Bilateral Laplace Transform + ROC**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{2t} u(-t) + e^{-3t} u(t)$, compute the bilateral Laplace transform and specify the ROC.

**(b)** Given $X(s) = \frac{3s + 5}{s^2 - s - 6}$ with ROC: $-1 < \text{Re}(s) < 2$, find the inverse bilateral Laplace transform $x(t)$.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Split into causal and anti-causal parts.

- Anti-causal: $x_1(t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$
- Causal: $x_2(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the bilateral transform of $x_1(t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$.

$$X_1(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{2t} e^{-st} dt = \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{-(s-2)t} dt$$

For convergence, we need $\text{Re}(s-2) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Re}(s) < 2$:

$$X_1(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s-2)t}}{-(s-2)} \right]_{-\infty}^{0} = \frac{1}{-(s-2)} [1 - 0] = \frac{1}{2-s} = \frac{-1}{s-2}$$

ROC: $\text{Re}(s) < 2$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the bilateral transform of $x_2(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$.

$$X_2(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t} e^{-st} dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+3)t} dt$$

For convergence, we need $\text{Re}(s+3) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Re}(s) > -3$:

$$X_2(s) = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+3)t}}{-(s+3)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{0 - 1}{-(s+3)} = \frac{1}{s+3}$$

ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > -3$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Combine by linearity.

$$X_B(s) = X_1(s) + X_2(s) = \frac{-1}{s-2} + \frac{1}{s+3}$$

$$X_B(s) = \frac{-(s+3) + (s-2)}{(s-2)(s+3)} = \frac{-5}{s^2 + s - 6}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Specify the ROC as the intersection.

$$\boxed{X_B(s) = \frac{-5}{s^2 + s - 6}, \quad \text{ROC: } -3 < \text{Re}(s) < 2}$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Factor the denominator.

$$s^2 - s - 6 = (s-3)(s+2)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the PFE form.

$$\frac{3s+5}{(s-3)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s-3} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve for residues.

$$A = \left. \frac{3s+5}{s+2} \right|_{s=3} = \frac{9+5}{3+2} = \frac{14}{5}$$

$$B = \left. \frac{3s+5}{s-3} \right|_{s=-2} = \frac{-6+5}{-2-3} = \frac{-1}{-5} = \frac{1}{5}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Rewrite $X(s)$.

$$X(s) = \frac{14/5}{s-3} + \frac{1/5}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Apply inverse bilateral transform pairs based on ROC.

Given ROC: $-1 < \text{Re}(s) < 2$... wait, this ROC is **inconsistent** with poles at $s=3$ and $s=-2$. 

**Correction:** The proper ROC consistent with two poles is $-2 < \text{Re}(s) < 3$. Re-evaluating:

- Pole at $s=3$: ROC is to the **left** of this pole ($s < 3$), so the term is **anti-causal**:

$$\frac{14/5}{s-3} \longleftrightarrow -\frac{14}{5} e^{3t} u(-t)$$

- Pole at $s=-2$: ROC is to the **right** of this pole ($s > -2$), so the term is **causal**:

$$\frac{1/5}{s+2} \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{5} e^{-2t} u(t)$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Final answer (with corrected ROC).

$$\boxed{x(t) = \frac{1}{5} e^{-2t} u(t) - \frac{14}{5} e^{3t} u(-t), \quad \text{ROC: } -2 < \text{Re}(s) < 3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC Specification (–2 marks):** Many students write $X(s) = \frac{1}{s+2}$ and immediately write $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$. This is **wrong** without specifying ROC. The same $X(s)$ with ROC $\text{Re}(s) < -2$ gives $x(t) = -e^{-2t} u(-t)$. **Always state the ROC.**
>
> 2. **Sign Errors in PFE Residues (–1 to –2 marks):** Be extremely careful with the cover-up method sign. The common mistake: $B = \left. \frac{N(s)}{s-p_{\text{other}}} \right|_{s=p_B}$, not $s = -p_B$.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\sin$ and $\cos$ Standard Pairs (–2 marks):** For $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$, the inverse is $\cos$. For $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$, the inverse is $\sin$. Mixing them up loses complete marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the Unit Step $u(t)$ (–1 mark):** Even in causal unilateral problems, including $u(t)$ explicitly is required by KTU rubrics for full marks. A bare $e^{-at}$ is technically ambiguous.
>
> 5. **Bilateral Contour Misjudgment (–3 marks):** In bilateral problems, the rule is: **Pole to the left of ROC = causal ($u(t)$); Pole to the right of ROC = anti-causal ($-u(-t)$).** Memorize this rule.
>
> 6. **Not Simplifying After Completing the Square (–1 mark):** Always factor out the coefficient of $\omega_0$ in the numerator to match the standard $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$ form. A coefficient of $3$ when $\omega_0 = 2$ must be written as $\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{...}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-minute exam prep):**

- **Inverse Laplace Transform (Bromwich):** $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} X(s) e^{st} ds$, integrated over a vertical line inside the ROC.

- **Bilateral Laplace Transform:** $X_B(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{-st} dt$. Captures both past ($t<0$) and future ($t>0$) signal content.

- **ROC Uniqueness Rule:** ROC must **exclude all poles** and is bounded by the poles. A right-sided signal has a right-half-plane ROC; left-sided has a left-half-plane ROC; two-sided has a **vertical strip** ROC.

- **Causality Decision Rule:** Pole at $s = p_k$ with ROC strictly to its right $\Rightarrow$ causal (multiply by $u(t)$). ROC strictly to its left $\Rightarrow$ anti-causal (multiply by $-u(-t)$).

- **Poles Location and Stability:** For causal systems, poles in **LHP** $\Rightarrow$ **BIBO stable**. Poles in **RHP** $\Rightarrow$ unstable. Poles on $j\omega$-axis $\Rightarrow$ marginally stable (simple poles) or unstable (repeated poles).

- **PFE Steps:** Verify proper → Factor → Choose form → Cover-up residues → Apply pairs → Multiply by $u(t)$ or $-u(-t)$ based on ROC.

- **Key Standard Pairs to Memorize:**
  - $\frac{1}{s+a} \leftrightarrow e^{-at} u(t)$
  - $\frac{1}{(s+a)^2} \leftrightarrow t e^{-at} u(t)$
  - $\frac{\omega_0}{s^2 + \omega_0^2} \leftrightarrow \sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$
  - $\frac{s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2} \leftrightarrow \cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$
  - $\frac{\omega_0}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \leftrightarrow e^{-at}\sin(\omega_0 t) u(t)$
  - $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2} \leftrightarrow e^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$

- **Bilateral to Unilateral Connection:** If $x(t)$ is causal, $X_B(s) = X_U(s)$ and the ROC of the bilateral is the same as the unilateral's ROC.

- **Existence Theorem:** A bilateral Laplace transform **exists if and only if** the signal is of **exponential order** in both directions (decays as $t \to \pm\infty$). The ROC is always a non-empty vertical strip in the $s$-plane.

- **Convolution Property (Bilateral):** $\mathcal{L}_B\{x_1(t) * x_2(t)\} = X_{B1}(s) X_{B2}(s)$, with ROC $\supseteq \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2$ (the ROC can shrink or stay the same due to pole-zero cancellations).

- **Exam Mantra:** *"State the ROC. State the ROC. State the ROC."* — This single habit can save 2–3 marks on every bilateral transform problem.

- **Final Sanity Check Rule:** Degree of numerator $<$ degree of denominator for proper rational functions. If not, perform **long division first** and invert the polynomial (impulse train) and proper remainder separately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of ROC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of ROC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Z-Transform**, the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** is formally defined as the set of all complex values of the variable $z$ (where $z = re^{j\omega}$ in polar form) for which the Z-transform summation converges to a finite, bounded value.

Mathematically, the bilateral Z-transform is expressed as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

The ROC is the subset of the complex z-plane where $\vert X(z) \vert < \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCECT402 Module 2):** The ROC is the region in the z-plane where the Z-transform of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ exists (converges to a finite value). It is bounded by the poles of $X(z)$ and excludes the poles themselves.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pouring water from a fountain onto an infinite flat ground. The ground has a few **drainage holes (poles)**. The **ROC** is the dry region of the ground where water **does not drain away infinitely** — it stays in finite puddles. Wherever the water drains (poles), the ground is **not** in the ROC. So, the ROC is the "safe walking zone" around the poles where the mathematical series of the Z-transform produces a sensible (finite) result.

A second analogy: think of a radio receiver tuning across frequencies. The ROC is the range of "frequencies" (complex exponentials $z^n$) for which the signal has a bounded, well-defined energy representation. Outside the ROC, the signal "explodes" in amplitude, just like feedback in a microphone.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Pole** | A value of $z$ where $X(z) \to \infty$ (denominator root) |
| **Zero** | A value of $z$ where $X(z) = 0$ (numerator root) |
| **Unit Circle** | The contour $\vert z \vert = 1$ in the z-plane |
| **Causal Signal** | $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (right-sided) |
| **Anti-causal Signal** | $x[n] = 0$ for $n > 0$ (left-sided) |
| **Two-sided Signal** | Non-zero for both positive and negative $n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Two different signals can have **identical Z-transform algebraic expressions** but **different ROCs**. The ROC is therefore an **integral part** of the Z-transform pair — without specifying the ROC, the inverse Z-transform is **not unique**.

### Physical / Engineering Significance

- The **location of the ROC relative to the unit circle** directly determines the **stability** of a discrete-time LTI system.
- A **causal and stable** system has its ROC strictly **outside the outermost pole AND must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- The ROC concept is foundational in **digital filter design**, **DSP controller implementation**, and **control system stability analysis** in industries ranging from audio processing to aerospace guidance systems.
- The standard metric used to assess convergence is the **absolute summability** condition: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x[n]\,z^{-n}\vert < \infty$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Existence of the Z-Transform

The Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ exists if and only if the infinite sum converges absolutely:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \left\vert x[n]\,z^{-n} \right\vert < \infty$$

By substituting $z = re^{j\omega}$, the absolute value simplifies as $\vert z^{-n} \vert = r^{-n}$. The convergence condition becomes:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \left\vert x[n] \right\vert \, r^{-n} < \infty$$

This single inequality must be satisfied; it determines the **inner radius** $R_1$ and **outer radius** $R_2$ of the ROC.

### 2.2 The Seven Cardinal Properties of ROC

The following properties are **routinely tested** in KTU university exams and must be memorized:

**Property 1 — Bounded by Poles**
The ROC is bounded by poles (and never includes any pole) of $X(z)$. If $X(z)$ is rational, the ROC is a ring (annular region) in the z-plane:

$$R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$$

It may extend to $R_1 = 0$ (excluding origin) or $R_2 = \infty$ (including infinity).

**Property 2 — Pole-Exclusion Rule**
Poles are **always excluded** from the ROC. At a pole, $\vert X(z) \vert \to \infty$, violating the convergence condition.

**Property 3 — Connected Region**
The ROC is a **connected region**. It cannot consist of disjoint patches in the z-plane (this is a theorem, not an empirical observation).

**Property 4 — Finite-Duration Sequences**
If $x[n]$ is **finite in duration** (non-zero only over a finite interval $N_1 \le n \le N_2$), then the ROC is the **entire z-plane** except possibly two points: $z = 0$ and $z = \infty$.
- If $N_1 \ge 0$ (right-sided finite), ROC includes $\infty$, excludes $0$.
- If $N_2 \le 0$ (left-sided finite), ROC includes $0$, excludes $\infty$.
- If both sides finite ($N_1 < 0 < N_2$), ROC is the entire z-plane.

**Property 5 — Right-Sided (Causal) Sequences**
For a **right-sided** sequence where $x[n] = 0$ for $n < N_1$, the ROC extends **outward** from the outermost pole to infinity:

$$\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$$

**Property 6 — Left-Sided (Anti-causal) Sequences**
For a **left-sided** sequence where $x[n] = 0$ for $n > N_2$, the ROC extends **inward** from the innermost pole to the origin:

$$\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$$

**Property 7 — Two-Sided Sequences**
For a **two-sided** sequence, the ROC is a **ring** between two poles:

$$R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$$

If no such annulus exists (e.g., only one pole or coincident poles), the Z-transform does **not exist**.

### 2.3 ROC and System Stability

A discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable) if and only if:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

This requires the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ to lie **within** the ROC of the system's transfer function $H(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Theorem:** A **causal LTI system is stable if and only if all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** (i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles $p_k$). Equivalently, the ROC must be the exterior of a circle of radius $< 1$, which automatically includes $\vert z \vert = 1$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Property** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** |
|------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Bilateral Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | Definition |
| Convergence Condition | $\sum \vert x[n] z^{-n} \vert < \infty$ | ROC existence |
| Polar form of $z$ | $z = re^{j\omega}$ | Magnitude & Phase |
| Magnitude term | $\vert z^{-n} \vert = r^{-n}$ | Decay/growth factor |
| General ROC form | $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ | Annular region |
| Causal ROC | $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$ | Right-sided sequence |
| Anti-causal ROC | $\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$ | Left-sided sequence |
| Two-sided ROC | $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ | Ring between poles |
| Stability condition | $\vert z \vert = 1 \in \text{ROC}$ | Unit circle inside ROC |
| Causal + Stable | $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all $k$ | All poles inside unit circle |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio Processing (MP3, AAC codecs):** ROC analysis guarantees that the inverse Z-transform of a designed filter is causal and stable before hardware implementation.
- **Control Engineering (Robotics, CNC Machines):** Stability of discrete-time controllers is verified by checking that ROC of the closed-loop transfer function includes the unit circle.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing (ECG, EEG):** ROC conditions in adaptive filters prevent filter divergence in real-time patient monitoring systems.
- **Communications (LTE/5G Channel Equalizers):** Decision-Feedback Equalizers (DFE) rely on ROC analysis to ensure causal-stable recursive structures.
- **Radar & Sonar Signal Processing:** Matched filters and correlators are designed using ROC properties to handle anti-causal pre-ringing for waveform analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ROC of the Causal Exponential Sequence

**Problem:** Determine the ROC of the Z-transform of $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, where $a$ is a real constant and $u[n]$ is the unit step.

**Step 1 — Write the Z-transform definition**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} a^n u[n]\, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the step function constraint**

Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$, the lower limit collapses:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} a^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the geometric series**

This is a geometric series of the form $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n$, which converges if and only if $\vert r \vert < 1$. Here $r = a z^{-1}$, so:

$$\left\vert a z^{-1} \right\vert < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{\vert a \vert}{\vert z \vert} < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 4 — Compute the closed-form sum**

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \qquad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 5 — Identify the pole**

The pole is located at $z = a$. The ROC is the **exterior of the circle of radius $\vert a \vert$** centered at the origin, excluding the pole itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For the causal exponential $a^n u[n]$, the Z-transform $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ has ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$, which is the **standard causal ROC form**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ROC of the Anti-Causal Exponential Sequence

**Problem:** Determine the ROC of $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$.

**Step 1 — Apply the shifted step function constraint**

Since $u[-n-1] = 0$ for $n \ge 0$ and $u[-n-1] = 1$ for $n \le -1$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} -a^n z^{-n} = -\sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} a^n z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Change of index variable**

Let $m = -n$, so $n = -m$. When $n = -\infty$, $m = +\infty$; when $n = -1$, $m = 1$:

$$X(z) = -\sum_{m=1}^{+\infty} a^{-m} z^{m} = -\sum_{m=1}^{+\infty} (a^{-1} z)^m$$

**Step 3 — Apply convergence criterion**

The geometric series $\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} (a^{-1} z)^m$ converges if $\vert a^{-1} z \vert < 1$:

$$\frac{\vert z \vert}{\vert a \vert} < 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$$

**Step 4 — Closed-form evaluation**

$$\sum_{m=1}^{\infty} r^m = \frac{r}{1 - r} \quad \text{for} \quad \vert r \vert < 1$$

With $r = a^{-1} z$:

$$X(z) = -\frac{a^{-1} z}{1 - a^{-1} z} = -\frac{z}{a - z} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the causal case**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** Both $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ (causal) and $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$ (anti-causal) yield the **algebraically identical** Z-transform $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, but with **different ROCs**:
> - Causal: ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$
> - Anti-causal: ROC is $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$
>
> This proves that the ROC is **essential** for uniquely specifying the inverse Z-transform.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ROC for Different Signal Types

| **Signal Type** | **Example** | **Pole(s) of $X(z)$** | **ROC** | **Includes Unit Circle?** |
|-----------------|-------------|------------------------|---------|---------------------------|
| Finite-duration (right-sided) | $\delta[n] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2]$ | None | $\vert z \vert > 0$ (entire plane except $0$) | ✅ Yes |
| Finite-duration (left-sided) | $\delta[n] + \delta[n+1]$ | None | $\vert z \vert < \infty$ (entire plane except $\infty$) | ✅ Yes |
| Finite-duration (two-sided) | $\delta[n+1] + \delta[n] + \delta[n-1]$ | None | Entire z-plane | ✅ Yes |
| Causal exponential | $a^n u[n], \vert a \vert < 1$ | $z = a$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | ✅ Yes (stable) |
| Causal exponential | $a^n u[n], \vert a \vert > 1$ | $z = a$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | ❌ No (unstable) |
| Anti-causal exponential | $-a^n u[-n-1], \vert a \vert > 1$ | $z = a$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | ✅ Yes (stable) |
| Anti-causal exponential | $-a^n u[-n-1], \vert a \vert < 1$ | $z = a$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | ❌ No (unstable) |
| Two-sided sequence | $a^n u[n] + b^n u[-n-1], \vert a \vert < \vert b \vert$ | $z = a, z = b$ | $\vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < \vert b \vert$ | ✅ Yes if $\vert a \vert < 1 < \vert b \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition Trick (for KTU exams):**
> - **Causal + Stable** = ROC is **outside** outermost pole **AND** includes unit circle → $\vert p_{\max} \vert < 1$
> - **Anti-causal + Stable** = ROC is **inside** innermost pole **AND** includes unit circle → $\vert p_{\min} \vert > 1$
> - **Two-sided + Stable** = ROC is a **ring** containing unit circle → $\vert p_1 \vert < 1 < \vert p_2 \vert$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Determining ROC from a Pole-Zero Plot

**Given:** A causal discrete-time LTI system has the pole-zero plot shown below:
- Pole at $z_1 = 0.5$ (real axis)
- Pole at $z_2 = -0.7$ (real axis)
- No zeros

**Step 1 — Identify the transfer function form**

$$H(z) = \frac{K}{(z - 0.5)(z + 0.7)} = \frac{K}{z^2 + 0.2z - 0.35}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the causal ROC**

Since the system is **causal**, the ROC extends outward from the outermost pole:

$$\text{Outermost pole magnitude} = \max(\vert 0.5 \vert, \vert -0.7 \vert) = 0.7$$

Therefore:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.7$$

**Step 3 — Test stability**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies **inside** the ROC ($1 > 0.7$ ✅), so the system is **stable**.

**Step 4 — BIBO check**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty \quad \text{(verified by ROC including unit circle)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Answer Format:** "Since the system is causal, ROC is $\vert z \vert > 0.7$. As the unit circle lies in the ROC, the system is BIBO stable."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of ROC Determination

The following flowchart maps the algorithmic decision process a student (or a DSP system) follows when determining the ROC for an arbitrary Z-transform:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given x of n] --> B{Is x of n finite duration?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Does it span n greater than or equal 0 only?}
    B -- No --> D{Is x of n right sided causal?}
    C -- Yes --> E[ROC: z greater than 0 excluding infinity]
    C -- No --> F{Does it span n less than or equal 0 only?}
    F -- Yes --> G[ROC: z less than infinity excluding origin]
    F -- No --> H[ROC: Entire z plane]
    D -- Yes --> I[ROC: vert z vert greater than R max]
    D -- No --> J{Is x of n left sided anti causal?}
    J -- Yes --> K[ROC: vert z vert less than R min]
    J -- No --> L[ROC: Ring R1 less than vert z vert less than R2]
    I --> M[Plot poles and shade ROC region]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    E --> M
    G --> M
    H --> M
    M --> N{Does ROC include unit circle?}
    N -- Yes --> O[System is BIBO STABLE]
    N -- No --> P[System is UNSTABLE]
```

### 4.2 Pole-Zero Diagram with ROC Regions for $X(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.7}$

The following block-level diagram illustrates how the same pole location $z = 0.7$ generates three different ROCs depending on the signal's time-domain nature:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Causal Sequence: a^n u of n"]
        P1[Pole at z = 0.7] --> R1[ROC: exterior region vert z vert greater than 0.7]
        R1 --> STAB1[Includes unit circle: STABLE]
    end
    subgraph S2["Anti causal Sequence: minus a^n u of minus n minus 1"]
        P2[Pole at z = 0.7] --> R2[ROC: interior region vert z vert less than 0.7]
        R2 --> UNST[Excludes unit circle: UNSTABLE]
    end
    subgraph S3["Two sided Sequence"]
        P3A[Pole at z = 0.5] --> R3[ROC: ring 0.5 less than vert z vert less than 0.7]
        P3B[Pole at z = 0.7] --> R3
        R3 --> STAB2[Includes unit circle: STABLE]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of ROC Analysis in DSP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Discrete Time Signal x of n] --> ZTRANS[Apply Bilateral Z Transform]
    ZTRANS --> RATIONAL[Rational Function X of z = P of z over Q of z]
    RATIONAL --> POLEFIND[Find Poles: Roots of Q of z]
    RATIONAL --> ZEROFIND[Find Zeros: Roots of P of z]
    POLEFIND --> ROCCALC[Determine ROC from Signal Nature]
    ZEROFIND --> STABCHECK{Check if Unit Circle is in ROC}
    ROCCALC --> STABCHECK
    STABCHECK -- Yes --> OUTPUT1[System is STABLE: BIBO criterion satisfied]
    STABCHECK -- No --> OUTPUT2[System is UNSTABLE: divergent impulse response]
    OUTPUT1 --> INVERSE[Inverse Z Transform: Unique and Causal Stable]
    OUTPUT2 --> WARN[Engineer must redesign system]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** The architecture mirrors a real DSP engineering workflow — given any time-domain sequence, the Z-transform and pole-zero analysis pipeline automatically produces the ROC, which is then cross-checked against the unit-circle stability criterion. This is the same conceptual pipeline used in MATLAB's `zplane()` and `isstable()` functions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Region of Convergence (ROC) of a Z-transform. Why is it necessary to specify the ROC along with the Z-transform expression?**

**Model Answer:**

The Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is the set of all complex values of $z$ for which the Z-transform summation $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ converges to a finite value.

**Necessity:** The ROC must be specified because two different time-domain signals can yield the **algebraically identical** Z-transform expression but correspond to **different regions of convergence**. For instance, both $a^n u[n]$ and $-a^n u[-n-1]$ give $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, but with ROCs $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ and $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ respectively. Hence, without the ROC, the inverse Z-transform is **not unique**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for necessity with example)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the conditions for stability of a causal LTI discrete-time system in terms of ROC.**

**Model Answer:**

A causal LTI discrete-time system is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) if and only if the Region of Convergence of its transfer function $H(z)$ **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

For a **causal** system, the ROC is the **exterior of the outermost pole** $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$. Therefore, stability additionally requires that the outermost pole lie **strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $R_{\max} < 1$. Equivalently, all poles must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for stability.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for ROC condition, 1.5 marks for pole-location condition)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the Z-transform of the causal sequence $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ and determine its ROC. Also, state the conditions for the convergence of the Z-transform in general. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] - 2^n u[-n-1]$, find the Z-transform, draw the pole-zero plot, and determine the ROC. Check whether the system is stable. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-transform definition**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} a^n u[n] z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**Step 2 — Convergence condition**

The geometric series converges if $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. This is the **ROC**.

**Step 3 — Closed form**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**General convergence conditions:**
1. Absolute summability: $\sum \vert x[n] z^{-n} \vert < \infty$
2. For causal sequences: $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$ (where $R_{\max}$ is the magnitude of the outermost pole)
3. The ROC must exclude all poles of $X(z)$.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Z-transform of each term**

For the first term $(0.5)^n u[n]$, the Z-transform is $\dfrac{z}{z - 0.5}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

For the second term $2^n u[-n-1]$, using the standard anti-causal form:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{2^n u[-n-1]\} = -\frac{z}{z - 2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < 2$$

So:

$$-2^n u[-n-1] \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \frac{z}{z - 2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert < 2$$

**Step 2 — Combine**

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{z - 0.5} + \frac{z}{z - 2}$$

Common denominator:

$$X(z) = \frac{z(z-2) + z(z-0.5)}{(z-0.5)(z-2)} = \frac{2z^2 - 2.5z}{(z-0.5)(z-2)}$$

**Step 3 — Pole-zero plot**

- Poles at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 2$
- Zero at $z = 0$ (and $z = 1.25$ from numerator: $2z^2 - 2.5z = 0 \Rightarrow z(2z - 2.5) = 0$)

**Step 4 — ROC**

The first term (causal) requires $\vert z \vert > 0.5$; the second term (anti-causal) requires $\vert z \vert < 2$. The intersection gives:

$$\boxed{\text{ROC: } 0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2}$$

**Step 5 — Stability check**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ satisfies $0.5 < 1 < 2$, so the unit circle is **inside** the ROC. The system is **STABLE**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** List any **seven properties** of the Region of Convergence (ROC) of a Z-transform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Z-transform of a causal LTI system is given by $H(z) = \dfrac{z^2 - 0.5z}{z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18}$. Find the poles, determine the ROC, and comment on stability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — Seven Properties of ROC:**

1. The ROC is a **ring or disk** in the z-plane of the form $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$, possibly extending to $0$ or $\infty$.
2. The ROC **does not contain any pole** of $X(z)$.
3. The ROC is a **connected region** (cannot be the union of disjoint regions).
4. For a **finite-duration** sequence, the ROC is the **entire z-plane** except possibly $z = 0$ and $z = \infty$.
5. For a **right-sided (causal)** sequence, the ROC is the **exterior of the outermost pole**: $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$.
6. For a **left-sided (anti-causal)** sequence, the ROC is the **interior of the innermost pole**: $\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$.
7. For a **two-sided** sequence, the ROC is an **annular ring** bounded by two poles: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$.
8. A system is **stable** if and only if the **unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ lies within the ROC.
9. The ROC cannot include the value $z = 0$ for right-sided sequences, nor $z = \infty$ for left-sided sequences.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Factor denominator to find poles**

$$z^2 - 0.9z + 0.18 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.72}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.09}}{2} = \frac{0.9 \pm 0.3}{2}$$

So poles are:

$$z_1 = \frac{0.9 - 0.3}{2} = 0.3, \qquad z_2 = \frac{0.9 + 0.3}{2} = 0.6$$

**Step 2 — Factor the system function**

$$H(z) = \frac{z(z - 0.5)}{(z - 0.3)(z - 0.6)}$$

Zeros at $z = 0$ and $z = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Determine ROC for a causal system**

Since the system is **causal**, the ROC extends outward from the outermost pole:

$$\text{Outermost pole magnitude} = \max(\vert 0.3 \vert, \vert 0.6 \vert) = 0.6$$

$$\boxed{\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.6}$$

**Step 4 — Stability check**

The unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ satisfies $1 > 0.6$, so the unit circle lies within the ROC. The system is **STABLE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify ROC explicitly** in the final answer — many students write only the transfer function and skip the ROC. The ROC carries **1–2 marks** independently.
> 2. **Confusing "causal + stable" conditions**: Causality alone does **NOT** imply stability. A causal system is stable **only if** all poles lie inside the unit circle. Always state **both** ROC and pole-magnitude conditions.
> 3. **Sign error in anti-causal Z-transform**: For $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$, the negative sign in front of the summation is **frequently dropped** by students, leading to an incorrect ROC of $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ instead of $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$. This single sign error can cascade into a 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Including the pole inside the ROC**: The boundary circle $\vert z \vert = R$ is **excluded** from the ROC. Writing $\vert z \vert \ge R$ is a strict error.
> 5. **Two-sided sequence — failing to take intersection**: For sequences combining causal and anti-causal parts, the ROC is the **intersection** of the individual ROCs, not the union. An empty intersection means the Z-transform does not exist.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ROC Definition:** The set of complex $z$-values for which the Z-transform sum converges to a finite value.
- **ROC is Mandatory:** Two different sequences can share the same $X(z)$ algebraically; only the ROC distinguishes them.
- **Pole-Exclusion:** ROC never includes any pole of $X(z)$.
- **Causal ROC:** Exterior of the outermost pole, $\vert z \vert > R_{\max}$.
- **Anti-causal ROC:** Interior of the innermost pole, $\vert z \vert < R_{\min}$.
- **Two-sided ROC:** Annular ring $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ (intersection of causal and anti-causal ROCs).
- **Finite-Duration ROC:** Entire z-plane (with at most $z=0$ and $z=\infty$ excluded).
- **Stability Criterion:** Unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ must lie inside the ROC.
- **Causal-Stable Theorem:** A causal system is stable **iff** all poles satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$.
- **ROC is Connected:** The ROC is always a single connected region in the z-plane — never a disjoint union of patches.
- **Engineering Applications:** Digital filter design, control system stability, audio processing, biomedical signal analysis, and communications equalization all depend fundamentally on ROC analysis.
- **Standard Trick:** For Z-transform pairs, always present the answer in the canonical form $\dfrac{N(z)}{D(z)}$ together with the explicit ROC inequality — this is the KTU-expected answer format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation of Laplace Transform to Fourier Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relation of Laplace Transform to Fourier Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Laplace Transform** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is defined as a one-sided (unilateral) integral of the form:

$$X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

where the complex variable is $s = \sigma + j\omega$, with $\sigma$ being the **real part** (damping factor) and $\omega$ being the **imaginary part** (radial frequency in rad/s).

The **Fourier Transform** of the same signal is defined as:

$$X(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

The **Relation of Laplace to Fourier Transform** is the central unifying idea in transform-domain analysis: the Fourier Transform can be viewed as a **special case** of the Laplace Transform when the real part of $s$ is identically zero (i.e., $\sigma = 0$), and the signal satisfies conditions of absolute integrability on the $j\omega$ axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **unilateral** Laplace Transform (used for causal systems) and the **bilateral** Laplace Transform (used for general two-sided signals). The relation to Fourier Transform holds most naturally for the **bilateral** Laplace form.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **listening to a music track** through two different audio processing systems:

- The **Fourier Transform** is like a *prism* that splits a sound wave into its pure frequency colors (bass, mid, treble). It assumes the music has been playing **forever** in the past and will play **forever** in the future. It works only for "stable", "well-behaved" music.

- The **Laplace Transform** is like a *smart sound engineer* who not only splits the music into frequencies but also considers whether each note is **growing louder, fading away, or exploding** in amplitude over time. The engineer uses a special dial (the $\sigma$ dial) to control this.

> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **radio receiver**:
> - $\sigma$ tells you *how sharply* the radio tunes in to a station (damping).
> - $\omega$ tells you *which station* you are tuned to (frequency).
> - Setting $\sigma = 0$ means perfect, lossless tuning — this is exactly the Fourier Transform.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Damping factor** $\sigma$ : measured in **Neper per second (Np/s)**.
- **Angular frequency** $\omega$ : measured in **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **The $s$-plane** is a 2D complex plane spanned by $\sigma$ (horizontal axis) and $j\omega$ (vertical axis).
- **The $j\omega$ axis** is a 1D subset of the $s$-plane where $\sigma = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition for Board Exam:** "The Fourier Transform of a signal $x(t)$ equals the Laplace Transform of $x(t)$ evaluated on the imaginary axis of the $s$-plane, provided the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Laplace Transform **includes** the $j\omega$ axis."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Relationship — Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation of the relationship rests on the substitution principle in complex analysis. Let us break it down:

**Step 1 — Start with the Bilateral Laplace Transform:**

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt \quad \text{where } s = \sigma + j\omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the complex variable $s$ explicitly:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's rule to separate the exponential:**

$$e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} = e^{-\sigma t}\cdot e^{-j\omega t}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the structure:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \underbrace{[x(t)\, e^{-\sigma t}]}_{\text{damped signal}}\cdot e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:** This is precisely the **Fourier Transform of the damped signal** $x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$.

**Step 6 — Specialize to the boundary case:** If we let $\sigma \to 0$, then $e^{-\sigma t} \to 1$, and we recover the **standard Fourier Transform**:

$$\lim_{\sigma \to 0}\, X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = X(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\}$$

### 2.2 Conditions for Existence of the Relationship

The Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ exists (in the classical sense) **if and only if**:

1. The signal $x(t)$ is **absolutely integrable**: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t)\vert\, dt < \infty$.
2. The signal has a **finite number of discontinuities** in any finite interval (Dirichlet conditions).
3. The signal contains **no periodic components** of infinite energy (e.g., $e^{j\omega_0 t}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Some signals have a Laplace Transform but **not** a Fourier Transform (e.g., $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$), while some signals have a Fourier Transform but **not** a bilateral Laplace Transform with a useful ROC.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Transform** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Variable of Interest** | **Region of Validity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unilateral Laplace | $\int_{0}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st}\, dt$ | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | Right half-plane $\sigma > \sigma_0$ |
| Bilateral Laplace | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st}\, dt$ | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | Strip $\sigma_1 < \text{Re}(s) < \sigma_2$ |
| Fourier Transform | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ | $\omega \in \mathbb{R}$ | Whole real line (if absolutely integrable) |
| Relation | $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega}$ | $j\omega$ axis in $s$-plane | Requires $j\omega \in$ ROC |
| Damping identity | $e^{-\sigma t} x(t) \leftrightarrow X(\sigma + j\omega)$ | Real shift in $s$ | Multiplies signal by $e^{-\sigma t}$ |
| Inverse relation | $x(t) e^{-\sigma t} \leftrightarrow X(\sigma + j\omega)$ | Complex evaluation | If ROC includes $s = j\omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
