> **Engineering Utility:** In control systems, the Laplace Transform handles **transient (unstable/growing)** signals, while the Fourier Transform analyzes the **steady-state (frequency-domain) behaviour** of stable systems. The relation allows engineers to switch between time-domain stability analysis and frequency-domain filter design seamlessly.

### 2.4 Why This Relationship Matters in Engineering

- **Control Systems:** Stability is determined by the ROC. If the ROC **includes** the $j\omega$ axis, the system is BIBO stable and the Fourier Transform exists.
- **Signal Processing:** The Fourier Transform is the Laplace Transform evaluated on the boundary of the ROC (a contour integral reduction).
- **Filter Design:** Filters are designed in the $s$-plane and analysed in the frequency ($\omega$) domain.
- **Communications:** Modulation theory uses the $j\omega$ shift property of the Fourier Transform, which is the imaginary component of the $s$-shift property of the Laplace Transform.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Unilateral Laplace to Fourier for Causal Signals

**Problem:** Show that for a causal, absolutely integrable signal $x(t)$, the Fourier Transform can be obtained from the unilateral Laplace Transform by setting $s = j\omega$.

**Step 1 — Write the Unilateral Laplace Transform:**

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

Since the signal is causal, $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, so the bilateral integral collapses to the unilateral one. This is the *convergence trick* used by engineers.

**Step 2 — Substitute the complex variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} x(t)\, e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Split the exponential using the law of exponents:**

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[x(t)\, e^{-\sigma t}\right] e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

> *Reasoning:* $e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} = e^{-\sigma t} \cdot e^{-j\omega t}$ (property of exponents).

**Step 4 — Identify the inner expression as a damped Fourier Transform:**

The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} g(t) e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$ is the **Fourier Transform of $g(t)$**.

Thus, if we define $g(t) = x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$, then:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\, e^{-\sigma t}\}$$

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $\sigma \to 0$:**

$$\lim_{\sigma \to 0^+}\, X(\sigma + j\omega) = \lim_{\sigma \to 0^+}\, \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\, e^{-\sigma t}\}$$

Since $e^{-\sigma t} \to e^{0} = 1$ for all $t \geq 0$:

$$\lim_{\sigma \to 0^+}\, X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = X(j\omega)$$

**Step 6 — Conclude the relation:**

$$\boxed{\,X(j\omega) = X(s)\,\Big\vert_{s=j\omega}\,}$$

This holds **if and only if** the ROC of $X(s)$ includes the $j\omega$ axis. Q.E.D.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Find the Laplace Transform of $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ (where $a > 0$), and then derive its Fourier Transform.

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform definition:**

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at}\, e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$X(s) = \left[\frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)}\right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 3 — Apply limits (requires $\text{Re}(s+a) > 0$):**

$$X(s) = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{s+a}\right) = \frac{1}{s+a}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the ROC:**

The ROC is the set of $s$ such that $\text{Re}(s) > -a$, which is the **right half-plane** to the right of the pole at $s = -a$.

**Step 5 — Check if the $j\omega$ axis lies in the ROC:**

For $j\omega$ to be in the ROC, we need $\text{Re}(j\omega) = 0 > -a$, which is true for any $a > 0$. So the Fourier Transform exists.

**Step 6 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ in the expression:**

$$X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s=j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega + a}$$

**Step 7 — Rationalize to obtain the standard form:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{1}{a + j\omega} \cdot \frac{a - j\omega}{a - j\omega} = \frac{a}{a^2 + \omega^2} - j\frac{\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 8 — Identify real and imaginary parts:**

- **Magnitude response:** $\vert X(\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a^2 + \omega^2}}$
- **Phase response:** $\angle X(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is the frequency response of a first-order **low-pass filter** with a **3-dB cutoff frequency** at $\omega_c = a$ rad/s.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Signals and Their Transform Relationship

| **Signal $x(t)$** | **Laplace $X(s)$** | **ROC** | **Fourier Exists?** | **Fourier $X(\omega)$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | Entire $s$-plane | Yes | $1$ |
| $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | Yes (as distribution) | $\pi\delta(\omega) + \dfrac{1}{j\omega}$ |
| $e^{-at} u(t),\ a>0$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | Yes | $\dfrac{1}{a+j\omega}$ |
| $e^{at} u(t),\ a>0$ | $\dfrac{1}{s-a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > a$ | No (unstable) | Does not exist classically |
| $\cos(\omega_0 t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ | Yes (as distribution) | $\pi[\delta(\omega-\omega_0) + \delta(\omega+\omega_0)]$ |
| $e^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t)u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_0^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | Yes | $\dfrac{a+j\omega}{(a+j\omega)^2 + \omega_0^2}$ |
| $t\, e^{-at} u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ | Yes | $\dfrac{1}{(a+j\omega)^2}$ |

### 3.4 Key Mathematical Insight: ROC Inclusion Test

The existence of the Fourier Transform from the Laplace Transform is governed by this **theorem**:

> **Theorem:** The Fourier Transform of $x(t)$ exists (in the classical sense) if and only if the **imaginary axis** $s = j\omega$ is contained within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of the bilateral Laplace Transform of $x(t)$.

**Proof Sketch:**

If $j\omega \in$ ROC, then the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t}\, dt$ converges at $\sigma = 0$, which is precisely the definition of the Fourier Transform. Conversely, if the Fourier Transform exists, the integral converges at $\sigma = 0$, so the ROC must extend to include $s = j\omega$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Time-Domain to s-Plane to Frequency

The relationship between the two transforms is best understood through a hierarchical flow diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Time Signal x(t)] --> B{Signal Class?}
    B -- Causal x t >= 0 --> C[Unilateral Laplace Transform]
    B -- Non-causal Two-sided --> D[Bilateral Laplace Transform]
    C --> E[Laplace Domain X of s]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Does ROC include jω axis?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Fourier Transform Exists]
    F -- No --> H[Fourier Transform Does Not Exist]
    G --> I[Evaluate X of s at s = jω]
    I --> J[Fourier Domain X of ω]
    J --> K[Frequency Response Analysis]
    H --> L[Only Laplace Domain Analysis Possible]
    E --> M[Determine Stability from ROC]
    M --> N{Is ROC in Left Half Plane?}
    N -- Yes --> O[BIBO Stable System]
    N -- No --> P[Unstable System]
```

### 4.2 s-Plane Visualization: ROC and the jω Axis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph splane[Complex s-Plane]
        A[jω axis vertical] -->|s = jω| B[Imaginary Axis s = jω]
        C[Real axis σ] -->|s = σ + j0| D[Real Axis]
        B --> E[ROC must include this axis for Fourier]
        D --> F[Poles of X of s marked as X]
        F --> G[Stable ROC: right of leftmost pole]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Transform Domain Conversion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x(t)] --> B[Laplace Transform X(s)]
    B --> C{s = jω substitution}
    C --> D[Fourier Transform X(ω)]
    B --> E[Inverse Laplace x(t)]
    D --> F[Inverse Fourier x(t)]
    E --> G[Same time-domain result]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Verify consistency]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Table: Transform Domain Properties

| **Property** | **Laplace Transform** | **Fourier Transform** |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Complex $s = \sigma + j\omega$ | Real $\omega$ (1D) |
| Convergence criterion | Exponential damping $e^{-\sigma t}$ | Absolute integrability |
| Handles unstable systems | Yes (ROC analysis) | No (requires absolute integrability) |
| Handles periodic signals | Indirectly (via impulses) | Yes (line spectra) |
| Inverse transform | Bromwich integral | Standard inverse formula |
| Primary use | Stability and transient analysis | Frequency spectrum and filter design |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**  
*State the conditions under which the Fourier Transform can be obtained from the Laplace Transform of a signal.*

**Model Answer:**

The Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ of a signal $x(t)$ can be obtained from its bilateral Laplace Transform $X(s)$ provided:

1. The signal $x(t)$ satisfies the **Dirichlet conditions** (finite discontinuities, piecewise smoothness, absolute integrability).
2. The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(s)$ **includes the $j\omega$ axis** (i.e., $s = j\omega$ lies within the ROC).
3. Under these conditions, the substitution $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s = j\omega}$ is valid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for stating the substitution rule, 1 mark for ROC condition, 1 mark for Dirichlet/absolute integrability condition.

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**  
*Why does the Fourier Transform of $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$ not exist, even though its Laplace Transform does?*

**Model Answer:**

The unilateral Laplace Transform of $x(t) = e^{2t} u(t)$ is:

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{2t} e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s-2)t}\, dt = \frac{1}{s-2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > 2$$

The ROC is the half-plane **strictly to the right of $\sigma = 2$**. The $j\omega$ axis (where $\sigma = 0$) **does not** lie in this ROC. Since the signal is **growing exponentially**, the integral $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert e^{2t} \vert\, dt$ diverges, violating the absolute integrability requirement of the Fourier Transform. Hence, $X(\omega)$ does not exist classically, although $X(s)$ does.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for computing $X(s)$, 1 mark for stating ROC, 1 mark for concluding non-existence based on $j\omega$ exclusion.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical relationship between the Laplace Transform and the Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. Clearly state the conditions required for this relationship to hold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the causal signal $x(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$, find its Laplace Transform, identify the ROC, and hence determine its Fourier Transform. Also plot the magnitude spectrum $\vert X(\omega)\vert$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the bilateral Laplace Transform:

$$X(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-st}\, dt \quad \text{where } s = \sigma + j\omega$$

Substituting the complex variable:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t}\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} [x(t) e^{-\sigma t}]\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

This is the **Fourier Transform of the exponentially damped signal** $x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$:

$$X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) e^{-\sigma t}\}$$

Taking the limit as $\sigma \to 0$:

$$\lim_{\sigma \to 0}\, X(\sigma + j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = X(j\omega)$$

Thus, the **relation** is:

$$\boxed{X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s = j\omega}}$$

**Conditions for validity:**

1. The signal $x(t)$ must be **absolutely integrable**: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$.
2. The **$j\omega$ axis must lie in the ROC** of $X(s)$.
3. The signal must satisfy **Dirichlet conditions** (finite discontinuities, piecewise smoothness).

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

Applying the Laplace Transform definition:

$$X(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t} e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+3)t}\, dt$$

Evaluating the integral (with $\text{Re}(s) > -3$):

$$X(s) = \frac{1}{s+3}, \quad \text{ROC: } \text{Re}(s) > -3$$

**Verification of Fourier existence:** The $j\omega$ axis ($\sigma = 0$) lies in the ROC since $0 > -3$. ✓

**Fourier Transform by substitution:**

$$X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s = j\omega} = \frac{1}{j\omega + 3} = \frac{1}{3 + j\omega}$$

**Magnitude spectrum:**

$$\vert X(\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \omega^2}}$$

| **$\omega$ (rad/s)** | $\vert X(\omega)\vert$ |
|---|---|
| $0$ | $1/3 \approx 0.333$ |
| $3$ (cutoff) | $1/\sqrt{18} \approx 0.236$ |
| $\infty$ | $0$ |

**Plot description:** The magnitude spectrum is a **monotonically decreasing** function of $\vert\omega\vert$, exhibiting a **low-pass filter** characteristic with a DC gain of $1/3$ and a **3-dB cutoff frequency** at $\omega = 3$ rad/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students forget to **state the ROC explicitly** and then **verify that the $j\omega$ axis lies within it**. Failing to verify this step costs **2 to 3 marks** even when the rest of the solution is correct. Always include the ROC check before substituting $s = j\omega$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the location of the $j\omega$ axis in the $s$-plane and describe its significance in the relationship between the Laplace and Fourier Transforms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ has the Laplace Transform $X(s) = \dfrac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$ with ROC: $\text{Re}(s) > -2$. Determine whether its Fourier Transform exists. If yes, compute $X(\omega)$ and separate it into real and imaginary parts. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 marks):**

The $s$-plane is a two-dimensional complex plane with the **real axis** $\sigma$ (horizontal) and the **imaginary axis** $j\omega$ (vertical). The $j\omega$ axis corresponds to all points of the form $s = 0 + j\omega = j\omega$, i.e., the locus where the real part $\sigma = 0$.

**Significance:** The $j\omega$ axis is the **boundary of the Laplace domain** that corresponds to the **Fourier domain**. Specifically:

- For $\sigma > 0$ (right half-plane), the signal is **damped** and decays.
- For $\sigma < 0$ (left half-plane), the signal is **growing** and unstable.
- For $\sigma = 0$ (**on the $j\omega$ axis**), there is **no damping**, and the transform reduces to the **Fourier Transform**.

**Diagram description:**

The $s$-plane is divided into:
- **Right half-plane (RHP):** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ — damped signals.
- **Left half-plane (LHP):** $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ — growing signals.
- **$j\omega$ axis:** $\text{Re}(s) = 0$ — lossless, oscillatory behaviour; **Fourier Transform evaluation line**.

The **ROC** of the Laplace Transform is a vertical strip (or half-plane) in the $s$-plane. For the Fourier Transform to exist, this strip must **include the $j\omega$ axis**. If the ROC is entirely in the LHP (unstable system), the Fourier Transform does not exist.

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

Given:

$$X(s) = \frac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 5s + 6} = \frac{2s + 5}{(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**Step 1 — Factor and identify poles:**

The poles are at $s = -2$ and $s = -3$. The ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > -2$.

**Step 2 — Check if $j\omega$ axis is in ROC:**

We need $\text{Re}(j\omega) = 0 > -2$. Since $0 > -2$ is true, the $j\omega$ axis **is** in the ROC. **Therefore, the Fourier Transform exists.** ✓

**Step 3 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{2(j\omega) + 5}{(j\omega + 2)(j\omega + 3)} = \frac{5 + j2\omega}{(2 + j\omega)(3 + j\omega)}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator:**

$$(2 + j\omega)(3 + j\omega) = 6 + j2\omega + j3\omega + j^2\omega^2 = (6 - \omega^2) + j5\omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute the full expression:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{(5 + j2\omega)[(6 - \omega^2) - j5\omega]}{(6 - \omega^2)^2 + 25\omega^2}$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator (real × complex conjugate pattern):**

$$(5 + j2\omega)[(6 - \omega^2) - j5\omega] = 5(6 - \omega^2) - j25\omega + j2\omega(6 - \omega^2) - j^2 10\omega^2$$

$$= [5(6 - \omega^2) + 10\omega^2] + j[2\omega(6 - \omega^2) - 25\omega]$$

$$= [30 - 5\omega^2 + 10\omega^2] + j[12\omega - 2\omega^3 - 25\omega]$$

$$= [30 + 5\omega^2] + j[-13\omega - 2\omega^3]$$

**Step 7 — Final form:**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{30 + 5\omega^2}{(6 - \omega^2)^2 + 25\omega^2} - j\frac{13\omega + 2\omega^3}{(6 - \omega^2)^2 + 25\omega^2}$$

- **Real part:** $\text{Re}\{X(\omega)\} = \dfrac{30 + 5\omega^2}{(6 - \omega^2)^2 + 25\omega^2}$
- **Imaginary part:** $\text{Im}\{X(\omega)\} = -\dfrac{13\omega + 2\omega^3}{(6 - \omega^2)^2 + 25\omega^2}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):** Two common mistakes cost marks: (1) Students often **skip the ROC verification step**, which is worth 1.5 marks in the official KTU key. (2) When separating real and imaginary parts, errors in **algebraic sign** (e.g., dropping the negative sign in the imaginary part) are flagged. Always re-derive the imaginary part carefully using $(a+jb)(c-jd) = (ac+bd) + j(bc - ad)$.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

- **Core Formula:** $X(\omega) = X(s)\vert_{s = j\omega}$ — this is the **single most important equation** for this topic.
- **ROC is King:** The Region of Convergence decides everything. **Fourier exists if and only if $j\omega$ axis is in the ROC.**
- **Laplacian = Fourier + Damping:** $X(\sigma + j\omega)$ is the Fourier Transform of $x(t) e^{-\sigma t}$. Setting $\sigma = 0$ recovers the standard Fourier Transform.
- **Stability Connection:** A causal LTI system is **BIBO stable** $\iff$ all poles lie in the **left half-plane** $\iff$ ROC includes $j\omega$ $\iff$ Fourier Transform exists.
- **Signals WITHOUT Fourier Transform (classical):** $e^{at} u(t)$ for $a > 0$, $t^n u(t)$, $e^{t^2} u(t)$ — all **growing** signals.
- **Signals WITH Fourier Transform:** $e^{-at} u(t)$ for $a > 0$, $e^{-a\vert t\vert}$, $u(t) e^{-at}$ — all **decaying** signals.
- **Substitution Trick:** To find the Fourier Transform from $X(s)$: (1) Write $X(s)$, (2) Verify ROC includes $j\omega$, (3) Replace $s$ with $j\omega$, (4) Simplify.
- **Existence of $X(\omega)$ vs. $X(s)$:** Fourier is a *boundary* of Laplace. Many signals have one but not the other.
- **Common KTU trap:** Forgetting to mention the ROC. Always state **both** $X(s)$ and its ROC explicitly.
- **Key signals to memorize for board exams:** $u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{s}$ (Re(s)>0); $e^{-at}u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{s+a}$ (Re(s)>-a); $\cos(\omega_0 t)u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{s}{s^2+\omega_0^2}$ (Re(s)>0).
- **Formula derivate:** Use $e^{-(\sigma + j\omega)t} = e^{-\sigma t} \cdot e^{-j\omega t}$ as the central algebraic identity in all derivations.
- **Fourier as a special case:** The Fourier Transform is the Laplace Transform evaluated **on the imaginary axis of the s-plane** — a one-line answer that often earns 1–2 marks in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of Continuous Time Signal to Discrete Time Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Continuous Time Signal to Discrete Time Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Conversion of a Continuous-Time (CT) Signal to a Discrete-Time (DT) Signal** is the process of obtaining a sequence of numerical values $x[n]$ from an analog waveform $x_c(t)$. In the KTU 2024 framework, this transformation is fundamentally realized through **uniform sampling**, a mathematical operation in which the continuous-time signal is multiplied by a periodic **Dirac Comb** (impulse train) of period $T$, thereby extracting the signal's instantaneous amplitude at evenly spaced time instants $t = nT$.

The end-to-end bridge between the analog physical world and the digital computational domain is governed by the **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Theorem)**, which states:

> A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ provided that the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and the corresponding maximum sampling interval is $T \leq \frac{1}{2f_m}$ seconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** Students must master (i) the mathematical model of the sampling process using impulse trains, (ii) the spectral relationship $X_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j\!\left(\Omega - \frac{2\pi k}{T}\right)\right)$, and (iii) the anti-aliasing pre-filter and ideal low-pass reconstruction (D/C) filter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stroboscopic Camera"

Imagine filming a spinning ceiling fan with a **stroboscopic camera** (a camera that opens its shutter only at fixed time intervals $T$):

- The fan is the **continuous-time signal** — it exists smoothly at *every* instant.
- The strobe is the **sampler** — it captures the fan's position only at discrete moments.
- The captured frames are the **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$.

If the strobe flashes **very fast** (high $f_s$), every blade position is recorded faithfully — we can reconstruct the smooth motion. If the strobe flashes **too slowly** (low $f_s$, below the Nyquist rate), the blades appear to rotate *backwards* or *stand still* — this visual artifact is the analog-domain equivalent of **aliasing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Every digital audio system (MP3, smartphones), medical imaging device (MRI, CT scanner), radar, and IoT sensor uses this exact conversion. The choice of $f_s$ determines whether your audio sounds "crisp" or "muffled/garbled."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Audio CD Sampling Rate | **44.1 kHz** | Captures human hearing up to 22.05 kHz |
| Speech (Telephony) | **8 kHz** | Captures voice up to 4 kHz |
| Nyquist Interval $T$ | **seconds (s)** | $T = 1/f_s$ |
| Antialiasing Filter | **Low-pass, cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$** | Removes frequencies above $f_m$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Operational Pipeline

The conversion from continuous-time to discrete-time (and back) is **never a single-step operation** — it is a structured pipeline:

**Stage 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filtering (Pre-Filter)**
- A **practical continuous-time signal** is *never* perfectly bandlimited; it always contains noise and high-frequency components above $f_m$.
- An analog **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c$ (typically $f_c = f_s/2$) is applied *before* sampling to band-limit the signal.
- Without this filter, high-frequency noise would **fold back** into the baseband upon sampling, causing **irreversible aliasing distortion**.

**Stage 2 — Sampling (C-to-D Conversion)**
- The filtered signal $x_c(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)$.
- The output is the **sampled signal** $\hat{x}_c(t) = x_c(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)$.
- The discrete sequence is then extracted as $x[n] = x_c(nT)$.

**Stage 3 — Reconstruction (D-to-C Conversion)**
- The discrete samples $x[n]$ are passed through an **ideal low-pass filter** (reconstruction filter) of gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_s/2$.
- The output is the **interpolated continuous-time signal** $x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why an impulse train?** Because $\delta(t - nT)$ extracts the value of $x_c(t)$ exactly at $t = nT$ and is mathematically tractable via the sifting property of impulses.
- **Why multiply by a low-pass filter after sampling?** Because multiplication in the time domain corresponds to **convolution in the frequency domain** with a sinc kernel — this is the mechanism of perfect bandlimited interpolation.
- **Why $f_s \geq 2f_m$?** Because if the spectral replicas (centered at $k \cdot 2\pi/T$) overlap, their superposition makes the original spectrum unrecoverable.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ | Discrete sample at index $n$ | $T$ in seconds, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 2 | $f_s = \frac{1}{T}$ | Sampling frequency | **Hz** |
| 3 | $\Omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T}$ | Sampling angular frequency | **rad/s** |
| 4 | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Nyquist Rate | **Hz** |
| 5 | $\Omega_N = 2\Omega_m$ | Nyquist angular frequency | **rad/s** |
| 6 | $\hat{x}_c(t) = \sum_n x_c(nT)\,\delta(t-nT)$ | Ideal sampled signal | Impulse train |
| 7 | $\hat{X}_c(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)$ | Spectrum of sampled signal | Periodic replication |
| 8 | $X_d(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j\frac{\omega-2\pi k}{T}\right)$ | DTFT of discrete sequence | $\omega = \Omega T$ |
| 9 | $x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT}{T}\right)$ | Ideal reconstruction | Whittaker–Shannon formula |
| 10 | $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ | Normalized sinc function | $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$ |
| 11 | Aliasing Condition | Overlap occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$ | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is mandatory |
| 12 | Folded Frequency | $f_{\text{fold}} = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f/f_s) \vert$ | For non-bandlimited inputs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Always remember that $f_s$ and $\Omega_s$ use **different units** (Hz vs rad/s). Mixing them is the #1 source of mark loss in KTU board exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio:** CDs use $f_s = 44.1$ kHz to capture the full 20 Hz–20 kHz human hearing range with a 10% guard band.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG systems sample at 250–1000 Hz using anti-aliasing filters to remove 50/60 Hz powerline noise.
- **Telecommunications:** Software-Defined Radios (SDR) oversample at $f_s \gg 2f_m$ to relax analog filter design.
- **Control Systems:** Sampled-data controllers (PLCs, microcontrollers) sample sensor signals at a rate dictated by the Nyquist criterion relative to the closed-loop bandwidth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum of an Ideally Sampled Signal

Let the continuous-time signal be $x_c(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X_c(j\Omega)$. The sampling impulse train is:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)
$$

Its Fourier series representation is:

$$
p(t) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\Omega_s t}, \quad \text{where } \Omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T}
$$

**Step 1 — Form the sampled signal:**

The sampled signal $\hat{x}_c(t)$ is the product of $x_c(t)$ and the impulse train:

$$
\hat{x}_c(t) = x_c(t) \cdot p(t) = x_c(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sifting property of the impulse:**

Using the property $x_c(t)\,\delta(t - nT) = x_c(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)$:

$$
\hat{x}_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)
$$

**Step 3 — Express the sampled signal using the Fourier series of $p(t)$:**

$$
\hat{x}_c(t) = x_c(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\Omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(t)\, e^{jk\Omega_s t}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides:**

Recall that $\mathcal{F}\{x_c(t)\,e^{jk\Omega_s t}\} = X_c\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)$ (frequency-shift property):

$$
\hat{X}_c(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The spectrum of the sampled signal is a **periodic replication** of the original spectrum $X_c(j\Omega)$, shifted by integer multiples of the sampling frequency $\Omega_s$ and scaled by $\frac{1}{T}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Reconstruction via Ideal Low-Pass Filter

**Step 1 — Condition for perfect recovery:**

If $X_c(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert \geq \Omega_m$ and $\Omega_s \geq 2\Omega_m$, then the spectral replicas do **not** overlap. The baseband replica ($k = 0$) can be isolated by an ideal LPF with gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_c$ where $\Omega_m \leq \Omega_c \leq \Omega_s - \Omega_m$.

**Step 2 — Output of the reconstruction filter:**

Let $H_r(j\Omega)$ be the ideal reconstruction filter:

$$
H_r(j\Omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert \Omega \vert \leq \Omega_c \\ 0, & \vert \Omega \vert > \Omega_c \end{cases}
$$

The reconstructed spectrum is:

$$
X_r(j\Omega) = \hat{X}_c(j\Omega) \cdot H_r(j\Omega) = X_c(j\Omega)
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Fourier Transform gives the time-domain reconstruction:**

The impulse response of the ideal LPF is:

$$
h_r(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi} \int_{-\Omega_c}^{\Omega_c} e^{j\Omega t}\,d\Omega = \frac{\sin(\Omega_c t)}{\pi t} = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_c t}{\pi}\right)
$$

With $\Omega_c = \pi/T$, this simplifies to:

$$
h_r(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T} \cdot \frac{1}{T} \cdot T = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Convolve the sampled signal with $h_r(t)$:**

$$
x_r(t) = \hat{x}_c(t) * h_r(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)\right] * \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)
$$

Distributing the convolution over the impulse train:

$$
x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(nT)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

This is the celebrated **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**.

### 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Board-Style Problem

**Problem:** A continuous-time signal $x_c(t) = 3\cos(100\pi t) + 2\sin(200\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 300$ Hz. Determine whether aliasing occurs. If so, find the aliased frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum frequency:**

Component 1: $f_1 = \frac{100\pi}{2\pi} = 50$ Hz  
Component 2: $f_2 = \frac{200\pi}{2\pi} = 100$ Hz  
Maximum frequency: $f_m = 100$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Compute the Nyquist rate:**

$$
f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the given sampling frequency:**

Given $f_s = 300$ Hz. Since $f_s = 300 > 200 = f_N$, the Nyquist condition is **satisfied** for both components. **No aliasing occurs.**

**Step 4 — (Counter-example for learning):** If instead $f_s = 150$ Hz, then $f_s < 2f_m$ ($150 < 200$), aliasing occurs. The 100 Hz component aliases to:

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - f_s \vert = \vert 100 - 150 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

The discrete-time signal would then be indistinguishable from a 50 Hz sinusoid — a classic case of **frequency folding**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sampling Scenarios

| Scenario | Condition | Result | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Oversampling | $f_s \gg 2f_m$ | Spectral replicas well separated | Relaxed analog filter design |
| Critical Sampling | $f_s = 2f_m$ | Replicas just touch at $f_m$ | Theoretical boundary, fragile |
| Undersampling | $f_s < 2f_m$ | Replicas overlap → Aliasing | Information loss — irreversible |
| Bandpass Sampling | $f_L < f_m < f_H$, $f_s < 2f_H$ | Intentional aliasing of unused bands | Used in SDR, sub-Nyquist sampling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram — A/D and D/A Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["x_c(t)\nContinuous Time Input"] --> nodeB["Anti-Aliasing\nAnalog LPF\ncutoff f_s/2"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Sampler\nC to D\nx[n] = x_c(nT)"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Digital\nSignal\nProcessor\nDSP"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Reconstruction\nIdeal LPF\nGain T cutoff pi/T"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["x_r(t)\nReconstructed CT Output"]
```

### 4.2 Spectral Replication Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Original Spectrum X_c(jOmega)\nBandlimited to Om"] --> nodeB["Sampled Spectrum\n(1/T) Sum X_c(j(Omega - k Omega_s))"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["k = 0 Baseband Replica"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["k = +1 Replica at Omega_s"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["k = -1 Replica at -Omega_s"]
    nodeC --> nodeF{"Condition:\nOmega_s >= 2 Om ?"}
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG["NO OVERLAP\nReconstruction Possible\nvia Ideal LPF"]
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH["OVERLAP / ALIASING\nInformation Lost\nReconstruction Impossible"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Signal Flow During Sampling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Analog Source\nx_c(t)"] --> nodeB["Impulse Train\np(t) = Sum delta(t - nT)"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Multiplier\nx_c(t) times p(t)"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Sampled Signal\nSum x_c(nT) delta(t - nT)"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Extract Weights\nx[n] = x_c(nT)"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Discrete Sequence\nx[n], n in Z"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DTFT\nX_d(exp j omega)\nPeriodic in 2 pi"]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Will Aliasing Occur?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Given Signal x_c(t)"] --> nodeB["Determine\nf_m = max freq"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Given\nf_s = 1 / T"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Is f_s >= 2 f_m ?"}
    nodeD -- "YES" --> nodeE["No Aliasing\nApply Anti-Alias LPF\nSample and Reconstruct"]
    nodeD -- "NO" --> nodeF["ALIASING Occurs\nIncrease f_s\nOR Apply LPF first"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Output:\nx_r(t) approx x_c(t)"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU exams:** Drawing the sampled spectrum (with $k = -1, 0, +1$ replicas) is a guaranteed 4-mark sub-part in the 14-mark Module 3 question. Mastering the block-level flow of sampler + reconstruction filter is essential for the CO3 course outcome.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem. What is the significance of the Nyquist Rate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A bandlimited continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its discrete samples $x[n] = x_c(nT)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The **Nyquist Rate** $f_N = 2f_m$ is the minimum sampling frequency that permits lossless reconstruction; sampling below this rate causes irreversible aliasing distortion. The corresponding maximum sampling interval is $T \leq 1/(2f_m)$ seconds. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is aliasing? How can it be prevented?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous-time signal, when sampled below the Nyquist rate, fold into the baseband and become indistinguishable from legitimate low-frequency components. It is **prevented** by (i) applying a sharp-cutoff **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ before sampling, and (ii) ensuring the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the Fourier Transform of an ideally sampled signal $x_c(t)$ sampled at a rate $f_s = 1/T$ Hz. Clearly state the conditions required for distortionless reconstruction from the samples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic impulse train:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\Omega_s t}
$$

The sampled signal is:

$$
\hat{x}_c(t) = x_c(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_c(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)
$$

Taking the Fourier Transform and using the modulation property:

$$
\hat{X}_c(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)
$$

For **distortionless reconstruction**, two conditions must hold:
1. $x_c(t)$ must be **bandlimited**: $X_c(j\Omega) = 0$ for $\vert \Omega \vert > \Omega_m$.
2. The sampling frequency must satisfy $\Omega_s \geq 2\Omega_m$ (i.e., $f_s \geq 2f_m$) so the spectral replicas do not overlap.

Under these conditions, an ideal LPF of gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_c \in [\Omega_m, \Omega_s - \Omega_m]$ recovers $X_c(j\Omega)$ exactly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time signal $x_c(t) = 5\cos(40\pi t) + 3\cos(80\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 60$ Hz. Determine:
  (i) The Nyquist rate for this signal.
  (ii) Whether the discrete samples contain aliasing.
  (iii) The discrete-time signal $x[n]$ after sampling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Nyquist Rate:**

The signal contains frequencies $f_1 = 20$ Hz and $f_2 = 40$ Hz. The maximum is $f_m = 40$ Hz.

$$
f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Aliasing Check:**

Given $f_s = 60$ Hz, and the Nyquist rate is 80 Hz. Since $f_s = 60 < 80 = f_N$, **aliasing occurs**.

The 40 Hz component folds into:

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 40 - 60 \vert = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

The 20 Hz component remains unaffected (since $20 < 30 = f_s/2$).

**(iii) Discrete-Time Signal:**

Sampling at $T = 1/60$ s, with $\omega_k = 2\pi f_k / f_s$:

For $f_1 = 20$ Hz: $\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \times 20}{60} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ rad/sample.  
For $f_2 = 40$ Hz (aliased to 20 Hz): $\omega_2' = \frac{2\pi \times 20}{60} = \frac{2\pi}{3}$ rad/sample.

Both components now appear at the same discrete frequency, causing interference:

$$
x[n] = 5\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} n\right) + 3\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} n\right) = 8\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{3} n\right)
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the functional block diagram of a complete Discrete-Time Signal Processing (DSP) system that converts a continuous-time signal to discrete form, processes it, and reconstructs it back to continuous form. Clearly state the role of the anti-aliasing filter and the reconstruction filter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A complete DSP system has the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Analog LPF):** A continuous-time low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ placed *before* the sampler. It removes frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent them from folding into the baseband upon sampling. It guarantees that the input to the sampler is bandlimited to $f_s/2$.

2. **C/D Converter (Sampler):** Multiplies the bandlimited signal by a periodic impulse train of period $T$, producing the discrete sequence $x[n] = x_c(nT)$.

3. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Operates on the sequence $x[n]$ to perform filtering, modulation, compression, or any other desired operation, producing output $y[n]$.

4. **D/C Converter (Reconstruction Filter):** An ideal low-pass filter with gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_s/2$ that interpolates $y[n]$ back into a continuous-time signal $y_c(t)$ using the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula.

$$
y_c(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

**Roles:**
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Prevents aliasing by enforcing the bandlimiting assumption.
- **Reconstruction filter:** Removes the spectral replicas generated by sampling, isolating the baseband copy.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** The signal $x_c(t) = \text{sinc}^2(100t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 150$ Hz. Determine:
  (i) The maximum frequency $f_m$ of the signal.
  (ii) Whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied.
  (iii) The expression for the reconstructed signal $x_r(t)$ in terms of $x[n]$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Frequency:**

The Fourier Transform of $\text{sinc}^2(100t)$ is a **triangular** spectrum of base width $\pm 100$ Hz. Therefore:

$$
f_m = 100 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist Check:**

$$
f_N = 2 f_m = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}
$$

Since $f_s = 150 < 200 = f_N$, **the Nyquist criterion is violated**, and aliasing will occur.

**(iii) Reconstruction Expression:**

Even with aliasing, the mathematical formula for reconstruction is:

$$
x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right), \quad T = \frac{1}{150} \text{ s}
$$

$$
x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(150(t - nT)\right)
$$

However, $x_r(t) \neq x_c(t)$ because of aliasing. The output will be a **distorted version** of the original. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{T}$ scaling factor** in the sampled spectrum $\hat{X}_c(j\Omega)$ — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing $f_s$ (Hz) and $\Omega_s$ (rad/s)** without clear unit declaration — examiners deduct 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating "aliasing can be removed by a filter"** after sampling — this is **wrong**; aliasing is irreversible. It must be *prevented* by an anti-aliasing filter *before* sampling.
> 4. **Not drawing the spectral replicas** (at $k = -1, 0, +1$) — KTU awards marks for the visual spectrum diagram in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Forgetting to check the condition $\Omega_s \geq 2\Omega_m$ explicitly** before writing the reconstruction formula.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The conversion chain is: **Anti-Alias LPF → Sampler (C/D) → DSP → Reconstruction LPF (D/C)**.
- The Nyquist Rate is $f_N = 2 f_m$; the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq f_N$.
- The sampled signal's spectrum is a **periodic replication** scaled by $\frac{1}{T}$:

$$
\hat{X}_c(j\Omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X_c\!\left(j(\Omega - k\Omega_s)\right)
$$

- The **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula** reconstructs a bandlimited signal using sinc interpolation:

$$
x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

- **Aliasing is irreversible** — prevention via an analog low-pass pre-filter is mandatory.
- Discrete-time angular frequency $\omega$ is related to continuous-time angular frequency $\Omega$ by $\omega = \Omega T$.
- DTFT of a sampled sequence is **periodic in $2\pi$** with period $\frac{2\pi}{T}$ in the $\Omega$-domain.
- The **anti-aliasing filter** has cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$; the **reconstruction filter** has gain $T$ and cutoff $\Omega_s/2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sampling Theorem for Lowpass Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sampling Theorem for Lowpass Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

The **Sampling Theorem for Lowpass Signals** (also known as the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**) is a foundational result in signal processing that establishes the necessary and sufficient condition for a bandlimited continuous-time signal to be reconstructed exactly from its discrete samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Standard Statement:**
> A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with **maximum frequency component** $W$ Hz (or $\omega_m = 2\pi W$ rad/sec) is uniquely determined by its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2W \quad \text{(samples/second)}$$
> The minimum sampling rate $f_s = 2W$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2W$ is the **Nyquist Frequency**.

The parameter $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ is the **sampling period** (time interval between consecutive samples).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Frame" Intuition

Imagine watching a slow-moving ceiling fan. If you take photographs at a fast-enough rate (say, 30 frames per second), your brain can piece together the smooth motion perfectly. Now, if you slow the camera down to 2 frames per second, the fan blades appear to rotate **backwards** — this illusion is the everyday equivalent of **aliasing**.

In the same way, the Sampling Theorem asks: *"How fast must we 'photograph' an electrical signal to capture all its wiggles and ripples faithfully?"* The answer is: at least **twice as fast as the highest frequency wobble** present in the signal.

- **Maximum signal frequency** $\rightarrow$ **Fan blade speed**
- **Sampling frequency** $\rightarrow$ **Camera frame rate**
- **Aliasing** $\rightarrow$ **Backward motion illusion**
- **Nyquist Rate** $\rightarrow$ **Minimum frame rate to avoid the illusion**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $W$ or $f_m$ | Maximum frequency in signal | **Hz** |
| $\omega_m$ | Maximum angular frequency | **rad/sec** |
| $f_s$ | Sampling frequency | **Hz** (samples/sec) |
| $T_s$ | Sampling period | **seconds** |
| $f_N = 2W$ | Nyquist frequency | **Hz** |
| $T_N = \frac{1}{2W}$ | Nyquist interval | **seconds** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bandlimited Signal:** A signal whose Fourier transform $X(\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > \omega_m$ is called **bandlimited** to $\omega_m$. The Sampling Theorem applies **only** to such signals.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

This topic directly addresses:
- **CO3:** *Analyze continuous-time signals in the frequency domain and apply sampling theory to digital signal processing applications.*
- **Cognitive Level (Revised Bloom's Taxonomy):** Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why and How

The sampling process can be viewed mathematically as the **multiplication** of the continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with a periodic train of unit impulses (an **impulse train sampler**).

**Step-by-step logical flow:**

1. **Modeling the sampler:** The impulse train $p(t)$ is a periodic signal with period $T_s$:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

2. **Sampled signal formation:** The output of the ideal sampler is:
$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

3. **Fourier Series expansion of $p(t)$:** The impulse train is periodic, so it can be represented as:
$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$$

4. **Fourier Transform of the sampled signal:** By the **multiplication property** of the Fourier Transform (multiplication in time $\Leftrightarrow$ convolution in frequency), we obtain the **replication theorem**:
$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$

5. **Reconstruction condition:** As long as the spectral replicas do not overlap, the original spectrum $X(\omega)$ can be recovered by lowpass filtering.

## 2.2 Three Sampling Methods (KTU 2024 Frequently Tested)

| Method | Mechanism | Spectral Effect |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Ideal / Impulse Sampling** | Mathematical idealization using $\delta(t)$ | Pure periodic replication of $X(\omega)$ with amplitude scaling by $1/T_s$ |
| **Natural Sampling** | Signal is multiplied by a **pulse train** (finite-width pulses) | Spectral replicas weighted by sinc envelopes |
| **Flat-Top (Sample-and-Hold) Sampling** | Each sample is held constant for duration $T$ | Spectral replicas weighted by $\mathrm{sinc}(\omega T / 2)$ with **aperture effect** |

## 2.3 Conditions for Perfect Reconstruction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No-Aliasing Condition:** $\omega_s > 2\omega_m$ (strictly greater than for safety margin in practical systems).

When $\omega_s < 2\omega_m$, the shifted spectral copies **overlap**, causing **aliasing distortion** — a phenomenon where high-frequency components masquerade as low-frequency ones.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Expression | Remarks |
|---|--------------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ | Samples per second |
| 2 | **Nyquist Rate** | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate |
| 3 | **Nyquist Interval** | $T_N = \frac{1}{2f_m}$ | Maximum allowable sampling period |
| 4 | **Impulse Train** | $p(t) = \sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$ | Ideal sampling switch |
| 5 | **Sampled Signal (Time)** | $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Output of ideal sampler |
| 6 | **Sampled Spectrum** | $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ | **Replication Theorem** |
| 7 | **Fourier Series of Impulse Train** | $p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k e^{jk\omega_s t}$ | For deriving spectrum |
| 8 | **Reconstruction Filter Gain** | $H(\omega) = T_s$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_m$, $0$ otherwise | Ideal LPF for recovery |
| 9 | **Sinc Interpolation Formula** | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Whittaker-Shannon formula |
| 10 | **Aliasing Frequency Relation** | $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ where $k = \mathrm{round}(f/f_s)$ | Maps $f$ into baseband |
| 11 | **Natural Sampling Spectrum** | $X_s(\omega) = \frac{\tau}{T_s}\sum_k \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k\omega_s \tau}{2}\right) X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ | $\tau$ = pulse width |
| 12 | **Flat-Top Spectrum** | $X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(\omega - k\omega_s) \cdot \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) e^{-j\omega T/2}$ | Aperture effect factor |
| 13 | **Aperture Effect Correction** | Divide reconstructed spectrum by $\mathrm{sinc}(\omega T/2)$ | Equalizer compensates droop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *Nyquist says sample at twice the highest frequency. No exceptions for strictly bandlimited signals.*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio (CDs):** Audio signals are bandlimited to **20 kHz**; CDs sample at **44.1 kHz** (slightly above Nyquist) to include an anti-aliasing filter transition band.
- **Telecommunications:** Speech is bandlimited to **4 kHz** and sampled at **8 kHz** for digital transmission.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** k-space sampling must obey Nyquist to avoid image artifacts.
- **Oscilloscopes and ADCs:** The Sampling Theorem dictates the **minimum ADC clock rate** for a given analog input bandwidth.

## 2.6 Natural vs. Flat-Top — KTU Comparison Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why flat-top is preferred in practice:** Although natural sampling preserves spectral amplitude across replicas, **flat-top sampling** is used in real sample-and-hold circuits because it provides a **constant amplitude per sample**, simplifying the A/D conversion hardware. Its drawback — the **aperture effect** — is corrected by an equalization filter.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spectrum of an Impulse-Sampled Signal

**Goal:** Prove that sampling in the time domain causes periodic replication in the frequency domain.

**Step 1 — Define the impulse train sampler.**
The mathematical model of an ideal switch that closes instantaneously every $T_s$ seconds is the Dirac comb:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

This is a **periodic signal** with period $T_s$ and fundamental frequency $\omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$.

**Step 2 — Express the sampled signal.**
The product of the original signal with the impulse train extracts samples:
$$x_s(t) = x(t)\,p(t) = x(t)\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

Using the **sifting property** of the Dirac delta, $x(t)\,\delta(t - nT_s) = x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$:
$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the Complex Exponential Fourier Series of $p(t)$.**
Since $p(t)$ is periodic with period $T_s$, its Fourier series coefficients are:
$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t)\,e^{-jk\omega_s t}\,dt = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

Therefore:
$$p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k\,e^{jk\omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform of $x_s(t)$.**
$$x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

Using the **frequency-shifting property** of the Fourier Transform, $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X(\omega - k\omega_s)$:
$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s) \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the **Replication Theorem** (or **Poisson Summation Formula** in continuous form).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reconstruction via Lowpass Filtering

**Goal:** Show that $x(t)$ can be recovered from $x_s(t)$ using an ideal lowpass filter when $f_s \geq 2W$.

**Step 1 — Identify one spectral copy.**
Assume $\omega_s > 2\omega_m$. The replicas $X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ are non-overlapping. The $k = 0$ term is the original spectrum, located in the interval $[-\omega_m, \omega_m]$.

**Step 2 — Design the reconstruction filter.**
Choose an ideal lowpass filter with:
$$H(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_m \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \omega_m \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the filter in frequency domain.**
$$Y(\omega) = X_s(\omega)\,H(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\cdot T_s \cdot X(\omega) = X(\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Fourier Transform.**
$$y(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(\omega)\} = x(t) \quad \blacksquare$$

The original signal is **perfectly reconstructed** with zero distortion.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sinc Interpolation (Whittaker-Shannon Formula)

**Goal:** Express $x(t)$ directly as a weighted sum of sinc functions evaluated at sample points.

**Step 1 — Inverse FT of the filtered spectrum.**
The reconstruction filter has impulse response:
$$h(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_m}^{\omega_m} T_s\,e^{j\omega t}\,d\omega = \frac{T_s}{2\pi}\cdot \frac{2\sin(\omega_m t)}{t} = \frac{\sin(\omega_m t)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

Since $\omega_s = 2\pi/T_s$ and $\omega_m = \omega_s/2$, we can write:
$$h(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s} = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)$$

where the normalized sinc is $\mathrm{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Step 2 — Convolve with sampled signal.**
$$x(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)\right] * h(t)$$

Using the sifting property of convolution with a delta:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,h(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Final reconstruction formula.**
$$\boxed{x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The reconstructed signal is a **weighted sum of shifted sinc functions**, where each sinc is centered at a sample time $nT_s$ and weighted by the sample value $x(nT_s)$. Each sinc has its zero crossings precisely at the other sample instants, ensuring **zero inter-symbol interference** at sampling times.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Natural Sampling Spectrum

**Step 1 — Define the pulse train.**
A rectangular pulse train with amplitude $A$, width $\tau$, and period $T_s$ is:
$$p(t) = A\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{\tau}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Fourier Series of $p(t)$.**
$$a_k = \frac{A\tau}{T_s}\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k\tau}{T_s}\right)$$

where $\mathrm{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Step 3 — Multiply by $x(t)$ and Fourier Transform.**
$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k\tau}{T_s}\right) X(\omega - k\omega_s) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Key insight:** Unlike impulse sampling, the natural sampling replicas are **weighted** by $\mathrm{sinc}(k\tau/T_s)$. As $\tau \to 0$, this approaches the impulse-sampling result.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Flat-Top Sampling Spectrum

**Step 1 — Model the flat-top sampler.**
Each sample value is held for duration $T$ (typically $T = T_s$). The output is:
$$x_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,p(t - nT_s)$$

where $p(t) = \mathrm{rect}(t/T)$.

**Step 2 — Take Fourier Transform.**
Using the modulation and shifting properties:
$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)\cdot H_p(\omega)$$

where $H_p(\omega) = T\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)e^{-j\omega T/2}$ is the spectrum of a single rectangular pulse of width $T$.

**Step 3 — Final expression.**
$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{T}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)e^{-j\omega T/2} X(\omega - k\omega_s) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aperture Effect:** The $\mathrm{sinc}(\omega T / 2\pi)$ factor introduces a **frequency-dependent droop** that attenuates high-frequency components within the baseband. To compensate, an equalization filter with response $1/\mathrm{sinc}(\omega T/2\pi)$ must follow the reconstruction filter.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A signal $x(t)$ contains frequencies up to **3 kHz**. Compute the Nyquist rate, the Nyquist interval, and the number of samples needed to represent 5 seconds of the signal.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Nyquist Rate: } f_N = 2f_m = 2 \times 3000 = 6000 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\text{Nyquist Interval: } T_N = \frac{1}{2f_m} = \frac{1}{6000} \approx 0.1667 \text{ ms}$$

$$\text{Total Samples: } N = f_s \times \text{Duration} = 6000 \times 5 = 30{,}000 \text{ samples}$$

If we choose a practical sampling rate of $f_s = 8$ kHz (oversampling by $4/3$), the total samples become $40{,}000$ in 5 seconds.

## 3.7 Aliasing Numerical Example

**Problem:** A signal with $f_m = 100$ Hz is sampled at $f_s = 150$ Hz. What is the apparent frequency of a true $80$ Hz component after sampling?

**Solution:** Compute $k = \text{round}(f/f_s) = \text{round}(80/150) = 1$.

$$f_a = \vert 80 - 1 \times 150 \vert = \vert -70 \vert = 70 \text{ Hz}$$

So a true 80 Hz tone appears as a 70 Hz tone — it has been **aliased** down into the baseband.

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full signal flow from continuous-time input to reconstructed analog output, including all critical filter stages.

```mermaid
    nodeD --> nodeE["Reconstruction LPF - Gain Ts Cutoff Wm"]

    nodeG --> nodeE

```

The following block-level diagram represents the **spectral replication** effect, where the original spectrum $X(\omega)$ is replicated at every multiple of $\omega_s$. The arrows indicate the non-overlapping condition required for perfect reconstruction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    copy1["Replicate at k = plus 1 - Ws minus Wm to Ws plus Wm"]

    copy3["Replicate at k = minus 1 - minus Ws minus Wm to minus Ws plus Wm"]
    copy4["Replicate at k = minus 2 - minus 2Ws minus Wm to minus 2Ws plus Wm"]
    lpFilter["Reconstruction LPF - Passes only k = 0 copy"]
    origSpec -- "1 over Ts amplitude scaling" --> copy1
    origSpec -- "1 over Ts amplitude scaling" --> copy2
    origSpec -- "1 over Ts amplitude scaling" --> copy3
    origSpec -- "1 over Ts amplitude scaling" --> copy4
    copy1 --> lpFilter
    copy2 --> lpFilter
    copy3 --> lpFilter

```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Avoiding Aliasing

This flow chart captures the decision logic an engineer follows when selecting a sampling rate, including the safeguards against spectral overlap.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Identify Maximum Signal Frequency Wm"] --> q1{"Is Signal Strictly Bandlimited"}
    q1 -- "Yes" --> applyLPF["Apply Anti Aliasing LPF - Cutoff at Wm"]
    q1 -- "No" --> lpfFirst["Lowpass Filter First to Make Bandlimited"]
    lpfFirst --> applyLPF
    applyLPF --> chooseFS["Choose fs greater than 2Wm - Add Safety Margin"]
    chooseFS --> checkOverlap{"Spectral Copies Overlap"}
    checkOverlap -- "No - No Aliasing" --> reconstruct["Proceed with Sampling and Reconstruction"]
    checkOverlap -- "Yes" --> increaseFS["Increase Sampling Rate"]
    increaseFS --> checkOverlap
    reconstruct --> output["Perfect Reconstruction x hat of t equals x of t"]
```
