# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Sampling Theorem for lowpass signals. Define Nyquist rate and Nyquist interval.**

**Model Answer:**

The Sampling Theorem states that a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ that is **strictly bandlimited** to a maximum frequency $W$ Hz (i.e., $X(\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > 2\pi W$) can be reconstructed exactly and uniquely from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2W$.

- **Nyquist Rate:** The minimum sampling frequency that avoids aliasing, $f_N = 2W$ samples/second.
- **Nyquist Interval:** The maximum allowable sampling period, $T_N = \frac{1}{2W}$ seconds.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**

**Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal, when sampled below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2W$), appear as **false low-frequency components** in the reconstructed signal due to spectral overlap of the shifted copies of $X(\omega)$.

**Prevention methods:**
1. Sample at a frequency $f_s > 2W$ (strictly greater than Nyquist rate).
2. Use a **sharp anti-aliasing lowpass filter** *before* sampling to bandlimit the signal to $W$ Hz.
3. In practice, use a sampling rate of $2.5W$ to $3W$ to allow for filter roll-off.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the spectrum of an impulse-sampled signal and explain the condition to avoid aliasing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = 5\cos(200\pi t) + 3\sin(400\pi t) + 2\cos(600\pi t + \pi/4)$, determine the Nyquist rate and sketch the spectrum of the sampled signal if $f_s = 500$ Hz. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Model the sampler as an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$.

**Step 2:** The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = x(t)p(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.

**Step 3:** Express $p(t)$ in exponential Fourier series:
$$p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$$

**Step 4:** Take Fourier transform:
$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$

**Step 5:** **Aliasing avoidance condition:** For the replicas of $X(\omega)$ to be non-overlapping, the gap between adjacent copies must be positive:
$$\omega_s - \omega_m > \omega_m \implies \omega_s > 2\omega_m \implies f_s > 2W \quad \blacksquare$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Identify frequency components.

From $5\cos(200\pi t)$: $f_1 = 100$ Hz.
From $3\sin(400\pi t)$: $f_2 = 200$ Hz.
From $2\cos(600\pi t + \pi/4)$: $f_3 = 300$ Hz.

**Step 2:** Maximum frequency $W = 300$ Hz.

$$\text{Nyquist rate: } f_N = 2W = 600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3:** Since $f_s = 500$ Hz $< f_N = 600$ Hz, **aliasing will occur**.

**Step 4:** Compute apparent (aliased) frequencies for each component.

For $f_1 = 100$ Hz: $k = 0$, $f_{a1} = 100$ Hz (no aliasing).
For $f_2 = 200$ Hz: $k = 0$, $f_{a2} = 200$ Hz.
For $f_3 = 300$ Hz: $k = 1$, $f_{a3} = \vert 300 - 1 \times 500 \vert = 200$ Hz (alias!).

**Step 5:** The 300 Hz component aliases onto 200 Hz, **adding constructively** to the existing 200 Hz sine wave, distorting the signal.

**Final spectrum sketch description (for the answer script):**
- Baseband spectrum contains impulses at $\pm 100$ Hz (amplitude scaled by $5/T_s$) and $\pm 200$ Hz (combined amplitude).
- Replicas appear at $\pm 500$, $\pm 1000$, $\pm 1500$ Hz, etc.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the natural sampling and flat-top sampling processes. Derive the spectrum of the flat-top sampled signal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The signal $x(t) = \mathrm{sinc}(100t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 150$ Hz. Will aliasing occur? Reconstruct the signal from its samples and verify using the Whittaker-Shannon formula. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Natural sampling:** The signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic rectangular pulse train of width $\tau$ and period $T_s$. During each pulse, the output follows the input continuously, hence the name "natural."

**Flat-top sampling:** Each sample value is **held constant** for the entire sampling period $T$ using a sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit. The output appears as a staircase approximation of $x(t)$.

**Derivation of Flat-Top Spectrum:**

The flat-top sampled signal is:
$$x_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,p(t - nT_s)$$

where $p(t) = \mathrm{rect}(t/T)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $T$.

Taking the Fourier transform:
$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)\cdot H_p(\omega)$$

with $H_p(\omega) = T\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)e^{-j\omega T/2}$.

**Final expression:**
$$X_{FT}(\omega) = \frac{T}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)e^{-j\omega T/2} X(\omega - k\omega_s) \quad \blacksquare$$

The $\mathrm{sinc}$ factor is the **aperture effect**, attenuating high-frequency baseband content.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Identify the bandwidth of $x(t) = \mathrm{sinc}(100t)$.

Recall: $\mathrm{sinc}(100t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{100}\,\mathrm{rect}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{200\pi}\right)$.

The spectrum is a rectangle from $-100\pi$ to $+100\pi$ rad/sec, i.e., from $-50$ Hz to $+50$ Hz.

So $W = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2:** Check Nyquist condition.

$$f_s = 150 \text{ Hz} > 2W = 100 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**No aliasing occurs.** Sampling is valid.

**Step 3:** Reconstruction.

The Nyquist interval is $T_N = 1/(2W) = 0.01$ s. Since $T_s = 1/150 \approx 0.00667$ s $< T_N$, reconstruction is possible.

By the Whittaker-Shannon formula:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

where the samples are $x(nT_s) = \mathrm{sinc}(100 \cdot nT_s) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{2n}{3}\right)$.

**Step 4:** Verification.

Substituting samples back:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{2n}{3}\right)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(150(t - n/150)\right) = \mathrm{sinc}(100t) \quad \blacksquare$$

This confirms exact recovery of the original $\mathrm{sinc}(100t)$ signal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ amplitude scaling** in the Replication Theorem — this costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist rate with Nyquist frequency** — Nyquist rate is a *sampling rate* (in Hz), while Nyquist frequency is the *bandwidth limit* (also in Hz). Use precise terminology.
> 3. **Ignoring aliasing in numerical problems** — many students compute $f_s$ and stop; you must explicitly **check** $f_s \geq 2W$ and state whether aliasing occurs.
> 4. **Skipping the Fourier series step** in the derivation of $X_s(\omega)$ — examiners specifically look for the step where $p(t)$ is expanded.
> 5. **Missing the aperture effect** in flat-top sampling problems — KTU 2024 specifically tests this with $\mathrm{sinc}$ droop corrections.
> 6. **Confusing natural and flat-top sampling diagrams** — natural sampling follows the input curve during the pulse, flat-top holds the value constant. Drawing the wrong one costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sampling Theorem for Lowpass Signals**

- **Sampling Theorem Statement:** A bandlimited signal with max frequency $W$ Hz can be reconstructed from its samples if $f_s \geq 2W$.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2W$ (minimum sampling rate).
- **Nyquist Interval:** $T_N = 1/(2W)$ (maximum sampling period).
- **Sampling frequency formula:** $f_s = 1/T_s$.
- **Impulse Train Model:** $p(t) = \sum_n \delta(t - nT_s)$.
- **Sampled signal (time):** $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.
- **Replication Theorem:** $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(\omega - k\omega_s)$.
- **Reconstruction LPF:** $H(\omega) = T_s$ for $\vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_m$, else $0$.
- **Sinc Interpolation:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$.
- **Aliasing occurs when:** $f_s < 2W$.
- **Anti-aliasing filter:** Must precede the sampler; cutoff at $W$ Hz.
- **Three sampling methods:** Impulse (ideal), Natural (pulse-train modulated), Flat-top (sample-and-hold).
- **Natural sampling spectrum:** Replicas weighted by $\mathrm{sinc}(k\tau/T_s)$.
- **Flat-top spectrum:** Has extra $\mathrm{sinc}(\omega T/2)$ factor — **aperture effect** — corrected by equalizer.
- **Aliasing formula:** $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ where $k$ is the nearest integer multiple.
- **Practical safety margin:** Choose $f_s$ at $2.5W$ to $3W$ to allow non-ideal filter roll-off.
- **CD audio example:** $f_m = 20$ kHz, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz.
- **Speech example:** $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 8$ kHz.
- **The Sampling Theorem is a sufficient condition** when the signal is **strictly bandlimited** and an **ideal lowpass filter** is used for reconstruction.
- **Practical real-world signals** are never perfectly bandlimited; hence anti-aliasing filters are mandatory in all digital systems.
- **Remember:** Sampling in time $\Rightarrow$ **Periodic replication** in frequency (by Convolution Theorem).
- **Whittaker-Shannon formula** is the time-domain dual of the Reconstruction LPF operation.
- **KTU 2024 weightage tip:** Expect 1 Part-A (3 marks) + 1 Part-B (7+7 marks) from this topic in End-Semester Exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nyquist Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Nyquist Criteria** (also called the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Criterion**) is a fundamental theorem in signal processing that establishes the necessary and sufficient conditions for perfectly reconstructing a continuous-time, bandlimited signal from its discrete samples without any loss of information or introduction of distortion (aliasing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement:** A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ that is strictly bandlimited to a maximum frequency $f_m$ (in Hz) can be completely reconstructed from its uniformly spaced samples $x(nT_s)$ **if and only if** the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
> The minimum allowable sampling rate, $f_s = 2 f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $2 f_m$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** (in terms of the original signal's bandwidth).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **movie camera** filming a spinning ceiling fan:

- If the camera captures **enough frames per second** (e.g., 30 fps for a slow fan), each frame catches the fan at a slightly different angle. When the frames are played back in sequence, the human eye perceives smooth, continuous motion — this is the **sampling rate being ABOVE the Nyquist rate**.
- If the camera shoots **too few frames per second** (e.g., 2 fps for a fast fan), some positions of the fan blades are completely missed. When played back, the fan may appear to spin **backwards, slowly, or freeze** — this is **aliasing**, which happens when the sampling rate is **BELOW the Nyquist rate**.

In short, the Nyquist Criteria tells us **"how fast you must 'take pictures' of a signal so that no information is lost."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight from KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners specifically look for the student to mention that the signal MUST be **bandlimited** before sampling. Sampling a non-bandlimited signal ALWAYS causes aliasing, regardless of how high the sampling rate is.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist Rate):** $\mathbf{f_s = 2 f_m}$ — measured in Hertz (Hz).
- **Sampling Interval:** $\mathbf{T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}}$ — measured in seconds (s).
- **Angular Nyquist Frequency:** $\mathbf{\omega_s = 2 \pi (2 f_m) = 4 \pi f_m}$ — measured in rad/s.
- **Nyquist–Shannon Reconstruction Cutoff:** The reconstruction filter's cutoff frequency must lie in the range $f_m \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_m$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logic Breakdown

The Nyquist Criteria is built on three operational pillars. Let us break them down:

**Pillar 1: Bandlimitedness (The Pre-Condition)**
- A signal $x(t)$ is **bandlimited** if its Fourier Transform $X(f) = 0$ for all $\vert f \vert > f_m$.
- In angular frequency terms: $X(j\omega) = 0$ for $\vert \omega \vert > \omega_m$, where $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$.
- **Why?** If the signal contains infinite frequency content, no finite sampling rate can capture it losslessly.

**Pillar 2: The Sampling Operation**
- The continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an **impulse train** $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$.
- The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)$.
- In the frequency domain, this multiplication becomes **convolution**, which **replicates** the spectrum $X(f)$ at every multiple of $f_s$.

**Pillar 3: The Non-Overlap Condition (The Core Criterion)**
- For the original spectrum to be recoverable without interference (aliasing), the shifted copies of $X(f)$ must **not overlap** in the frequency domain.
- This is mathematically equivalent to demanding: $f_s - f_m \geq f_m$, which simplifies to the golden rule $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.

### 2.2 Three Sampling Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Over-sampling** | $f_s > 2 f_m$ | Guard band exists between spectral replicas. Easy reconstruction. |
| **Critical Sampling** | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Spectral replicas just touch at $f_m$. Reconstruction is theoretically possible but practically fragile. |
| **Under-sampling (Aliasing)** | $f_s < 2 f_m$ | Spectral replicas overlap. Information is **permanently lost**. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Rate | $f_s = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Maximum Sampling Interval | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ | Seconds |
| 3 | Angular Nyquist Frequency | $\omega_s = 4 \pi f_m$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Condition for no aliasing | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Inequality |
| 5 | Ideal LPF Cutoff Range | $f_m \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_m$ | Hz |
| 6 | Sampling Frequency in terms of $\omega_m$ | $f_s = \dfrac{\omega_m}{\pi}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Reconstruction (Whittaker–Shannon) | $x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ | Time domain |
| 8 | Guard Band Width | $\Delta f = f_s - 2 f_m$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** Notice in row 5 we used $\vert f_m \vert$ with `\vert` — never use raw `|` in KTU table cells or the markdown will break.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio (CD Quality):** Audio is bandlimited at $f_m = 20\,\text{kHz}$, so $f_s = 44.1\,\text{kHz}$ is used (CD standard).
- **Telecommunications:** Speech is bandlimited to $4\,\text{kHz}$, sampled at $8\,\text{kHz}$.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** Higher sampling rates (over-sampling) are used to maximize the guard band and minimize aliasing artifacts.
- **Oversampling ADCs:** Used in precision instrumentation to relax analog anti-aliasing filter requirements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Criteria (Why does $f_s \geq 2 f_m$?)

**Step 1: Define the bandlimited signal.**
Let $x(t)$ be a continuous-time signal whose Fourier Transform $X(f)$ satisfies:
$$X(f) = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert > f_m$$

**Step 2: Define the impulse train sampler.**
An ideal impulse train with sampling period $T_s$ is given by:
$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3: Find the Fourier Transform of the impulse train.**
The impulse train is periodic with period $T_s$. Its Fourier series representation in the frequency domain is itself an impulse train:
$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(f - k f_s\right)$$

**Step 4: Multiplication in time domain becomes convolution in frequency domain.**
The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$, so:
$$X_s(f) = X(f) * P(f)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the convolution.**
Substituting $P(f)$ and using the sifting property of the impulse:
$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(f - k f_s\right)$$

**Step 6: Identify the replication pattern.**
This equation tells us that the original spectrum $X(f)$ (centered at $0$ Hz) is replicated at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \pm 3f_s, \ldots$

**Step 7: Impose the non-overlap condition.**
Each replica $X(f - k f_s)$ occupies a bandwidth of $2 f_m$ (from $-f_m$ to $+f_m$ shifted by $k f_s$). The right edge of the $k$-th replica is at $k f_s + f_m$, and the left edge of the $(k+1)$-th replica is at $(k+1) f_s - f_m$. To prevent overlap:
$$k f_s + f_m \leq (k+1) f_s - f_m$$

**Step 8: Simplify the inequality.**
Subtracting $k f_s$ from both sides:
$$f_m \leq f_s - f_m$$

Adding $f_m$ to both sides yields the **Nyquist Criteria**:
$$\boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reconstruction Formula (Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation)

**Step 1: Apply an ideal lowpass filter to the sampled spectrum.**
To extract the baseband replica, we multiply $X_s(f)$ by an ideal rectangular filter of height $T_s$ and width $2 f_m$:
$$H(f) = T_s \cdot \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{2 f_m}\right)$$

**Step 2: Take the inverse Fourier Transform.**
The impulse response of this LPF is the sinc function:
$$h(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

**Step 3: Reconstruct the original signal by convolution.**
The reconstructed signal is:
$$x(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \left[\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s)\right] * \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right)$$

**Step 4: Simplify the convolution.**
Using the sifting property of the delta function:
$$\boxed{x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula.** It proves that if the Nyquist Criteria is met, the continuous signal can be **perfectly** reconstructed from its samples using only an ideal lowpass filter.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A signal $x(t)$ contains frequency components up to $5\,\text{kHz}$. Determine the minimum sampling rate and maximum sampling interval to avoid aliasing.

**Step 1: Identify $f_m$.**
$$f_m = 5\,\text{kHz} = 5000\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Nyquist Criteria.**
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000\,\text{Hz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the minimum sampling rate.**
$$\boxed{f_s = 10\,\text{kHz}}$$

**Step 4: Compute the maximum sampling interval.**
$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 100\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Specify the LPF cutoff range.**
The reconstruction filter cutoff must satisfy:
$$5000 \leq f_c \leq 10{,}000 - 5000 = 5000\,\text{Hz}$$
Hence, the **only valid cutoff** in this critical-sampling scenario is $f_c = 5\,\text{kHz}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Sampling & Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Continuous Signal x(t)"] --> B["Anti-Aliasing Filter LPF f_m"]
    B --> C["Multiplier: Impulse Train p(t)"]
    C --> D["Sampled Signal xs(t) with spectrum replicas"]
    D --> E["Reconstruction LPF cut at f_c"]
    E --> F["Reconstructed Signal xr(t) approximates x(t)"]
    
    G["Impulse Train Generator f_s = 1 over T_s"] --> C
    
    styleA[style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px]
    styleB[style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px]
    styleC[style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px]
    styleD[style D fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px]
    styleE[style E fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px]
    styleF[style F fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#0097A7,stroke-width:2px]
    styleG[style G fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px]
```

### 4.2 Frequency Domain Spectrum Replication Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Baseband["Baseband Spectrum X(f) bandwidth 2fm centered at 0 Hz"]
    Rep1["Replica 1 centered at fs Hz"]
    RepN1["Replica centered at minus fs Hz"]
    Rep2["Replica 2 centered at 2fs Hz"]
    
    Baseband --- Rep1
    Rep1 --- Rep2
    Baseband --- RepN1
    
    Crit["If fs greater than 2fm, no overlap (valid)"]
    Ali["If fs less than 2fm, overlap occurs (aliasing)"]
    
    styleBaseband[style Baseband fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px]
    styleRep1[style Rep1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px]
    styleRep2[style Rep2 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px]
    styleRepN1[style RepN1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px]
    styleCrit[style Crit fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px]
    styleAli[style Ali fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Sampling Regime Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Is signal x(t) bandlimited with maximum frequency fm?"]
    Stop1["If NO, aliasing is unavoidable. Apply pre-filtering."]
    Branch["If YES, compare fs to 2fm."]
    Case1["Case A: fs greater than 2fm -- Over-sampling. No aliasing, guard band present."]
    Case2["Case B: fs equals 2fm -- Critical sampling. Theoretical reconstruction possible, fragile."]
    Case3["Case C: fs less than 2fm -- Under-sampling. Aliasing occurs, info loss."]
    
    Start --> Stop1
    Start --> Branch
    Branch --> Case1
    Branch --> Case2
    Branch --> Case3
    
    styleStart[style Start fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px]
    styleStop1[style Stop1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px]
    styleCase1[style Case1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px]
    styleCase2[style Case2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px]
    styleCase3[style Case3 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Nyquist Criteria for sampling a bandlimited signal.**

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist Criteria states that a continuous-time, bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ can be completely reconstructed from its uniformly spaced samples if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Nyquist Rate" and "Nyquist Interval."**

**Model Answer:**
The **Nyquist Rate** is the minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing, given by $f_s = 2 f_m$ Hz. The **Nyquist Interval** is the corresponding maximum sampling period, $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ seconds.

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** A signal $x(t) = 5 \cos(200\pi t) + 3 \sin(400\pi t) + 2 \cos(600\pi t)$ is to be sampled.
**(i)** Find the maximum frequency component present. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the Nyquist rate. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Determine the maximum sampling interval. **(1 mark)**
**(iv)** State the condition for no aliasing. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram and frequency spectrum, explain the natural sampling process. Also, derive the condition for no aliasing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**(i)** Maximum frequency:
The three frequency components are $100\,\text{Hz}$, $200\,\text{Hz}$, and $300\,\text{Hz}$. Hence $f_m = 300\,\text{Hz}$.

**(ii)** Nyquist rate:
$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 300 = 600\,\text{Hz}$$

**(iii)** Maximum sampling interval:
$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{600} = 1.667\,\text{ms}$$

**(iv)** Condition for no aliasing:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_s \geq 600\,\text{Hz}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Natural Sampling:** In natural sampling (also called *chopper sampling*), the signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a **pulse train** $p(t)$ of finite pulse width $\tau$ and period $T_s$ (not impulses).

**Block Diagram (mental model):**
$x(t)$ enters a **multiplier** together with the pulse train $p(t)$. The output is the natural-sampled signal $x_s(t)$ — top of each pulse follows the contour of $x(t)$.

**Frequency Spectrum:**
The Fourier Series of the pulse train is:
$$P(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \delta(f - k f_s), \quad C_k = \frac{\tau}{T_s} \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{k \tau}{T_s}\right)$$

Multiplying $x(t)$ by $p(t)$ gives:
$$X_s(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k \cdot X(f - k f_s)$$

**Derivation of non-aliasing condition:**
Each replica of $X(f)$ is centered at $k f_s$ with width $2 f_m$. The right edge of the $k$-th replica is at $k f_s + f_m$, and the left edge of the $(k+1)$-th replica is at $(k+1) f_s - f_m$. To avoid overlap:
$$k f_s + f_m \leq (k+1) f_s - f_m$$
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

This is the **Nyquist Criteria**.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Explain the sampling theorem. Mention its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ has $f_m = 8\,\text{kHz}$. It is sampled at $f_s = 25\,\text{kHz}$.
**(i)** Find the sampling interval. **(2 marks)**
**(ii)** Calculate the guard band. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** Determine the acceptable range of cutoff frequency for the reconstruction filter. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Sampling Theorem:**

The sampling theorem states that *a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$, when sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, can be completely reconstructed from its samples using an ideal lowpass filter.* The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the Nyquist rate. Reconstruction is given by the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

**Limitations:**
1. The signal must be **strictly bandlimited** (which is rarely true in practice — real signals have infinite bandwidth).
2. The reconstruction filter must be **ideal** (sharp cutoff, flat passband) — physically unrealizable.
3. An infinite number of samples is required for perfect reconstruction.
4. Practical systems introduce quantization noise and timing jitter.

---

**Part (b):**

**(i)** Sampling interval:
$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{25{,}000} = 40\,\mu\text{s}$$

**(ii)** Guard band:
$$\Delta f = f_s - 2 f_m = 25{,}000 - 2(8000) = 25{,}000 - 16{,}000 = 9000\,\text{Hz} = 9\,\text{kHz}$$

**(iii)** Acceptable cutoff range for reconstruction LPF:
The cutoff frequency $f_c$ must satisfy $f_m \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_m$:
$$8\,\text{kHz} \leq f_c \leq (25 - 8)\,\text{kHz}$$
$$8\,\text{kHz} \leq f_c \leq 17\,\text{kHz}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $f_s = 2 f_m$ as "equal to" instead of "$\geq$". Always write the **inequality** unless specifically asked for the Nyquist rate.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that the signal must be **bandlimited**. If a signal has a DC offset plus a sinusoid, the maximum frequency is the higher of the two — not the lower.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing $f_m$ (maximum signal frequency) with $f_s / 2$ (Nyquist frequency of the sampled system). They are the same in a critical-sampling scenario, but $f_s / 2$ is the *system's* Nyquist frequency, while $f_m$ is the *signal's* bandwidth.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing reconstruction as $\mathrm{sinc}(\pi t / T_s)$ instead of $\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$ — the **time-shift** $nT_s$ is essential.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The Nyquist Criteria is the **inequality** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, not an equality.
- ✅ **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_m$ (the *minimum* sampling rate).
- ✅ **Nyquist Interval** = $T_s = \dfrac{1}{2 f_m}$ (the *maximum* time between samples).
- ✅ The signal **must be bandlimited** for the theorem to hold.
- ✅ In the **frequency domain**, sampling creates **periodic replicas** of the spectrum centered at multiples of $f_s$.
- ✅ **Aliasing** occurs when these replicas overlap ($f_s < 2 f_m$). It is **irreversible**.
- ✅ Reconstruction is done using an **ideal lowpass filter** whose impulse response is a **sinc** function.
- ✅ **Guard band** = $f_s - 2 f_m$ (the empty region between spectral replicas).
- ✅ Reconstruction LPF cutoff range: $f_m \leq f_c \leq f_s - f_m$.
- ✅ **Over-sampling** ($f_s > 2 f_m$) is preferred in practice for robustness and relaxed filter design.
- ✅ **Practical applications:** CD audio ($44.1\,\text{kHz}$ for $20\,\text{kHz}$ audio), speech processing ($8\,\text{kHz}$ for $4\,\text{kHz}$ voice).
- ✅ The Whittaker–Shannon formula is the **key reconstruction equation** to memorize for KTU exams.
- ✅ Remember: **angle Nyquist frequency** $\omega_s = 4 \pi f_m$, not $2 \pi f_m$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing. Discrete Time Fourier Series for Discrete Periodic Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aliasing. Discrete Time Fourier Series for Discrete Periodic Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — TOPIC 1: ALIASING & DISCRETE TIME FOURIER SERIES (DTFS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Aliasing — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which two different continuous-time sinusoidal signals become **indistinguishable** (i.e., they "alias" or map onto each other) when sampled at the same sampling rate. Formally, if a continuous-time sinusoid $x_a(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \theta)$ is sampled at a rate $F_s = 1/T$ samples/sec, the resulting discrete-time signal is identical to that of another sinusoid with frequency $f_1$ whenever
> $f_1 = f_0 + k F_s$ for some integer $k$.

The apparent (aliased) digital frequency is given by the unique value $f_a$ lying in the principal band $\left[ -\dfrac{F_s}{2},\ \dfrac{F_s}{2} \right]$ such that $f_a \equiv f_0 \pmod{F_s}$.

### 1.2 Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem (Recap for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> A band-limited signal with **maximum frequency component** $F_{max}$ **(measured in Hz)** can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$F_s \;\geq\; 2 F_{max}$$
> The minimum allowable rate $F_N = 2 F_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate** (measured in **samples/second**), and $2 F_{max}$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** (measured in **Hz**).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **spinning fan** with three blades rotating at 600 rpm clockwise. Under a **stroboscope** flashing 25 times per second (so 75 flashes cover the 3 blades), the blades appear to rotate *slowly in reverse*. The eye/brain samples the motion at 25 Hz, but the true rotation is 10 Hz. Your visual system *aliases* a 10 Hz motion as a slow backward drift.

The same effect happens in DSP:
- **True signal frequency** = fan speed (e.g., 8 kHz audio)
- **Sampling frequency** = strobe rate (e.g., 6 kHz)
- **Apparent (aliased) frequency** = 2 kHz, but *with reversed direction*

This is **why old Western movies** show wagon wheels spinning backward — the 24 fps camera samples a fast wheel and folds the rotation into a lower (apparent) frequency.

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Aliasing is *irreversible*. Once it occurs at the ADC stage, the original high-frequency information is **permanently lost**. The cure is **prevention** using an **anti-aliasing filter** placed *before* the sampler.

### 1.4 Discrete Time Fourier Series (DTFS) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS)** representation expresses any **discrete-time periodic signal** $x[n]$ with fundamental period $N$ (where $N$ is a **positive integer**) as a sum of harmonically related complex exponentials:
> $$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \, e^{\,j k \Omega_0 n}, \qquad \Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$
> where the coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are themselves **periodic with period $N$** in $k$.

The notation $\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle}$ denotes summation over any contiguous set of $N$ successive integer values of $k$ (most commonly $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from CTFS:** Unlike continuous-time Fourier series (which uses $e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ with $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$ for an *arbitrary real* period $T$), the DTFS uses complex exponentials whose **digital frequency is fundamentally discrete**: $\Omega_0 = 2\pi/N$ is a rational multiple of $\pi$ when $N$ is finite.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Aliasing Occur? — The Mathematical Mechanism

When a continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ is sampled at instants $t = nT$, the resulting discrete-time sequence is:

$$x[n] = x_c(nT) = \cos(2\pi f_0 nT + \theta)$$

Using the Euler identity and the periodicity of complex exponentials:

$$e^{j 2\pi f_0 n T} = e^{j 2\pi n (f_0 \bmod F_s) T}$$

Because $e^{j 2\pi m n} = 1$ for any integer $m$, frequencies that differ by integer multiples of $F_s$ produce **exactly the same samples**. Hence they are *aliases* of one another.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The sampling operation multiplies $x_c(t)$ by an impulse train $\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT)$.
2. Multiplication in time ↔ periodic convolution in frequency (with period $F_s$).
3. If the original spectrum extends beyond $\pm F_s/2$, the replicas **overlap**.
4. The overlapping region contains *summed* spectral content — the true information is destroyed.
5. The observed digital spectrum is the *folded* version of the original around $\pm F_s/2$.

## 2.2 Computing the Aliased Frequency (KTU Favorite Question)

> [!TIP]
> To find the aliased frequency $f_a$ in the band $\bigl[-F_s/2,\ F_s/2\bigr]$:
> 1. Compute $r = f_0 / F_s$ (normalized frequency).
> 2. Find the fractional part: $\rho = \{r\}$ (i.e., $r - \lfloor r \rceil$).
> 3. The aliased frequency is $f_a = \rho \cdot F_s$ (with sign depending on parity of $\lfloor r \rfloor$).
>
> **Equivalent closed form:**
> $$f_a = f_0 - k F_s, \quad \text{where } k = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{f_0}{F_s}\right)$$

**Worked mini-example (for intuition):** $f_0 = 7\,\text{kHz}$, $F_s = 6\,\text{kHz}$.

$k = \mathrm{round}(7/6) = 1 \Rightarrow f_a = 7 - 1(6) = 1\,\text{kHz}$. So a 7 kHz tone *appears* as a 1 kHz tone.

## 2.3 Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is a **low-pass analog filter** placed *before* the ADC. It must have a cutoff frequency $f_c \le F_s/2$ (in practice, slightly below, e.g., $0.45 F_s$ for non-ideal filters) to band-limit the input signal and thus *prevent* aliasing.

## 2.4 DTFS — Conditions for Existence

A discrete-time periodic signal $x[n]$ with period $N$ possesses a DTFS representation if and only if it is **finite energy over one period**:

$$E = \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n] \vert^2 < \infty$$

This is the discrete Dirichlet condition — *much weaker* than the continuous case because no discontinuities can occur in a discrete sequence.

## 2.5 Properties of DTFS (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Property | Continuous Signal | DTFS Coefficients | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linearity | $A x[n] + B y[n]$ | $A a_k + B b_k$ | Superposition of harmonic components |
| Time Shifting | $x[n - n_0]$ | $a_k\, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n_0}$ | Delay lines, phase response analysis |
| Frequency Shifting | $x[n]\, e^{j M \Omega_0 n}$ | $a_{k-M}$ | Modulation, up/down conversion |
| Time Reversal | $x[-n]$ | $a_{-k}$ | Symmetry analysis (even/odd signals) |
| Periodic Convolution | $\sum_{m=\langle N\rangle} x[m]\, y[n-m]$ | $N\, a_k\, b_k$ | Filtering in frequency domain |
| Multiplication | $x[n]\, y[n]$ | $\sum_{m=\langle N\rangle} a_m\, b_{k-m}$ | Modulation, windowing in DSP |
| Conjugation | $x^*[n]$ | $a_{-k}^*$ | Hermitian symmetry, real signal properties |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N\rangle} \vert x[n]\vert^2$ | $= \sum_{k=\langle N\rangle} \vert a_k \vert^2$ | Energy conservation; spectral power calculation |
| Real & Even $x[n]$ | $a_k = a_{-k}$ real | $a_k$ real and even | Cosine Fourier series form |
| Real & Odd $x[n]$ | $a_k = -a_{-k}$ purely imaginary | $a_k$ purely imaginary and odd | Sine Fourier series form |

## 2.6 Complete DTFS Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` for absolute value below — kept out of table pipes to preserve markdown.

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental digital frequency | $\Omega_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{N}$ | radians/sample |
| Synthesis (Inverse DTFS) | $x[n] = \displaystyle\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k (2\pi/N)\, n}$ | Sequence $x[n]$ |
| Analysis (DTFS coefficient) | $a_k = \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k (2\pi/N)\, n}$ | Complex scalar |
| Periodicity in $k$ | $a_{k+N} = a_k$ | Always |
| Periodicity in $n$ | $x[n+N] = x[n]$ | Given |
| Nyquist Rate (Analog) | $F_N = 2\,F_{max}$ | samples/second |
| Nyquist Frequency | $F_s/2$ | Hz |
| Aliased frequency | $f_a = \vert f_0 - k F_s \vert$ for $k$ s.t. $\vert f_a \vert \le F_s/2$ | Hz |
| Normalized digital frequency | $\omega = 2\pi\, f / F_s = \Omega T$ | radians/sample |
| AAF cutoff rule | $f_c \le 0.5\, F_s$ | Hz |
| Parseval's Power Identity | $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} \vert a_k \vert^2$ | Power |
| Discrete-Time Parseval | $\sum_{k=\langle N\rangle} \vert a_k \vert^2$ = average power | Watts (normalized) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - **Aliasing prevention** is critical in **digital audio** (CD uses $F_s = 44.1$ kHz to capture 20 kHz human hearing).
> - **DTFS** is the mathematical backbone of the **DFT/FFT**, which powers MP3, JPEG, OFDM in 4G/5G, and MRI reconstruction.
> - In **biomedical instrumentation** (ECG, EEG), anti-aliasing filters are mandatory before digitisation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DTFS Analysis Equation (Coefficients $a_k$)

**Goal:** Given a discrete-time periodic signal $x[n]$ with period $N$, derive the formula for the DTFS coefficients $a_k$.

**Step 1 — Hypothesis (Synthesis assumption):**
Assume $x[n]$ can be expressed as:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{\,j k \Omega_0 n}, \qquad \Omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $e^{-j m \Omega_0 n}$ and sum over one period:**

$$\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j m \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle}\left[ \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{\,j k \Omega_0 n}\right] e^{-j m \Omega_0 n}$$

**Step 3 — Interchange the order of summation:**

$$= \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \left[ \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} e^{\,j (k - m) \Omega_0 n}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inner sum (orthogonality of complex exponentials):**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j (k - m) \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{\,j (k - m)(2\pi/N)\, n}$$

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r^n = \dfrac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ with $r = e^{j (k-m)(2\pi/N)}$:

$$r^N = e^{j (k-m)(2\pi/N)\cdot N} = e^{j (k-m) 2\pi} = 1$$

Therefore the numerator $1 - r^N = 1 - 1 = 0$, while the denominator $1 - r \ne 0$ (since $k - m$ is not a multiple of $N$ when $k \ne m$).

Hence:

$$\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} e^{\,j (k - m) \Omega_0 n} = \begin{cases} N, & k = m \pmod{N} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \;\equiv\; N\, \delta_{k,m}$$

where $\delta_{k,m}$ is the Kronecker delta.

**Step 5 — Substitute the orthogonality result back:**

$$\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j m \Omega_0 n} = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k \cdot N\, \delta_{k,m} = N\, a_m$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $a_m$ (relabel $m \to k$):**

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N}\, \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k \Omega_0 n} = \frac{1}{N}\, \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k (2\pi/N)\, n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **DTFS Analysis Equation** — the most important formula in this topic for KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Aliased Frequency Formula

**Given:** Continuous sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ sampled at $F_s = 1/T$.

**Step 1 — Express sampled signal:**

$$x[n] = \cos\!\left(2\pi f_0\, n T\right) = \cos\!\left(2\pi n\,\frac{f_0}{F_s}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define the normalized frequency $\hat f_0 = f_0 / F_s$:**

$$x[n] = \cos(2\pi n \hat f_0)$$

**Step 3 — Reduce $\hat f_0$ modulo 1 (because cosine has period $2\pi$ in its argument):**

Any integer $k$ can be added to the coefficient of $2\pi n$ without changing the value:

$$2\pi n \hat f_0 = 2\pi n(\hat f_0 - k) + 2\pi n k$$

Since $\cos(2\pi n k + \theta) = \cos(\theta)$ for integer $k$, we get:

$$x[n] = \cos\!\big(2\pi n(\hat f_0 - k)\big)$$

**Step 4 — Choose $k = \mathrm{round}(\hat f_0)$ to place $\hat f_a = \hat f_0 - k$ in $[-0.5, 0.5]$:**

$$\hat f_a = \hat f_0 - \mathrm{round}(\hat f_0) \;\in\; \left[-\tfrac{1}{2},\ \tfrac{1}{2}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to Hz:**

$$f_a = \hat f_a \cdot F_s = f_0 - k F_s, \quad k = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{f_0}{F_s}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign-flip rule:** If $k$ is **odd**, the aliased signal also has its phase negated (i.e., the apparent direction of rotation is *reversed*). This explains the strobe/wagon-wheel effect.

## 3.3 Derivation of Parseval's Theorem for DTFS

**Starting from** $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k \Omega_0 n}$, compute the average power:

$$\frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, x^*[n]$$

Substitute the synthesis expansion for $x[n]$ and $x^*[n] = \sum_{m=\langle N \rangle} a_m^*\, e^{-j m \Omega_0 n}$:

$$= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} \left[\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{j k \Omega_0 n}\right]\left[\sum_{m=\langle N \rangle} a_m^*\, e^{-j m \Omega_0 n}\right]$$

**Interchange the sums:**

$$= \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=\langle N \rangle}\sum_{m=\langle N \rangle} a_k a_m^* \left[\sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} e^{j (k - m)\Omega_0 n}\right]$$

**Apply orthogonality (sum in brackets equals $N\delta_{k,m}$):**

$$= \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, a_k^* = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} \vert a_k \vert^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical meaning:** The **average power of a periodic discrete signal** equals the **sum of the squared magnitudes of its DTFS coefficients**. This is a direct energy-conservation identity.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: DTFS of a Rectangular Pulse Train

**Signal:** $x[n]$ is periodic with period $N = 8$, defined over one period as:

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0, 1, 2, 3 \\ 0, & n = 4, 5, 6, 7 \end{cases}$$

**Find $a_k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$.**

**Apply the analysis formula:**

$$a_k = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{n=0}^{7} x[n]\, e^{-j k (2\pi/8) n} = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{n=0}^{3} e^{-j k \pi n / 4}$$

**Geometric sum (length 4, ratio $r = e^{-j k \pi / 4}$):**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{3} r^n = \frac{1 - r^4}{1 - r} = \frac{1 - e^{-j k \pi}}{1 - e^{-j k \pi / 4}}$$

**Numerator:** $1 - e^{-j k \pi} = 1 - \cos(k\pi) + j\sin(k\pi) = \begin{cases} 0, & k \text{ even} \\ 2, & k \text{ odd} \end{cases}$

**For $k = 0$ (DC term):** Sum equals 4 directly, so $a_0 = 4/8 = 1/2$.

**For $k = 2$:** Numerator = 0, hence $a_2 = 0$.

**For $k = 1$:** $r^4 = e^{-j\pi} = -1$, so numerator = 2. Denominator = $1 - e^{-j\pi/4} = 1 - \tfrac{\sqrt 2}{2} + j\tfrac{\sqrt 2}{2}$. Compute $|1 - e^{-j\pi/4}|^2 = 2 - \sqrt 2$.

$$a_1 = \frac{1}{8}\cdot \frac{2}{1 - e^{-j\pi/4}} = \frac{1}{4(1 - e^{-j\pi/4})}$$

Multiplying by conjugate:

$$a_1 = \frac{1 + e^{j\pi/4}}{4 \cdot 2(1 - \cos(\pi/4))} = \frac{1 + e^{j\pi/4}}{4(2 - \sqrt 2)}$$

Final numerical value (after simplification):

$$a_1 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{e^{j\pi/8}}{2\sin(\pi/8)} = \frac{1}{8\sin(\pi/8)}\, e^{j\pi/8}$$

Numerical: $\sin(\pi/8) \approx 0.3827$, so $\vert a_1\vert \approx 0.3266$, $\angle a_1 = 22.5°$.

By conjugate symmetry (since $x[n]$ is real), $a_{-1} = a_1^*$, and so on. The remaining coefficients $a_3, a_4, \ldots, a_7$ follow from the periodicity $a_{k+8} = a_k$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Forgetting that $a_k$ is **periodic in $k$ with period $N$**. When asked to "find all $a_k$", report only $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ — the rest are repeats. Markers deduct 2 marks for redundant listing.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sampling Pipeline with Anti-Aliasing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Analog Input Signal xa of t"] --> nodeB["Anti Aliasing Lowpass Filter AAF with cutoff Fc less than Fs divided by 2"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Sampler at rate Fs samples per second"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Quantizer and Coder"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Digital Signal x of n"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DTFS Analysis Stage"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Coefficients ak periodic in k with period N"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DTFS Synthesis Stage"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Reconstructed Periodic Sequence"]

    nodeJ["If Fc exceeds Fs divided by 2"] -.->|violation| nodeK["ALIASING OCCURS in nodeC"]
    nodeK -.->|causes| nodeL["High frequency content folds back into baseband"]
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Domain Schematic: Spectral Folding Due to Aliasing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    orig["Original Spectrum: impulses at plus or minus F0 and plus or minus 3F0"]
    fs["Sampling Operation multiplies by impulse train"]
    rep["Replicated Spectra centered at 0 plus or minus m Fs for integer m"]
    fold["If F0 greater than Fs divided by 2: replicas overlap"]
    loss["Overlapped region: original information is LOST"]
    obs["Observed baseband spectrum contains folded alias at Fa"]

    orig --> fs
    fs --> rep
    rep --> fold
    fold --> loss
    loss --> obs
```

## 4.3 DTFS Synthesis–Analysis Loop (Conceptual Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["Time Domain x of n with period N"] -->|"DTFS Analysis a_k = 1/N times sum x of n e to minus j k Omega0 n"| s2["Frequency Domain coefficients a_k periodic with period N"]
    s2 -->|"DTFS Synthesis x of n = sum a_k e to j k Omega0 n"| s1
    s2 --> s3["Magnitude Spectrum vertical bar a_k vertical bar"]
    s2 --> s4["Phase Spectrum angle a_k"]
    s3 --> s5["Parseval: sum vertical bar a_k vertical bar squared = average power"]
    s4 --> s5
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Is Aliasing Occurring?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{"Is Fs greater than or equal to 2 times Fmax"}
    q1 -->|YES| n1["NO aliasing - reconstructible signal"]
    q1 -->|NO| q2{"Is an anti aliasing filter present before ADC"}
    q2 -->|YES and Fc less than Fs divided by 2| n2["NO aliasing - filter band limits input"]
    q2 -->|NO or Fc too high| y1["ALIASING will occur"]
    y1 --> y2["Altered frequency: fa = f0 minus k Fs with k = round f0 by Fs"]
    y2 --> y3["Odd k gives phase reversed alias"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define aliasing. Under what sampling condition is aliasing completely avoided in a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $F_{max}$ Hz?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which two different continuous-time signals, when sampled at the same rate, produce identical sample sequences, making them indistinguishable. Mathematically, signals whose frequencies differ by an integer multiple of the sampling frequency $F_s$ are aliases of one another.

**Condition (1 mark):** Aliasing is completely avoided if and only if the sampling rate satisfies the **Nyquist criterion:**

$$F_s \;\geq\; 2 F_{max}$$

where $F_{max}$ is the highest frequency component (in Hz) present in the band-limited signal. The minimum rate $F_s = 2 F_{max}$ is termed the **Nyquist rate**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the Discrete-Time Fourier Series (DTFS) synthesis and analysis equations for a periodic discrete-time signal $x[n]$ with fundamental period $N$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Synthesis Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N \rangle} a_k\, e^{\,j k (2\pi/N)\, n}$$

This reconstructs the time-domain sequence from its spectral coefficients.

**Analysis Equation (1.5 marks):**

$$a_k = \frac{1}{N}\, \sum_{n=\langle N \rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k (2\pi/N)\, n}$$

This extracts the $k$-th harmonic coefficient from the periodic signal over one period. The coefficients $a_k$ are themselves periodic in $k$ with period $N$: $a_{k+N} = a_k$.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]*

**(a)** A continuous-time sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 7500\, t)$ is sampled at $F_s = 5000$ samples/second.
   **(i)** Determine whether aliasing occurs. Justify with calculation. *(3 marks)*
   **(ii)** Find the apparent (aliased) frequency of the sampled signal. *(4 marks)*

**(b)** Find the DTFS representation of the discrete-time periodic signal:
$$x[n] = \{1,\ 1,\ 0,\ 0\}, \quad \text{with period } N = 4$$
Sketch the magnitude and phase spectra of $a_k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a)(i) — Whether aliasing occurs (3 marks)

Maximum frequency: $F_{max} = 7500\,\text{Hz}$.
Nyquist rate: $F_N = 2 F_{max} = 15000\,\text{samples/s}$.
Given sampling rate: $F_s = 5000\,\text{samples/s}$.

Since $F_s = 5000 < F_N = 15000$, the Nyquist criterion is **violated** → **aliasing WILL occur.** (3 marks)

##### Part (a)(ii) — Apparent aliased frequency (4 marks)

Normalized frequency:

$$\hat f_0 = \frac{f_0}{F_s} = \frac{7500}{5000} = 1.5$$

Choose $k = \mathrm{round}(1.5) = 2$ (rounding to nearest even integer for *unique* folded result, or use $k = 1$ yielding $f_a = 2.5\,\text{kHz}$ — let us use the standard folded-into-baseband method):

The principal band is $\bigl[-F_s/2,\ F_s/2\bigr] = [-2500,\ 2500]\,\text{Hz}$. Reduce $f_0$ by integer multiples of $F_s$:

$$f_a = f_0 - k F_s = 7500 - 1 \times 5000 = 2500\,\text{Hz} \;(\text{still on edge})$$

Or $f_a = 7500 - 2 \times 5000 = -2500\,\text{Hz}$ (equivalent alias — both at Nyquist edge).

**Aliased frequency** $f_a = 2500\,\text{Hz}$ (or $-2500\,\text{Hz}$, phase reversed). (4 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write $f_a = 2500$ Hz without specifying it lies on the band *edge*. Always verify that $\vert f_a \vert \le F_s / 2$. On the edge, the signal is *borderline* — adding a tiny safety margin is good practice. Losing 1 mark for missing the edge-case justification is common.

##### Part (b) — DTFS of $x[n] = \{1, 1, 0, 0\}$, $N = 4$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the analysis formula:**

$$a_k = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n]\, e^{-j k (\pi/2) n} = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(1 + e^{-j k \pi/2}\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Compute each coefficient:**

- $k = 0$: $a_0 = \frac{1}{4}(1 + 1) = \dfrac{1}{2}$

- $k = 1$: $a_1 = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(1 + e^{-j\pi/2}\bigr) = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(1 - j\bigr) = \dfrac{1}{4}\sqrt{2}\, e^{-j\pi/4}$
  $\Rightarrow \vert a_1 \vert = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{4} \approx 0.3536,\ \angle a_1 = -45°$

- $k = 2$: $a_2 = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(1 + e^{-j\pi}\bigr) = \frac{1}{4}(1 - 1) = 0$

- $k = 3$: $a_3 = \frac{1}{4}\bigl(1 + e^{-j 3\pi/2}\bigr) = \frac{1}{4}(1 + j) = \dfrac{\sqrt 2}{4}\, e^{j\pi/4}$
  $\Rightarrow \vert a_3 \vert = 0.3536,\ \angle a_3 = +45°$

**Step 3 — Verify with periodicity:** $a_4 = \frac{1}{4}(1 + e^{-j 2\pi}) = \frac{1}{4}(1+1) = a_0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Verify Parseval:**

$$\frac{1}{4}\sum_{n=0}^{3} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4}(1 + 1 + 0 + 0) = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\sum_{k=0}^{3} \vert a_k \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + 0 + \frac{1}{8} = \frac{1}{2}\ \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude spectrum sketch:**

```
|ak|
0.5  *              a0
0.35 *        *           *        *
0.25
0.15
     |---|---|---|---|---|--- k
     0   1   2   3   4   5 ...
     a0  a1   0   a3  a0 ...
```

**Step 6 — Phase spectrum:**

```
∠ak (rad)
 +π/4              *  a3
   0   *  a0       *  a1
 -π/4
        |---|---|---|---|--- k
        0   1   2   3   4 ...
```

(Magnitude and phase spectra are 7 marks total — break down: 3 for coefficients, 2 for magnitude, 2 for phase.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]*

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of aliasing with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of an **anti-aliasing filter** in a digital signal processing system. Why must this filter be an *analog* filter placed *before* the ADC? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Determine the DTFS coefficients $a_k$ for the signal:
$$x[n] = \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\, n\right) + 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\, n\right)$$
Show that $a_{k+12} = a_k$, hence state the fundamental period $N$. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Aliasing explanation with diagram and AAF role (7 marks)

**Aliasing explanation (3 marks):**
When a continuous-time signal $x_a(t)$ is sampled uniformly at instants $t = nT$, the resulting discrete-time sequence is $x[n] = x_a(nT)$. By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, if the original signal contains frequency components above $F_s/2$, the periodic replication of the spectrum (in the frequency domain) causes adjacent replicas to **overlap**, producing an irreversible distortion termed **aliasing**.

**Diagrammatic explanation (1.5 marks):** *(refer to Section 4.2 above for the folding schematic)*

**Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF) (1.5 marks):**
An AAF is a low-pass analog filter with cutoff $f_c \le F_s/2$. It band-limits the analog input *before* sampling, ensuring the signal presented to the ADC contains no frequency components above $F_s/2$, thereby **preventing aliasing** entirely.

**Why analog and pre-ADC? (1 mark):**
The AAF *must* be analog and placed *before* the ADC because:
1. Once aliasing occurs *inside* the ADC, the information is destroyed.
2. A digital filter operates on *already-sampled* data — it cannot recover information lost in the analog band.
3. Practical anti-aliasing is most effectively implemented with passive/active analog components at the front-end of the data-acquisition chain.

##### Part (b) — DTFS of $x[n] = \cos(\pi n / 6) + 2\sin(\pi n / 3)$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the constituent frequencies:**

- $\cos(\pi n / 6)$ has $\Omega_1 = \pi/6 = 2\pi/12 \Rightarrow$ harmonic number $k_1 = 1$, period $N_1 = 12$.
- $\sin(\pi n / 3)$ has $\Omega_2 = \pi/3 = 4\pi/12 = 2\pi \cdot 2/12 \Rightarrow$ harmonic number $k_2 = 2$, period $N_2 = 6$.

**Step 2 — Fundamental period:** $N = \mathrm{lcm}(12, 6) = 12$. So $\Omega_0 = 2\pi/12 = \pi/6$. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Express as complex exponentials:**

$$\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(e^{j \pi n /6} + e^{-j \pi n /6}\right)$$

$$2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{3}\right) = 2\cdot\frac{1}{2j}\!\left(e^{j \pi n /3} - e^{-j \pi n /3}\right) = \frac{1}{j}\!\left(e^{j 2\pi n/12 \cdot 2} - e^{-j 2\pi n/12 \cdot 2}\right) = -j\!\left(e^{j\pi n/3} - e^{-j\pi n/3}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}e^{j\pi n/3} - \frac{1}{2}e^{-j\pi n/3} \cdot (-1) = \text{wait — recompute carefully.}$$

$$2\sin(\pi n/3) = 2\cdot \frac{e^{j\pi n/3} - e^{-j\pi n/3}}{2j} = \frac{e^{j\pi n/3} - e^{-j\pi n/3}}{j} = -j\!\left(e^{j\pi n/3} - e^{-j\pi n/3}\right) = je^{-j\pi n/3} - je^{j\pi n/3}$$

So in terms of harmonics $k$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2}e^{j \pi n/6} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-j \pi n/6} - je^{j \pi n/3} + je^{-j\pi n/3}$$

Using $\pi n/6 = (2\pi/12) \cdot n$, $\pi n/3 = (2\pi/12) \cdot 2n$:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2}e^{j(2\pi/12)(1)n} + \frac{1}{2}e^{-j(2\pi/12)(1)n} + (-j)e^{j(2\pi/12)(2)n} + (j)e^{-j(2\pi/12)(2)n}$$

**Step 4 — Read off $a_k$ (matching coefficients of $e^{j(2\pi/12)k\,n}$):**

| $k$ | $a_k$ |
|---|---|
| $1$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ |
| $-1 \equiv 11$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ |
| $2$ | $-j$ |
| $-2 \equiv 10$ | $j$ |
| $0, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9$ | $0$ |

**Step 5 — Verify periodicity $a_{k+12} = a_k$ (1 mark):**

Since $a_{1+12} = a_{13} = a_1 = 1/2$ (all others shift cyclically), periodicity in $k$ with period 12 is confirmed. The fundamental period is $N = 12$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Forgetting the LCM rule:** Students often state the period as $\max(12, 6) = 12$ by *luck*. Always use the rigorous formula $N = \mathrm{lcm}(N_1, N_2)$ for sums of periodic sequences. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 2. **Sign error in $2\sin$ expansion:** A common mistake is writing $2\sin\theta = e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}$ (missing the $1/j$ factor). Always re-derive the Euler identity:
> $$\sin\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$$
> (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> 3. **Not reporting all $a_k$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$:** Examiners expect *all* 12 coefficients in the answer. Listing only the nonzero ones forfeits the "completeness" check mark. (Loses 1 mark.)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Aliasing & DTFS**

- ✅ **Aliasing** = high-frequency signal misrepresented as a lower frequency after sampling. Caused by $F_s < 2 F_{max}$.
- ✅ **Nyquist Rate** $F_N = 2 F_{max}$ in Hz; **Nyquist Frequency** = $F_s/2$ in Hz.
- ✅ **Aliased frequency** formula: $f_a = f_0 - k F_s$ with $k = \mathrm{round}(f_0/F_s)$ ensuring $\vert f_a\vert \le F_s/2$.
- ✅ **Odd $k$ → phase-reversed alias**; **Even $k$ → same-phase alias**.
- ✅ **Anti-aliasing filter**: analog, low-pass, placed *before* the ADC, with cutoff $f_c \le F_s/2$.
- ✅ **DTFS Synthesis**: $x[n] = \sum_{k=\langle N\rangle} a_k\, e^{j k (2\pi/N)\,n}$.
- ✅ **DTFS Analysis**: $a_k = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N\rangle} x[n]\, e^{-j k (2\pi/N)\,n}$.
- ✅ $a_k$ is **periodic in $k$ with period $N$** — only $N$ unique complex exponentials.
- ✅ **Orthogonality identity** (key to derivation): $\sum_{n=\langle N\rangle} e^{j(k-m)\Omega_0 n} = N\,\delta_{k,m}$.
- ✅ **Parseval's Theorem**: $\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=\langle N\rangle} \vert x[n]\vert^2 = \sum_{k=\langle N\rangle} \vert a_k\vert^2$ (average power).
- ✅ **Real & even** $x[n] \Rightarrow a_k$ real & even; **real & odd** $x[n] \Rightarrow a_k$ purely imaginary & odd.
- ✅ **Time shift** property: $x[n - n_0] \Leftrightarrow a_k\, e^{-jk\Omega_0 n_0}$.
- ✅ **Frequency shift** property: $x[n]\,e^{jM\Omega_0 n} \Leftrightarrow a_{k-M}$.
- ✅ **Periodic convolution** property: $\sum_{m=\langle N\rangle} x[m]y[n-m] \Leftrightarrow N\, a_k b_k$.
- ✅ **Multiplication** property: $x[n]y[n] \Leftrightarrow \sum_{m=\langle N\rangle} a_m b_{k-m}$ (periodic convolution of coefficients).
- ✅ For a sum of sinusoids: $N = \mathrm{lcm}(N_1, N_2, \ldots)$ of individual periods.
- ✅ **Common errors** to avoid: forgetting $|f_a| \le F_s/2$ check; reporting $a_k$ for $k > N-1$ as new information; omitting Parseval verification; missing the factor $1/j$ in sine expansion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) - Convergence Condition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) - Convergence Condition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of DTFT

The **Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is formally defined as a complex-valued function of the continuous angular frequency variable $\omega$ (in radians per sample):

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The **Inverse DTFT (IDTFT)** that recovers the original sequence is given by:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DTFT is the *frequency-domain representation* of a discrete-time, non-periodic sequence. It is **periodic in $\omega$ with period $2\pi$** because $e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = e^{j\omega n}$. Always plot the magnitude $|X(e^{j\omega})|$ only over the principal interval $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

### 1.2 What is the "Convergence Condition"?

A mathematical series is said to **converge** if the infinite summation reaches a *finite, well-defined value*. The DTFT is an infinite sum — therefore, the critical question is: **"Under what conditions on $x[n]$ does this sum not blow up to infinity?"**

The answer is what we call the **Convergence Condition of the DTFT**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Bucket and the Rain

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket"**
>
> Imagine an infinitely tall **bucket** that catches raindrops. Each raindrop represents one sample $x[n]$ of your signal.
>
> - If the raindrops keep falling in **finite total quantity** (e.g., the storm eventually stops or weakens), the water level in the bucket **stays finite** → the DTFT sum **converges**.
> - If an **infinite total mass** of raindrops is falling (e.g., a constant signal $x[n] = 1$ for all $n$), the bucket **overflows** → the DTFT sum **diverges**, and the DTFT **does not exist** in the conventional sense.
>
> The mathematical statement of "finite total rain" is the condition $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ — i.e., the sequence is **absolutely summable**.

### 1.4 The Two Master Conditions (Sufficient & Necessary)

| # | Condition Name | Mathematical Statement | Type |
|---|----------------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | **Absolute Summability** (Sufficient) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ | Sufficient |
| 2 | **Square Summability / Finite Energy** (Weaker) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$ | Necessary (for $L^2$ sense) |

The DTFT **converges uniformly** to a continuous function when condition (1) holds. When only condition (2) holds, the DTFT exists in the **mean-square sense** (allowing impulses like the Dirac comb in the frequency domain).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Why Does Convergence Matter?

A sum of infinitely many complex exponentials may not exist as a finite number. For example:

$$S = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + \dots = \infty \quad \text{(diverges)}$$

To guarantee the DTFT sum is a meaningful, finite function of $\omega$, we need a constraint on the sequence $x[n]$. Without convergence, the magnitude spectrum $|X(e^{j\omega})|$ is undefined and the system analysis tools (filtering, convolution theorem) collapse.

### 2.2 The "How" — Logical Steps of Convergence Analysis

1. **Start with the DTFT sum:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$.
2. **Take the absolute value on both sides** using the triangle inequality:
   $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert$.
3. **Recognize that** $\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = 1$ for all real $\omega$ and integer $n$.
4. **Conclude** that $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert$.
5. **Therefore**, if $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$, the right-hand side is finite, and so is $|X(e^{j\omega})|$ for **every** $\omega$. The DTFT is **bounded** and **converges uniformly**.
6. **By the Weierstrass M-Test**, the series converges uniformly to a continuous function of $\omega$.

### 2.3 The Three Dirichlet Conditions (Rigorous Statement)

For the DTFT of a sequence $x[n]$ to converge pointwise at a frequency $\omega$:

- **Condition 1:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolute summability).
- **Condition 2:** $x[n]$ is of bounded variation on any finite interval.
- **Condition 3:** $x[n]$ has only a finite number of discontinuities and finite jumps (maxima/minima) in any finite interval.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In KTU exam answers, you only need to mention the **sufficient condition** (absolute summability) for full marks. Mentioning Dirichlet conditions shows depth and earns extra credit.

### 2.4 Connection with the Z-Transform

The DTFT is precisely the **Z-Transform evaluated on the unit circle** $z = e^{j\omega}$ in the complex $z$-plane:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

Therefore, the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of the Z-Transform must include the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ for the DTFT to exist. This gives us the geometric test: a causal sequence $a^n u[n]$ has DTFT existing only if $\vert a \vert < 1$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** Stability of an LTI system requires the ROC to include the unit circle → DTFT (frequency response) exists → filter can be reliably characterized.
- **Spectral Analysis:** Speech, audio, biomedical (ECG/EEG) signals are energy signals, hence their DTFT exists in the $L^2$ sense.
- **Communication Systems:** OFDM and PSK modulators rely on DTFT-based symbol shaping; convergence ensures no aliasing artifacts.
- **Image Processing:** 2D-DFT of natural images converges because pixel intensities are square-summable (finite energy).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Statement | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | DTFT (Analysis) | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ principal interval |
| 2 | IDTFT (Synthesis) | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$ | Recovers sequence |
| 3 | Absolute Summability | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ | **Sufficient** condition for DTFT existence |
| 4 | Square Summability | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$ | Necessary for $L^2$ convergence |
| 5 | Boundedness Result | $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert$ | Follows from triangle inequality |
| 6 | Periodicity | $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | Always periodic with period $2\pi$ |
| 7 | Z-Transform Link | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | ROC must contain unit circle |
| 8 | Geometric Test | $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ → DTFT exists iff $\vert a \vert < 1$ | Causal exponential case |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Boundedness Inequality (Triangle Inequality Proof)

**Goal:** Prove that if $x[n]$ is absolutely summable, then $X(e^{j\omega})$ is finite and bounded for all $\omega$.

**Step 1:** Start with the DTFT definition and apply the magnitude operator on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \\[4pt]
\Big\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \Big\vert &= \left\vert \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **triangle inequality** for complex sums, which states that the magnitude of a sum is at most the sum of the magnitudes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Big\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \Big\vert &\le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \Big\vert x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \Big\vert \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Use Euler's identity. Since $e^{-j\omega n} = \cos(\omega n) - j\sin(\omega n)$, its magnitude is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert &= \sqrt{\cos^{2}(\omega n) + \sin^{2}(\omega n)} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = 1$ back into the inequality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Big\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \Big\vert &\le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot 1 \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** If $\sum \vert x[n] \vert$ is finite (the convergence condition), then $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is **bounded** by that finite value for **every** $\omega$. The DTFT exists and is finite everywhere — this proves absolute summability is a **sufficient condition**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: DTFT of a Causal Exponential — Convergence Test

**Problem:** Find the DTFT of $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ and determine the convergence condition on $a$.

**Step 1:** Write the DTFT sum (using $u[n] = 1$ for $n \ge 0$ and $0$ otherwise):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^{n} u[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** This is an infinite geometric series with common ratio $r = a e^{-j\omega}$. Recall that $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1-r}$ converges only if $\vert r \vert < 1$.

**Step 3:** Compute $\vert r \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert r \vert &= \vert a e^{-j\omega} \vert = \vert a \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\omega} \vert = \vert a \vert \cdot 1 = \vert a \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, convergence requires $\vert a \vert < 1$. The closed-form DTFT is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \text{for } \vert a \vert < 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A growing exponential $\vert a \vert > 1$ has no DTFT — the sum diverges. This is the canonical "non-convergent" example KTU examiners love.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Counter-Example — The Constant Sequence Diverges

**Problem:** Show that $x[n] = 1$ for all $n$ has **no** DTFT in the conventional sense.

**Step 1:** Attempt to compute the DTFT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} 1 \cdot e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j\omega n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Test absolute summability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert 1 \vert = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} 1 = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The condition $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ is **violated** → DTFT does not converge in the ordinary sense. (However, using **Generalized Functions / Distributions**, one writes $X(e^{j\omega}) = 2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$, an impulse train.)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Sequence $x[n]$ | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert$ | DTFT Exists? | Result |
|------------------|--------------------------|---------------|--------|
| $a^{n} u[n]$, $\vert a \vert < 1$ | $\frac{1}{1 - \vert a \vert} < \infty$ | ✅ Yes | $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ |
| $a^{n} u[n]$, $\vert a \vert \ge 1$ | $\infty$ | ❌ No | Diverges |
| $u[n]$ (unit step) | $\infty$ | ❌ No (ordinary) | $X = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \delta(\omega)$ (generalized) |
| $\delta[n]$ (impulse) | $1 < \infty$ | ✅ Yes | $X(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ (infinite) | $\infty$ | ❌ No (ordinary) | $\pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$ (generalized) |
| Finite-duration sequence | Finite sum of $\vert x[n] \vert$ | ✅ Always Yes | Always converges |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Decision Flow — Does the DTFT Converge?

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **decision logic** an engineer (or an examiner!) follows when asked whether a given sequence has a DTFT.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sequence x of n] --> B{Is sum of absolute value of x of n finite?}
    B -- YES, finite --> C[DTFT converges uniformly]
    C --> D[Continuous bounded X of e to the j omega]
    D --> E[All frequency tools apply: convolution, modulation, filtering]
    B -- NO, infinite --> F{Is sum of x of n squared finite?}
    F -- YES, finite energy --> G[DTFT exists in L2 sense]
    G --> H[Mean square convergence: allows impulses and discontinuities in spectrum]
    F -- NO, infinite energy --> I[DTFT does NOT exist in ordinary sense]
    I --> J[Need generalized functions: impulses, principal value integrals]
    J --> K[Example: constant, sinusoid, unit step]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Z-Transform to DTFT Link

The diagram below illustrates the **hierarchical relationship** between the Z-Transform, DTFT, and the convergence condition imposed by the unit circle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Z Transform of x of n] -->|Evaluate on unit circle| D[DTFT of x of n]
    Z --> R[Region of Convergence ROC]
    R --> Q{Does ROC include unit circle?}
    Q -- YES --> D
    Q -- NO --> N[DTFT does not exist]
    D --> P[Periodic in omega with period 2 pi]
    P --> U[Useful for spectral analysis and filter design]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Triangle Inequality Pipeline

A block-level depiction of how the **triangle inequality** controls the DTFT bound:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input samples x of n] --> B[Multiply by e to the negative j omega n]
    B --> C[Sum over all n from negative infinity to positive infinity]
    C --> D{Is sum of absolute value of x of n finite?}
    D -- YES --> E[DTFT is bounded: absolute value of X of e to the j omega less than or equal to sum of absolute value of x of n]
    D -- NO --> F[DTFT unbounded: no convergence]
    E --> G[Continuous spectrum ready for system analysis]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Follow the arrows from input sequence to the final spectrum. Each diamond (`{}`) is a decision gate. Each rectangle (`[]`) is a processing stage. The flow ends with either a valid continuous spectrum or a flagged non-existence — this is the KTU-recommended way to answer "Does this sequence have a DTFT?"
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the condition for the existence of the Discrete Time Fourier Transform of a sequence $x[n]$. Justify your answer using the triangle inequality.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The DTFT of $x[n]$ exists if $x[n]$ is **absolutely summable**, i.e.,

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

**Justification (using triangle inequality):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} \\
\Big\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \Big\vert &\le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert \\
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot 1 < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = 1$, the magnitude of $X(e^{j\omega})$ is bounded by a finite number, ensuring the DTFT converges uniformly to a continuous function. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Explain why the sequence $x[n] = 1$ (constant for all $n$) does not have a DTFT in the conventional sense.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The DTFT requires absolute summability:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert 1 \vert = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} 1 = \infty$$

Since the sum is **infinite**, the absolute summability condition is violated. Therefore, $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum e^{-j\omega n}$ does not converge to a finite function of $\omega$ in the ordinary sense. It can, however, be represented in the **generalized sense** as a Dirac impulse train:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$$

**(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Derive the DTFT of the sequence $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$, and clearly state the **convergence condition** on $a$. Show all steps using the geometric series formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the sequence $y[n] = n \cdot a^{n} u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$, determine whether its DTFT converges. Justify your answer using the absolute summability test, and compute the DTFT using the differentiation-in-frequency property. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the DTFT definition. Since $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^{n}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - r}$, which converges if and only if $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}, \quad \text{for } \vert a e^{-j\omega} \vert < 1$$

**Step 3:** Since $\vert e^{-j\omega} \vert = 1$, the condition becomes $\vert a \vert < 1$.

**Convergence Condition:** $\boxed{\vert a \vert < 1}$ — sequence is **absolutely summable** with $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert a \vert^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - \vert a \vert}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convergence Test:**
We need to check $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert n a^{n} \vert$. Using the ratio test:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\vert (n+1) a^{n+1} \vert}{\vert n a^{n} \vert} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n+1}{n} \cdot \vert a \vert = \vert a \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L = \vert a \vert < 1$, the series converges → DTFT exists.

**Step 2 — Differentiation Property:**
Recall that if $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j\omega})$, then $n\,x[n] \leftrightarrow j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Step 3:** From part (a), $X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$. Differentiate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dX}{d\omega} &= \frac{d}{d\omega}(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^{-1} \\
&= -1 \cdot (1 - a e^{-j\omega})^{-2} \cdot (-a)(-j)e^{-j\omega} \\
&= \frac{-j a e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $j$ to get the DTFT of $n a^{n} u[n]$:

$$\boxed{Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{a e^{-j\omega}}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^{2}}, \quad \text{for } \vert a \vert < 1}$$

**(7 Marks)**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Define the Discrete Time Fourier Transform. Explain the **Dirichlet conditions** for its convergence. Show that absolute summability is a sufficient condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the DTFT of a finite-duration sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ where $x[0] = 1$, $x[1] = 2$, $x[2] = 3$. Verify that the convergence condition is satisfied and plot the magnitude response for three values $\omega = 0, \pi/2, \pi$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The DTFT of $x[n]$ is:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Dirichlet Conditions (2 Marks):** The DTFT converges if:
1. $x[n]$ is absolutely summable: $\sum \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$.
2. $x[n]$ has bounded variation on every finite interval.
3. $x[n]$ has only finitely many discontinuities in any finite interval.

**Sufficiency Proof (3 Marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, by the triangle inequality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \cdot \vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $X(e^{j\omega})$ is bounded for all $\omega$, and the series converges uniformly (by the Weierstrass M-test). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convergence Check (2 Marks):**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{2} \vert x[n] \vert = \vert 1 \vert + \vert 2 \vert + \vert 3 \vert = 6 < \infty$$

Absolute summability is satisfied. DTFT converges.

**Step 2 — Compute DTFT (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
