X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=0}^{2} x[n] e^{-j\omega n} = 1 + 2 e^{-j\omega} + 3 e^{-j2\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at Specific Frequencies (2 Marks):**

At $\omega = 0$:

$$X(e^{j0}) = 1 + 2(1) + 3(1) = 6$$

At $\omega = \pi/2$:

$$X(e^{j\pi/2}) = 1 + 2 e^{-j\pi/2} + 3 e^{-j\pi} = 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) = -2 - 2j$$

Magnitude: $\vert -2 - 2j \vert = \sqrt{4 + 4} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$.

At $\omega = \pi$:

$$X(e^{j\pi}) = 1 + 2 e^{-j\pi} + 3 e^{-j2\pi} = 1 + 2(-1) + 3(1) = 1 - 2 + 3 = 2$$

| $\omega$ | $X(e^{j\omega})$ | $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| $0$ | $6$ | $6$ |
| $\pi/2$ | $-2 - 2j$ | $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$ |
| $\pi$ | $2$ | $2$ |

**(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — DTFT Convergence Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the convergence condition explicitly.** Even if you derive the DTFT correctly, you **lose 2 marks** if you don't write "this is valid only when $\vert a \vert < 1$" or the absolute summability condition.
> 2. **Ignoring $\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = 1$.** Many students skip this step, which is the *key* reason why absolute summability is sufficient. Always show this.
> 3. **Confusing DTFT with DFT.** DTFT is *continuous* in $\omega$; DFT is *discrete* samples of DTFT. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Not specifying the principal interval.** The DTFT is periodic with period $2\pi$, so always write $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
> 5. **Forgetting the $1/(2\pi)$ in IDTFT.** The synthesis equation has the $1/(2\pi)$ factor; the analysis equation does not. Mixing these is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DTFT** is the frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic, possibly infinite-length sequence: $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$.
- **Convergence Condition (Sufficient):** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ (absolute summability).
- **Weaker Condition (Square Summable / Finite Energy):** $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$ → DTFT exists in $L^2$ (mean-square) sense.
- **Triangle Inequality Boundedness:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert \le \sum \vert x[n] \vert$ — the proof backbone of the sufficient condition.
- **Dirichlet Conditions:** absolute summability + bounded variation + finite discontinuities.
- **Periodicity:** $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **always periodic** with period $2\pi$.
- **Z-Transform Link:** DTFT exists if the ROC of the Z-Transform **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Canonical Convergent Example:** $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ converges iff $\vert a \vert < 1$; result $X = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$.
- **Canonical Divergent Example:** $x[n] = 1$ has $\sum \vert x[n] \vert = \infty$, so no ordinary DTFT.
- **Differentiation Property:** $n\,x[n] \leftrightarrow j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$ — useful for ramp-type signals.
- **Finite-duration sequences always converge** (they have a finite sum of $\vert x[n] \vert$).
- **KTU Golden Rule:** Always state the convergence condition alongside the DTFT expression — even for "easy" sequences — to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DTFT of Standard Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DTFT of Standard Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is DTFT? (Recap before Standard Signals)

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is a frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic signal. It transforms a discrete sequence $x(n)$ into a **continuous and periodic** function of frequency $\omega$.

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DTFT of an absolutely summable sequence $x(n)$ is defined as:
> $$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$
> and the inverse DTFT (IDTFT) is:
> $$x(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

**Properties that matter for standard signals:**
- $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **always periodic** with period $2\pi$.
- $X(e^{j\omega})$ is **always continuous** in $\omega$.
- The fundamental period is $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ (one period).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have a **musical chord** played on a piano. Your ear cannot easily tell which individual keys (frequencies) are pressed together. The DTFT acts like a **sophisticated spectrum analyzer** — it takes a complex discrete-time signal (the chord) and tells you **exactly how much of each frequency** is present in it.

For **standard signals**, we are essentially pre-computing the "spectrum" of the most fundamental building-block signals (impulse, step, sinusoid, exponential), so that any arbitrary signal built from these blocks can be analyzed using the **linearity** and **time-shift** properties of DTFT.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Mastering the DTFT of these standard signals is non-negotiable because Module 3 question papers heavily test the ability to combine these transforms using properties (linearity, time-shifting, modulation, convolution).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **Nyquist Normalized Frequency Range:** $-\pi \leq \omega \leq \pi$ (radians/sample)
- **Sampling Frequency Equivalent:** $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ (normalized)
- **Convergence Constant:** $\vert a \vert < 1$ for causal exponentials
- **DC Value Identifier:** $X(e^{j0}) = \sum x(n)$

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Compute DTFT of Standard Signals?

Standard signals form the **orthogonal basis** of the discrete-time signal space. Any arbitrary signal $x(n)$ can be expressed as a linear combination of these standard signals. Knowing their transforms allows us to:

1. Solve LTI system analysis problems via convolution in frequency.
2. Apply modulation and time-shift theorems easily.
3. Tackle filter design questions that appear in later modules.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Each Standard Signal

#### **A. Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(n)$**

- $\delta(n) = 1$ at $n=0$, and $0$ elsewhere.
- DTFT: only $n=0$ contributes.
- Result is **flat spectrum** = 1 (white spectrum).

#### **B. Unit Step Signal $u(n)$**

- Sum of $e^{j\omega n}$ from $n=0$ to $\infty$ does not converge in the ordinary sense.
- It contains a **DC component** (an impulse at $\omega = 0, \pm 2\pi, ...$) plus a **rational part**.
- Must use the identity involving $\delta(\omega)$.

#### **C. Causal Exponential $a^n u(n)$, $\vert a \vert < 1$**

- Geometric series in complex form.
- Converges only if $\vert a \vert < 1$.
- The pole of $X(e^{j\omega})$ lies at $z = a$ on the real axis inside the unit circle.

#### **D. Rectangular Pulse $x(n) = 1$ for $-N_1 \leq n \leq N_2$**

- Finite duration, so always absolutely summable.
- DTFT has the **Dirichlet (aliased sinc)** form: $\frac{\sin(\omega(M+1)/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$.

#### **E. Complex Exponential $e^{j\omega_0 n}$**

- Spectral density is concentrated entirely at $\omega = \omega_0 + 2\pi k$.
- DTFT is a **periodic impulse train** in $\omega$.

#### **F. Sinusoidal Signals $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ and $\sin(\omega_0 n)$**

- Obtained by linear combination of two complex exponentials.
- Spectrum consists of **two symmetric impulses** in the $\omega$-domain.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized verbatim** — these transforms appear in nearly every KTU university exam for this module.

| **Signal $x(n)$** | **DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$** | **Key Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| $\delta(n)$ | $1$ | Always |
| $1$ (DC, all $n$) | $2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ | Periodic in $\omega$ |
| $u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ | Contains impulse at $\omega = 0$ |
| $a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ | $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| $-a^n u(-n-1)$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ | $\vert a \vert > 1$ |
| $(n+1)a^n u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1 - a e^{-j\omega})^2}$ | $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| Rect. pulse $1, \;\vert n \vert \leq N$ | $\dfrac{\sin\left(\omega(N + \frac{1}{2})\right)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$ | Finite duration |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | $2\pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)$ | Line spectrum |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\pi \sum_{k} \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k) \right]$ | Even spectrum |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $-j\pi \sum_{k} \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) - \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k) \right]$ | Odd (imaginary) spectrum |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u(n)$ | $\dfrac{1 - e^{-j\omega} \cos(\omega_0)}{1 - 2e^{-j\omega}\cos(\omega_0) + e^{-j2\omega}}$ | Causal cosine |

> **Note on Table:** No `|` symbol used inside the table cells — `vert a vert` notation is used to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** The DTFT of $\delta(n)$ and $u(n)$ are the foundations of FIR/IIR filter frequency responses.
- **Audio Signal Processing:** The line spectrum of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ models pure tones; their transforms predict how speakers and equalizers behave.
- **Communication Systems:** The complex exponential $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ is the carrier signal in digital modulation (PSK, QAM, OFDM).
- **Image Processing:** Rectangular pulse DTFT directly models the blurring caused by finite-size image windows.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: DTFT of Unit Impulse $\delta(n)$

By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(n) e^{-j\omega n} \\
&= \delta(0) \cdot e^{-j\omega \cdot 0} + \sum_{n \neq 0} \delta(n) e^{-j\omega n} \\
&= 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = 1}$ for all $\omega$.

**Logic:** Since $\delta(n)$ is non-zero only at $n=0$, only the $n=0$ term survives in the summation, and $e^0 = 1$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: DTFT of Causal Exponential $a^n u(n)$, $\vert a \vert < 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u(n) e^{-j\omega n} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\omega n} \quad \text{(since } u(n) = 0 \text{ for } n < 0\text{)} \\
&= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a geometric series. For convergence we need $\vert a e^{-j\omega} \vert = \vert a \vert < 1$. The sum becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} \quad \text{(using sum of infinite GP: } S = \frac{1}{1-r} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}}$, valid for $\vert a \vert < 1$.

**Verification at $a=0$:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = 1$ which matches DTFT of $\delta(n)$ (since $0^n u(n) = \delta(n)$). ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: DTFT of Complex Exponential $e^{j\omega_0 n}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n} e^{-j\omega n} \\
&= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0) n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the fundamental identity of DTFT:

$$
\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0) n} = 2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = 2\pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)}$

This is a **train of impulses** in the frequency domain, located at $\omega = \omega_0 \pm 2\pi, \omega_0 \pm 4\pi, \ldots$, reflecting the $2\pi$-periodicity of DTFT.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: DTFT of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$

Using Euler's identity: $\cos(\omega_0 n) = \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n}\right)$.

By linearity of DTFT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \frac{1}{2}\left[2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + 2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k)\right] \\
&= \pi \sum_k \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = \pi \sum_k \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k) \right]}$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: DTFT of Rectangular Pulse

Define $x(n) = 1$ for $-N_1 \leq n \leq N_2$ and $0$ otherwise. Let $M = N_1 + N_2 + 1$ (total length).

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=-N_1}^{N_2} e^{-j\omega n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply and divide by $e^{j\omega/2}$ to symmetrize:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= e^{j\omega(N_1 - N_2)/2} \sum_{n=-N_1}^{N_2} e^{-j\omega(n - (N_1 - N_2)/2)} \\
&= e^{j\omega(N_1 - N_2)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin\left(\omega(N_1 + N_2 + 1)/2\right)}{\sin(\omega/2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the symmetric case $N_1 = N_2 = N$ (so the pulse is even), the linear phase factor becomes $1$:

$$
\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin\left(\omega(N + \frac{1}{2})\right)}{\sin(\omega/2)} = \frac{\sin((2N+1)\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}}
$$

**Special check at $\omega = 0$:** Using L'Hôpital's rule, $X(e^{j0}) = 2N + 1$, which equals the sum of the pulse samples. ✓

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: DTFT of Unit Step $u(n)$ (Most Tested)

For $u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{-j\omega n} \\
&= 1 + e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

The series **does not converge** in the ordinary sense. Decompose using:

$$
u(n) = \lim_{r \to 1^-} r^n u(n) + \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{sgn}(n)\right]
$$

Applying DTFT (using limit $r \to 1^-$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(e^{j\omega}) &= \lim_{r \to 1^-} \frac{1}{1 - r e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)
\end{aligned}
$$

The limit part can be rationalized:

$$
\lim_{r \to 1^-} \frac{1}{1 - r e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}
$$

**Final Result:** 

$$
\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)}
$$

The $\frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$ term has a **pole at $\omega = 0$** (and $2\pi k$). The impulse term cancels out the singular behavior of the rational part.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Tree of Standard DTFT Pairs

This block-level schematic shows the **logical organization** of standard signals and the nature of their DTFT spectra — a frequent question in KTU vivas.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Standard Discrete Signals x of n] --> B[Energy Signals]
    A --> C[Power Signals]
    A --> D[Neither Finite nor Absolutely Summable]

    B --> B1[Impulse delta n]
    B --> B2[Finite Rectangular Pulse]
    B --> B3[Causal Exponential a to the n u of n, vert a vert less than 1]

    C --> C1[DC Signal x of n equals 1]
    C --> C2[Complex Exponential e to the j omega0 n]
    C --> C3[Cosine and Sine sinusoids]

    D --> D1[Unit Step u of n]

    B1 --> R1[DTFT equals 1, flat spectrum]
    B2 --> R2[Dirichlet form, sin x over sin]
    B3 --> R3[Rational form 1 over 1 minus a e to the minus j omega]

    C1 --> R4[Periodic Impulse Train in omega]
    C2 --> R5[Impulse Train at omega0 plus 2 pi k]
    C3 --> R6[Two symmetric impulses in omega]

    D1 --> R7[Rational part plus impulse at omega equals 0]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Strategy for Solving DTFT Standard Signal Problems

A typical KTU problem asks: "Find the DTFT of $x(n) = a^{\vert n \vert}$" or "Determine and plot the magnitude spectrum." Use this decision flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Identify x of n] --> S1{Is x of n finite duration?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Use direct summation: sum of e to the minus j omega n]
    S1 -- No --> S3{Does x of n have a closed form expression?}

    S3 -- Causal exponential a to the n u of n --> S4[Apply Geometric Series, result is 1 over 1 minus a e to the minus j omega]
    S3 -- Two sided exponential a to the absolute n --> S5[Split into causal plus anti-causal, use linearity]
    S3 -- Sinusoid cos or sin --> S6[Use Euler identity, express as sum of complex exponentials]
    S3 -- Unit Step u of n --> S7[Apply limit of r to the n u of n as r approaches 1]

    S2 --> S8[Simplify using sin over sin form]
    S4 --> S9[Check convergence: vert a vert less than 1]
    S5 --> S9
    S6 --> S10[Apply DTFT of e to the j omega0 n]
    S7 --> S11[Add impulse train at multiples of 2 pi]

    S8 --> S12[Final Answer X of e to the j omega]
    S9 --> S12
    S10 --> S12
    S11 --> S12
```

### 4.3 Spectrum Magnitude Plots (Conceptual ASCII Schematic)

For $x(n) = a^n u(n)$ with $a = 0.5$ (causal exponential), the magnitude spectrum is:

```
|X(e^jω)|
  ↑
 1|    ╭─────────────╮
  |   ╱               ╲
0.5|  ╱                 ╲
  | ╱                   ╲___
  |╱                        ‾‾‾─────
  +──────────────────────────────→ ω
  -π   -π/2    0    π/2    π
       (peak at ω=0, smooth decay)
```

For $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ (line spectrum):

```
|X(e^jω)|
  ↑
π |        │                │
  |        │                │
 0|────────┼────────────────┼────────→ ω
  -π      -ω₀        0       ω₀      π
         (impulses at ±ω₀)
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> **Q1.** State and explain the Dirichlet condition for the existence of DTFT of a discrete-time signal. Mention the convergence criteria.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The DTFT of a sequence $x(n)$ exists if it is **absolutely summable**: $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert < \infty$. (1 mark)
- Equivalently, $x(n)$ must have **finite energy**: $E = \sum \vert x(n) \vert^2 < \infty$. (1 mark)
- A sufficient but not necessary condition is absolute summability; some signals (like $u(n)$) require generalized DTFT with impulses. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2.** Find the DTFT of the unit impulse sequence $\delta(n)$. Mention any two properties of the result.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum \delta(n) e^{-j\omega n} = 1$ (since only $n=0$ term survives). (2 marks)
- Properties: (i) Magnitude is constant = 1 for all $\omega$ (flat spectrum); (ii) Phase is zero; (iii) Acts as identity for LTI systems (impulse response = system function). (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module 3 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
>
> **QUESTION A (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Derive the DTFT of the causal exponential signal $x(n) = a^n u(n)$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$. Sketch its magnitude response for $a = 0.5$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Using Euler's formula, determine the DTFT of $x(n) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$. Hence, sketch the spectrum indicating impulse locations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — DTFT of $a^n u(n)$:** (7 marks)

By definition:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a e^{-j\omega})^n$$

This is a geometric series with ratio $r = a e^{-j\omega}$. Since $\vert a \vert < 1$, $\vert r \vert < 1$ for all $\omega$, so the series converges. Using the sum formula $S = \frac{1}{1-r}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos(\omega) + a^2}}$

For $a = 0.5$: Peak magnitude at $\omega = 0$ is $\frac{1}{1-0.5} = 2$. As $\omega \to \pi$, magnitude $\to \frac{1}{1+0.5} = 0.667$. The plot is a smooth low-pass shape.

**Part (b) — DTFT of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$:** (7 marks)

$$\cos(\omega_0 n) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n}\right]$$

By linearity:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2}\left[2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + 2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k)\right]$$

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \pi \sum_k \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k) \right]$$

**Spectrum:** Two impulses of strength $\pi$ located at $\omega = \pm \omega_0$ in the principal period $[-\pi, \pi]$, repeated every $2\pi$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Alternative Choice]**
>
> **QUESTION B (14 Marks):**
>
> **(a)** Determine the DTFT of a rectangular pulse $x(n) = 1$ for $-2 \leq n \leq 2$ and zero otherwise. Plot its magnitude spectrum. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the DTFT of the unit step signal $u(n)$. Explain why a direct summation is not possible. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Rectangular Pulse:** (7 marks)

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-2}^{2} e^{-j\omega n} = e^{j2\omega} + e^{j\omega} + 1 + e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega}$$

Group symmetric terms:
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 2\cos(\omega) + 2\cos(2\omega)$$

Using Dirichlet form ($N_1 = N_2 = 2$):
$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sin(5\omega/2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

At $\omega = 0$: $X(e^{j0}) = 5$ (sum of 5 ones). Magnitude shows main lobe of width $2\pi/5$ with sidelobes.

**Part (b) — Unit Step DTFT:** (7 marks)

Direct summation $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{-j\omega n}$ does not converge because $\vert e^{-j\omega n} \vert = 1$ for all $n$ — the partial sums oscillate, never approaching a limit. Hence the **generalized DTFT** must be used:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$$

The $\delta$-train term handles the singular behavior at $\omega = 0$ (and $\pm 2\pi k$). The remaining term can be rewritten as:
$$\frac{1}{1 - e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{j}{2}\cot\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in DTFT standard signal problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the impulse train in $u(n)$ DTFT** — writing only $\frac{1}{1-e^{-j\omega}}$ loses **3 of 7 marks** in part (b). Examiners specifically look for the $\pi \sum \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$ term.
>
> 2. **Missing convergence condition** — for $a^n u(n)$, you MUST state $\vert a \vert < 1$. No mention = **-1 mark** penalty even if the final formula is correct.
>
> 3. **Sign error in sine DTFT** — $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ gives $-j\pi[\delta(\omega - \omega_0) - \delta(\omega + \omega_0)]$, **not** the cosine form. Mixing them up is a classic trap.
>
> 4. **Ignoring $2\pi$-periodicity in line spectra** — students often plot only one period of impulses. Examiners expect you to mention the periodic repetition $\omega_0 + 2\pi k$.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to apply L'Hôpital at $\omega = 0$** for the rectangular pulse DTFT — failing to show $X(e^{j0}) = 2N+1$ is checked and loses a mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DTFT of $\delta(n)$:** Always $= 1$. Flat spectrum. Acts as identity in convolution.
- **DTFT of $u(n)$:** $\frac{1}{1-e^{-j\omega}} + \pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - 2\pi k)$. The **impulse train is mandatory**.
- **DTFT of $a^n u(n)$:** $\frac{1}{1-ae^{-j\omega}}$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$. Pole at $z = a$ on the z-plane.
- **DTFT of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$:** Pair of impulses at $\pm \omega_0$ with strength $\pi$ each, repeated every $2\pi$.
- **DTFT of $\sin(\omega_0 n)$:** Imaginary spectrum: $-j\pi[\delta(\omega-\omega_0) - \delta(\omega+\omega_0)]$.
- **DTFT of $e^{j\omega_0 n}$:** $2\pi$ impulse train at $\omega_0 + 2\pi k$.
- **Rectangular pulse DTFT:** $\frac{\sin(\omega(N+1/2))}{\sin(\omega/2)}$ — Dirichlet form, value at $\omega=0$ is $2N+1$.
- **$X(e^{j\omega})$ is ALWAYS:** continuous in $\omega$ AND periodic with period $2\pi$.
- **DC value trick:** $X(e^{j0}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)$ (the area under the time-domain signal).
- **Use Euler's identity** to break sinusoids into complex exponentials before applying DTFT.
- **Use $r^n u(n)$ with $r \to 1^-$ trick** to handle $u(n)$ rigorously.
- **Always verify the pole-zero condition** $\vert a \vert < 1$ for any exponential signal.
- **Line spectra are NOT functions** — they are generalized functions (impulses); never plot them as smooth curves.
- **Symmetry of $X(e^{j\omega})$ for real $x(n)$:** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ is even, $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ is odd.
- **The Dirichlet form is the most-tested formula** in the KTU Module 3 paper — memorize it.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of DTFT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of DTFT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DTFT

The **Discrete Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is a frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic sequence $x[n]$. Unlike the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), the DTFT produces a **continuous and periodic** function of the angular frequency $\omega$.

The transform pair is defined as:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

where $X(e^{j\omega})$ is the DTFT of the sequence $x[n]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DTFT is **always periodic** with period $2\pi$ in $\omega$. This is a defining trait that distinguishes it from the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT). The variable $X(e^{j\omega})$ is written with the argument $e^{j\omega}$ to explicitly remind us that we are dealing with the **z-transform evaluated on the unit circle** ($z = e^{j\omega}$).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you have a **digital audio song** (the discrete sequence $x[n]$) — a long list of numbers representing air pressure samples over time. The DTFT acts like a **spectral prism** that separates this complex song into its **continuous rainbow of frequency components**.

Unlike a finite DFT prism that gives you a discrete list of color buckets, the DTFT gives you an **infinitely fine, smooth color spectrum** that repeats every time you spin it through one full revolution ($2\pi$ radians). This periodicity comes from the fact that digital systems cannot distinguish between a frequency $\omega$ and $\omega + 2\pi$ — they "alias" the same way in the digital world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why periodicity matters in engineering:** In digital filter design (FIR/IIR), we exploit the $2\pi$ periodicity of the DTFT to design frequency responses that are inherently periodic. The unit circle $|z|=1$ in the z-plane directly corresponds to one period of the DTFT.

## 1.3 Existence Condition

The DTFT of $x[n]$ exists (converges) if and only if the sequence is **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$$

This is the **Dirichlet condition** for discrete-time signals. A weaker sufficient condition is **square-summability** (finite energy):

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} < \infty$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Transform Pair (Reference)

The DTFT operates on a sequence $x[n]$ and produces a **continuous, periodic** spectrum $X(e^{j\omega})$:

**Analysis Equation (Forward DTFT):**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Synthesis Equation (Inverse DTFT):**

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> The integration interval is **any $2\pi$ length** because of periodicity. The interval $(-\pi, \pi]$ (or equivalently $[0, 2\pi)$) is the **principal period** (fundamental range) where the spectrum is unique.

## 2.2 Proof of Periodicity

To prove $X(e^{j\omega})$ is periodic with period $2\pi$, substitute $\omega + 2\pi$:

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j(\omega + 2\pi)n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}\, e^{-j2\pi n}$$

Since $e^{-j2\pi n} = \cos(2\pi n) - j\sin(2\pi n) = 1$ for all integer $n$:

$$X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega}) \quad \checkmark$$

## 2.3 Comprehensive KTU Formula Sheet

The following table lists **all 13+ properties** that are examinable in KTU 2024 ESE and Internal assessments. Every property is given as: **operation in time** $\longleftrightarrow$ **operation in frequency**.

| # | Property | Time Domain (Sequence) | Frequency Domain (DTFT) | Exam Weight |
|---|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Periodicity** | — | $X(e^{j(\omega+2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | High |
| 2 | **Linearity** | $a\,x_1[n] + b\,x_2[n]$ | $a\,X_1(e^{j\omega}) + b\,X_2(e^{j\omega})$ | Very High |
| 3 | **Time Shifting** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$ | Very High |
| 4 | **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x[n]$ | $X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$ | Very High |
| 5 | **Time Reversal** | $x[-n]$ | $X(e^{-j\omega})$ | High |
| 6 | **Time Expansion** | $x[k\,n]$ (zero-stuffing) | $X(e^{j\omega k})$ | Medium |
| 7 | **Differentiation in Freq.** | $n\,x[n]$ | $j\, \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$ | High |
| 8 | **Convolution in Time** | $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | $X_1(e^{j\omega})\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$ | Very High |
| 9 | **Multiplication in Time** | $x_1[n]\, x_2[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_1(e^{j\theta})\, X_2(e^{j(\omega-\theta)})\, d\theta$ | Very High |
| 10 | **Modulation / Windowing** | $x[n]\, \cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\frac{1}{2}\left[ X(e^{j(\omega-\omega_0)}) + X(e^{j(\omega+\omega_0)}) \right]$ | High |
| 11 | **Conjugation** | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ | Medium |
| 12 | **Conjugate Symmetry** (real $x[n]$) | $x[n]$ real | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$ | Very High |
| 13 | **Parseval's Theorem** | $\sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}\, d\omega$ | Very High |
| 14 | **Duality** | $X(e^{j\omega}) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2\pi}\, x[-n]$ (with $\omega \leftrightarrow n$) | — | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** The **negative sign convention** in the time-shifting property is the single most common source of mark loss. Always verify: $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$. A **positive shift** in time (right shift) introduces a **negative** exponential in frequency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Time Shifting** $\rightarrow$ Used in **beamforming**, **echo cancellation**, and **equalization** in digital communication systems.
- **Convolution Property** $\rightarrow$ Foundation of **LTI system analysis**: $Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega})\, X(e^{j\omega})$, where $H(e^{j\omega})$ is the frequency response of the system.
- **Multiplication Property** $\rightarrow$ Models **windowing effects** in spectrum analyzers and **amplitude modulation (AM)** in transmitters.
- **Parseval's Theorem** $\rightarrow$ Used in **noise power calculations** in **AWGN channels** of 5G/6G communication systems.
- **Modulation Property** $\rightarrow$ Underpins **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** in Wi-Fi and LTE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$. By definition of the DTFT:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} y[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Use the change of variables $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$ and $d(\text{sum index}) = d(\text{sum index})$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega (m + n_0)}$$

**Step 3:** Split the exponential using $e^{a+b} = e^{a} e^{b}$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \left( \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m} \right) e^{-j\omega n_0}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the summation as the DTFT of $x[n]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{-j\omega n_0} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ is a **pure phase term** with unit magnitude $\vert e^{-j\omega n_0} \vert = 1$. Therefore, time shifting **does not alter the magnitude spectrum** $\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ — it only rotates the **phase spectrum** $\angle X(e^{j\omega})$ by $-\omega n_0$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Convolution in Time (Most Important)

**Statement:** If $x_1[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X_1(e^{j\omega})$ and $x_2[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X_2(e^{j\omega})$, then $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X_1(e^{j\omega})\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$.

**Proof:**

Let $y[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k]\, x_2[n-k]$.

**Step 1:** Take the DTFT of both sides:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k]\, x_2[n-k] \right) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Interchange the order of summation (valid under absolute convergence):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n-k]\, e^{-j\omega n} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Recognize the inner sum using the **time-shifting property** (with shift amount $k$):

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n-k]\, e^{-j\omega n} = e^{-j\omega k}\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $X_2(e^{j\omega})$ (does not depend on $k$):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X_2(e^{j\omega}) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k]\, e^{-j\omega k}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the remaining sum as the DTFT of $x_1[n]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X_1(e^{j\omega})\, X_2(e^{j\omega}) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** This is the cornerstone of LTI system analysis. If a signal $x[n]$ is passed through an LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$, the output is $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$. In the frequency domain, this is **simple multiplication**: $Y(e^{j\omega}) = H(e^{j\omega})\, X(e^{j\omega})$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Multiplication in Time (Duality with Convolution)

**Statement:** $x_1[n]\, x_2[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_1(e^{j\theta})\, X_2(e^{j(\omega-\theta)})\, d\theta$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Use the synthesis equation to express $x_2[n]$ in terms of its DTFT:

$$x_2[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_2(e^{j\phi})\, e^{j\phi n}\, d\phi$$

**Step 2:** Multiply with $x_1[n]$:

$$x_1[n]\, x_2[n] = x_1[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_2(e^{j\phi})\, e^{j\phi n}\, d\phi$$

**Step 3:** Take the DTFT of both sides:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( x_1[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_2(e^{j\phi})\, e^{j\phi n}\, d\phi \right) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange (interchange sum and integral):

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_2(e^{j\phi}) \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[n]\, e^{-j(\omega - \phi)n} \right) d\phi$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the inner sum as $X_1(e^{j(\omega - \phi)})$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_2(e^{j\phi})\, X_1(e^{j(\omega - \phi)})\, d\phi$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\phi = \theta$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X_1(e^{j(\omega - \theta)})\, X_2(e^{j\theta})\, d\theta \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This integral is called the **periodic convolution** (or **circular convolution over one period**). It is a direct consequence of the **duality** between time and frequency domains: convolution in one domain equals multiplication in the other, scaled by $2\pi$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

**Statement:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}\, d\omega$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write the energy in the time domain:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, x^*[n]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the synthesis equation for $x^*[n]$ (using the **conjugation property** $x^*[n] \xleftrightarrow{} X^*(e^{-j\omega})$):

$$x^*[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X^*(e^{j\phi})\, e^{-j\phi n}\, d\phi$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the energy expression:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X^*(e^{j\phi})\, e^{-j\phi n}\, d\phi$$

**Step 4:** Interchange sum and integral:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X^*(e^{j\phi}) \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\phi n} \right) d\phi$$

**Step 5:** Identify the inner sum as $X(e^{j\phi})$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} X^*(e^{j\phi})\, X(e^{j\phi})\, d\phi = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{2\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}\, d\omega \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quantity $\frac{\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}}{2\pi}$ is the **energy spectral density (ESD)** of the signal. Parseval's theorem is a statement of **energy conservation** between the time and frequency domains — total energy is invariant under the DTFT.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Modulation Property

**Problem:** Find the DTFT of $x[n] = \cos(\omega_0 n)$ for $0 < \omega_0 < \pi$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the inverse DTFT of a shifted delta train:

$$\delta[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \delta[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0}$$

**Step 2:** We know that the DTFT of a periodic impulse train in time is a periodic impulse train in frequency. Specifically:

$$\frac{1}{2\pi}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega k} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi m)$$

Hence, the DTFT pair:

$$1 \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\omega - 2\pi m)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **frequency-shifting property** to shift this impulse train by $\pm \omega_0$:

$$e^{j\omega_0 n} \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \sum_{m} \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

$$e^{-j\omega_0 n} \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} 2\pi \sum_{m} \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi m)$$

**Step 4:** Apply **linearity** with $x[n] = \frac{1}{2}(e^{j\omega_0 n} + e^{-j\omega_0 n})$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \pi \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi m) + \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi m) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result shows that a discrete-time cosine contains **two impulses per period**, located at $\pm \omega_0$ (modulo $2\pi$). This is the discrete-time analog of Euler's identity in the frequency domain.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Property Mapping Diagram

The following diagram shows how each **time-domain operation** is mapped to a **frequency-domain operation** by the DTFT.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Discrete Time Signal x of n] --> B{DTFT Operator}
    B --> C[Continuous Periodic Spectrum X of e to j omega]
    
    A1[Linear Combination a x1 plus b x2] --> B1[Linearity Property]
    A2[Time Shift x of n minus n0] --> B2[Multiply by e to minus j omega n0]
    A3[Modulation e to j omega0 n times x of n] --> B3[Frequency Shift by omega0]
    A4[Time Reversal x of minus n] --> B4[Spectrum Reflection]
    A5[Convolution x1 star x2] --> B5[Multiplication X1 times X2]
    A6[Multiplication x1 times x2] --> B6[Periodic Convolution over 2 pi]
    A7[Differentiation n times x of n] --> B7[j d by d omega of X]
    A8[Conjugation x star of n] --> B8[Conjugate Reflected X star of e to minus j omega]
    A9[Parseval Energy] --> B9[Integral of |X| squared d omega over 2 pi]
    
    B1 --> C
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
    B7 --> C
    B8 --> C
    B9 --> C
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 LTI System Analysis Block Diagram (Application of Convolution Property)

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Input x of n] --> MUL{X Multiplication in Frequency Domain}
    H[System H of e to j omega] --> MUL
    MUL --> OUTPUT[Output Y of e to j omega]
    YIDFT[Inverse DTFT] --> OUTPUT2[Output y of n in Time Domain]
    
    A[Input x of n] --> CONV{Convolution in Time Domain star}
    B[Impulse Response h of n] --> CONV
    CONV --> Y[Output y of n]
    
    style MUL fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CONV fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Y fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTPUT2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Energy Conservation Flow (Parseval's Theorem)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TS[Time Domain Energy Sum over n of |x of n| squared] --> EQ{Equality by Parseval}
    FS[Frequency Domain Energy 1 over 2 pi times Integral of |X of e to j omega| squared d omega]
    EQ --> TS
    EQ --> FS
    
    X1[Compute Time Energy] --> SUM[Sum over all n]
    X2[Compute DTFT] --> MAG[Square Magnitude Response]
    MAG --> INT[Integrate over one period 2 pi]
    INT --> DIV[Divide by 2 pi]
    
    style TS fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FS fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EQ fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.4 Modulation Property Spectral Shift Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Spectrum X of e to j omega] -->|Multiply by cos omega0 n| B[Shifted Spectrum]
    B --> B1[Copy 1 at omega equals plus omega0]
    B --> B2[Copy 2 at omega equals minus omega0]
    B1 --> C[Superimposed Result in One 2 pi Period]
    B2 --> C
    
    style A fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the time-shifting property of DTFT.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$.

**Proof:**

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

Substituting $m = n - n_0$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega (m + n_0)} = e^{-j\omega n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, e^{-j\omega m} = e^{-j\omega n_0}\, X(e^{j\omega})$$

Since $\vert e^{-j\omega n_0} \vert = 1$, the **magnitude spectrum is unchanged** by time shifting; only the **phase spectrum** is altered by $-\omega n_0$ radians.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is Parseval's theorem for DTFT? State its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:**

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}\, d\omega$$

**Significance:**
1. The total energy of the signal computed in the time domain equals the total energy computed in the frequency domain.
2. It defines the **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** as $S_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2}}{2\pi}$.
3. It is used to compute the **output signal power** of an LTI system with frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ driven by an input signal: $P_y = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2} S_{xx}(\omega)\, d\omega$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Convolution Theorem

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** State and prove the convolution property of DTFT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given $x_1[n] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ and $x_2[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ (both causal, starting at $n=0$), find the DTFT of the convolution $y[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ using the convolution property. Also verify by direct convolution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof:** See Section 3.2 above for the complete derivation.

**Statement:** $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X_1(e^{j\omega})\, X_2(e^{j\omega})$.

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute DTFT of $x_1[n] = \delta[n] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2]$:

$$X_1(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega} = e^{-j\omega} \left( e^{j\omega} + 1 + e^{-j\omega} \right) = e^{-j\omega} \left( 1 + 2\cos\omega \right)$$

**Step 2:** Compute DTFT of $x_2[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2]$:

$$X_2(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 2e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega} = e^{-j\omega} \left( e^{j\omega} + 2 + e^{-j\omega} \right) = e^{-j\omega} \left( 2 + 2\cos\omega \right)$$

**Step 3:** Apply convolution property:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X_1(e^{j\omega})\, X_2(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega} \left( 1 + 2\cos\omega \right) \left( 2 + 2\cos\omega \right)$$

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = 2 e^{-j2\omega} \left( 1 + 2\cos\omega \right) \left( 1 + \cos\omega \right)$$

**Step 4:** Verification by direct convolution:

$$y[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{2} x_1[k]\, x_2[n-k]$$

| $n$ | Calculation | $y[n]$ |
|-----|-------------|--------|
| 0 | $1 \cdot 1$ | **1** |
| 1 | $1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1$ | **3** |
| 2 | $1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1$ | **4** |
| 3 | $1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2$ | **3** |
| 4 | $1 \cdot 1$ | **1** |

So $y[n] = \{1, 3, 4, 3, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 5:** Verify by computing DTFT of $y[n]$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + 3e^{-j\omega} + 4e^{-j2\omega} + 3e^{-j3\omega} + e^{-j4\omega}$$

Factoring out $e^{-j2\omega}$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j2\omega} \left( e^{j2\omega} + 3e^{j\omega} + 4 + 3e^{-j\omega} + e^{-j2\omega} \right) = e^{-j2\omega} \left( 4 + 6\cos\omega + 2\cos(2\omega) \right)$$

This matches the result from Step 3. $\blacksquare$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Frequency Shifting & Modulation

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Supplementary 2024]`**

**(a)** State and prove the frequency-shifting property of DTFT. Discuss its role in **amplitude modulation** of discrete-time signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the signal $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$. Using the DTFT, find the DTFT of $y[n] = (-1)^n x[n]$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Proof:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j\omega})$, then $e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x[n] \xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$.

**Proof:**

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_0 n}\, x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)n} = X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$$

**Application in AM:** For a discrete AM signal $y[n] = x[n] \cos(\omega_c n)$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{2} X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_c)}) + \frac{1}{2} X(e^{j(\omega + \omega_c)})$$

The baseband spectrum is **translated** to $\pm \omega_c$ — this is the essence of modulation.

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Note that $(-1)^n = e^{j\pi n}$, so $y[n] = e^{j\pi n} x[n]$.

**Step 2:** The DTFT of $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ is (using geometric series):

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( a e^{-j\omega} \right)^n = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **frequency-shifting property** with $\omega_0 = \pi$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = X(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j(\omega - \pi)}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using $e^{-j(\omega - \pi)} = -e^{-j\omega}$:

$$Y(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a(-e^{-j\omega})} = \frac{1}{1 + a e^{-j\omega}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result is **consistent with direct computation:** $y[n] = (-a)^n u[n]$, and $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-a)^n e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1}{1 - (-a) e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{1}{1 + a e^{-j\omega}}$. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in shifting:** $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$ (NOT $+j\omega n_0$). A positive time shift corresponds to a **negative** exponential in frequency.
> 2. **Forgetting $2\pi$ scaling:** The multiplication property and inverse DTFT both contain $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factors. Skipping this leads to a magnitude mismatch in energy calculations.
> 3. **Misapplying periodicity:** When integrating over a $2\pi$ interval, students often write $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ instead of $\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}$. This is incorrect and loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing DFT and DTFT properties:** DFT uses circular convolution with **no** $\frac{1}{N}$ scaling in the time-domain product; DTFT uses periodic convolution with a $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor.
> 5. **Linearity constants:** If the question gives $y[n] = 3 x[n] - 2 x[n-5]$, students often forget the **exponential term** for the shifted copy: $Y(e^{j\omega}) = 3 X(e^{j\omega}) - 2 e^{-j5\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Properties of DTFT (KTU 2024)**

- **The DTFT is continuous and periodic** with period $2\pi$. The principal range is $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi]$.
- **Linearity** preserves both magnitude and phase; it is the only property that allows **superposition** in LTI systems.
- **Time Shifting** $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow e^{-j\omega n_0} X(e^{j\omega})$: affects **phase only**, not magnitude.
- **Frequency Shifting** $e^{j\omega_0 n} x[n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{j(\omega - \omega_0)})$: used in **modulation** and **upconversion**.
- **Time Reversal** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(e^{-j\omega})$: produces a **mirror image** of the spectrum.
- **Differentiation in Frequency** $n x[n] \leftrightarrow j \frac{d}{d\omega} X(e^{j\omega})$: used to derive frequency-response properties of differentiators.
- **Convolution Theorem**: Convolution in **time** = Multiplication in **frequency** (no scaling factor). This is the **fundamental LTI relation**.
- **Multiplication Theorem**: Multiplication in **time** = Periodic Convolution in **frequency** (scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$). Models **windowing** and **AM modulation**.
- **Conjugation** $x^*[n] \leftrightarrow X^*(e^{-j\omega})$: leads to **conjugate symmetry** for real signals.
- **Conjugate Symmetry** for real $x[n]$: $X(e^{j\omega}) = X^*(e^{-j\omega})$, meaning **magnitude is even** and **phase is odd** in $\omega$.
- **Parseval's Theorem** relates **time-domain energy** to **frequency-domain energy** with a $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor.
- **Duality** of DTFT: The DTFT of $X(e^{j\omega})$ (viewed as a sequence in $\omega$) is $2\pi x[-n]$.
- The DTFT of $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ is a **periodic impulse train** in the frequency domain: $2\pi \sum_k \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k)$.
- The DTFT of $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ is $\pi[\sum_k \delta(\omega - \omega_0 - 2\pi k) + \sum_k \delta(\omega + \omega_0 - 2\pi k)]$.
- The DTFT of $a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert < 1$ is $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$.
- The DTFT of a rectangular pulse $x[n] = 1$ for $N_1 \le n \le N_2$ is a **Dirichlet kernel** (aliased sinc function): $\frac{\sin[\omega(N_2 - N_1 + 1)/2]}{\sin(\omega/2)} e^{-j\omega(N_1+N_2)/2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Transform. Z Transform - ROC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inverse Transform. Z Transform - ROC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Z-Transform — Formal Definition

The **Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable of the form $z = r e^{j\omega}$, with $r$ being the magnitude and $\omega$ the angle in the complex z-plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Z-Transform is the discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace Transform. Where Laplace handles continuous-time signals with the variable $s$, the Z-Transform handles discrete-time signals with the variable $z = e^{sT}$ (sampled at sampling period $T$).

The **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** is:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

## 1.2 The Region of Convergence (ROC) — Formal Definition

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$ is the set of all complex values of $z$ in the z-plane for which the Z-transform summation **converges to a finite value**, i.e.,

$$\text{ROC} = \left\{ z \in \mathbb{C} : \left\vert \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n} \right\vert < \infty \right\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A given expression $X(z)$ (algebraic form) does **NOT uniquely** identify a signal $x[n]$ unless the ROC is specified. The same $X(z)$ with different ROCs corresponds to completely different time-domain signals.

## 1.3 The Inverse Z-Transform — Formal Definition

The **Inverse Z-Transform** recovers the discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its $X(z)$ and ROC, using the contour integral:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$$

where $C$ is a **closed counter-clockwise contour** in the ROC encircling the origin.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

### Analogy 1: The Z-Transform as a "Recipe Book"
Imagine each discrete signal $x[n]$ is a unique **recipe** and $X(z)$ is a written **cooking instruction set**. The ROC is the **kitchen environment** (temperature, humidity) under which the recipe is valid. Two different kitchens can produce two completely different dishes using the same written instructions — but the food will only "turn out right" inside the right kitchen (ROC).

### Analogy 2: The ROC as a "Valid Operating Zone"
Think of $X(z)$ as the **mathematical engine of a car**, and the ROC as the **valid speed range** (say, 40 km/h to 120 km/h) where the engine works correctly. The poles are **"red-line" limits** — the engine literally cannot be operated there (mathematically, $X(z)$ blows up to $\infty$). The ROC is everything in between or beyond, where the math is safe.

### Analogy 3: Inverse Transform as "Decoding a Song"
If a song is encoded as $X(z)$ (a frequency-domain-style representation), the Inverse Z-Transform is the **decoder** that plays back the original melody $x[n]$ — but **only if** you tell the decoder which "frequency band" (ROC) the song was recorded in. Wrong ROC = wrong song.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Whenever you see a Z-transform problem on the exam, the **first thing to write** is the ROC. Even if the question doesn't ask for it, mentioning the ROC earns easy partial credit and demonstrates mastery of the topic.

## 1.5 Standard Z-Transform Pairs (Reference Table)

| Signal $x[n]$ | Z-Transform $X(z)$ | ROC |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | All $z$ |
| $u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $-u[-n-1]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ |
| $a^{n} u[n]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $-a^{n} u[-n-1]$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| $n a^{n} u[n]$ | $\dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^{2}} = \dfrac{a z}{(z-a)^{2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^{2} - z \cos \omega_0}{z^{2} - 2z \cos \omega_0 + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $\sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z \sin \omega_0}{z^{2} - 2z \cos \omega_0 + 1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| $a^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^{2} - a z \cos \omega_0}{z^{2} - 2 a z \cos \omega_0 + a^{2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| $a^{n} \sin(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{a z \sin \omega_0}{z^{2} - 2 a z \cos \omega_0 + a^{2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the ROC Matters — The Uniqueness Problem

Two completely different signals can have the **exact same** algebraic $X(z)$. For example:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

* If ROC $= \vert z \vert > \frac{1}{2}$ → $x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$ (right-sided, decaying)
* If ROC $= \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{2}$ → $x[n] = -\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[-n-1]$ (left-sided, growing backward)

**Without the ROC, the signal is ambiguous.**

## 2.2 Properties of the ROC — The Five Golden Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Topic:** These five properties appear in nearly every KTU exam paper as a 3-mark short answer or part of a 14-mark question.

### Property 1: ROC is a Ring or Disk in the z-Plane
The ROC is always bounded by circles centered at the **origin** of the z-plane. Possible shapes:
* **Outside a circle** (right-sided signal)
* **Inside a circle** (left-sided signal)
* **Annular ring between two circles** (two-sided signal)
* **Entire z-plane** (finite-duration signal, bounded)

### Property 2: ROC Cannot Contain Any Poles
If $X(z)$ has a pole at $z = p$, then $\vert X(z) \vert = \infty$ at $z = p$. Since the ROC requires $X(z)$ to be finite, **no pole can lie inside the ROC**.

### Property 3: ROC is Bounded by Poles (for Rational $X(z)$)
The boundary of the ROC coincides with the locations of the poles of $X(z)$.

### Property 4: Sequence Length Determines ROC Type

| Sequence Type | ROC Form |
| :--- | :--- |
| Right-sided ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < n_0$) | $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ (outside outermost pole) |
| Left-sided ($x[n] = 0$ for $n > n_0$) | $\vert z \vert < r_{\min}$ (inside innermost pole) |
| Two-sided | $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ (annular ring) |
| Finite duration, right-sided | All $z$ except $z = 0$ |
| Finite duration, left-sided | All $z$ except $z = \infty$ |
| Finite duration, two-sided | Entire z-plane (if includes $n=0$) |

### Property 5: Stability Implication
A **causal LTI system is stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert z \vert < 1$). Equivalently, the ROC of the system's transfer function (when causal) must **include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Property / Concept | Formula | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Forward Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | Bilateral definition |
| Inverse Z-Transform | $x[n] = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) z^{n-1} dz$ | Contour integral |
| ROC Convergence | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert \cdot \vert z \vert^{-n} < \infty$ | Absolute convergence condition |
| Stability Condition | All poles $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ and system causal | Equivalent: ROC $\supseteq$ unit circle |
| Causal System ROC | $\vert z \vert > \vert p \vert_{\max}$ | Outside outermost pole |
| Causal + Stable ROC | $1 < \vert z \vert < \infty$ actually $\vert z \vert > \vert p \vert_{\max}$ with $\vert p \vert_{\max} < 1$ | ROC includes unit circle |
| Linearity | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n] \leftrightarrow a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | ROC contains $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Time Shift | $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | ROC unchanged (except $z=0,\infty$) |
| Time Reversal | $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z)$ | ROC: $1/r_1 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_2$ |
| Convolution | $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | ROC contains $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Multiplication by $a^{n}$ | $a^{n} x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z/a)$ | ROC scaled by $\vert a \vert$ |
| Differentiation | $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | ROC unchanged |
| Initial Value | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Only if $x[n]$ causal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No $\vert \cdot \vert$ pipes in tables:** Note the use of `\vert` instead of literal `|` in the table cells above to avoid breaking markdown rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Digital Filter Design:** The Z-transform converts difference equations (time domain) into algebraic equations (z-domain), enabling design of IIR and FIR filters.
2. **Digital Control Systems:** Stability of sampled-data control systems is determined by pole locations in the z-plane (must be inside the unit circle).
3. **Speech Processing:** Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) uses Z-transforms to model the vocal tract.
4. **Communications:** Digital receivers use Z-transform-based matched filters and equalizers.
5. **Image Processing:** 2D Z-transforms are used in image filtering and reconstruction.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Convergence Condition for a Causal Exponential

Consider $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ where $u[n]$ is the unit step.

**Step 1: Write the Z-transform summation.**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{+\infty} a^{n} u[n] \, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{+\infty} a^{n} z^{-n}$$

**Step 2: Apply the absolute convergence test.**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert a^{n} \vert \cdot \vert z \vert^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left( \frac{\vert a \vert}{\vert z \vert} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 3: This is an infinite geometric series.** It converges only when the ratio is less than 1.

$$\frac{\vert a \vert}{\vert z \vert} < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 4: Sum the geometric series.**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

**Step 5: State the ROC explicitly.**

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

This pole-zero plot has one pole at $z = a$ and one zero at $z = 0$ (from the $z$ in the numerator), with the ROC being the region outside the circle of radius $\vert a \vert$.

## 3.2 Inverse Z-Transform — Method 1: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2$$

**Step 1: Express in standard form by multiplying by $z$/$z$ (optional but cleaner).**

$$X(z) = \frac{z^{2}}{(z - \frac{1}{2})(z - 2)}$$

**Step 2: Set up the partial fraction expansion in terms of $z/(z - p)$ form.**

$$X(z) = A \cdot \frac{z}{z - \frac{1}{2}} + B \cdot \frac{z}{z - 2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for residues $A$ and $B$ using the cover-up method.**

For $A$ (at $z = 1/2$):

$$A = \left. \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}} \right|_{z = 1/2} = \left. \frac{z}{z - 2} \right|_{z = 1/2} = \frac{1/2}{1/2 - 2} = \frac{1/2}{-3/2} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

For $B$ (at $z = 2$):

$$B = \left. \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \right|_{z = 2} = \left. \frac{z}{z - 1/2} \right|_{z = 2} = \frac{2}{2 - 1/2} = \frac{2}{3/2} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 4: Write $X(z)$ with the computed coefficients.**

$$X(z) = -\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{z}{z - \frac{1}{2}} + \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{z}{z - 2}$$

**Step 5: Apply the ROC to determine which standard pair to use.**

Since ROC $= \vert z \vert > 2$, both terms are **right-sided** (causal). Use:

$$\frac{z}{z - a} \quad \leftrightarrow \quad a^{n} u[n], \quad \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 6: Write the inverse Z-transform.**

$$x[n] = -\frac{1}{3} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n] + \frac{4}{3} (2)^{n} u[n]$$

**Verification (KTU Examiner's Trick):** The first term decays (stable), the second term grows (unstable). Since the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 2$ and does NOT include the unit circle, the system is **unstable** — consistent with a growing output.

## 3.3 Inverse Z-Transform — Method 2: Power Series / Long Division

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 1: Perform long division of numerator by denominator.**

$$1 \div (1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1})$$

Performing polynomial long division in powers of $z^{-1}$:

$$X(z) = 1 + \frac{1}{2} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{4} z^{-2} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-3} + \cdots$$

**Step 2: Generalize the pattern.**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} z^{-n}$$

**Step 3: Read off $x[n]$ directly.**

By definition $X(z) = \sum x[n] z^{-n}$, so:

$$x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$$

This matches the standard pair. Long division is best suited when you only need the first few samples.

## 3.4 Inverse Z-Transform — Method 3: Residue (Contour Integration)

**Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform using residues for

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 1$$

**Step 1: Set up the contour integral.**

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} \cdot z^{n-1} \, dz = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} \frac{z^{n}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} \, dz$$

**Step 2: Identify the poles of the integrand.**

The integrand $F(z) = \dfrac{z^{n}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)}$ has poles at $z = 1$ and $z = 0.5$.

**Step 3: Determine which poles are enclosed by $C$.**

For $n \geq 0$: Both poles lie inside $C$ (since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$).

**Step 4: Apply the Residue Theorem.**

$$x[n] = \sum \text{Residues of } F(z) \text{ at enclosed poles}$$

Residue at $z = 1$ (simple pole):

$$\text{Res}_{z=1} = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{z^{n}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{1^{n}}{1 - 0.5} = 2$$

Residue at $z = 0.5$ (simple pole):

$$\text{Res}_{z=0.5} = \lim_{z \to 0.5} (z - 0.5) \cdot \frac{z^{n}}{(z-1)(z-0.5)} = \frac{(0.5)^{n}}{0.5 - 1} = \frac{(0.5)^{n}}{-0.5} = -2(0.5)^{n}$$

**Step 5: Sum the residues.**

$$x[n] = 2 - 2(0.5)^{n} = 2\left[1 - (0.5)^{n}\right] u[n]$$

**Verification at $n=0$:** $x[0] = 2[1 - 1] = 0$ ✓ (The signal starts at $n=0$ with value $0$, then grows toward 2.)

## 3.5 ROC Determination — Worked Example

**Problem:** Determine the ROC for the signal $x[n] = (0.5)^{n} u[n] - (2)^{n} u[-n-1]$.

**Step 1: Take the Z-transform of each term.**

First term: $X_1(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

Second term: $X_2(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$ (with negative sign), ROC: $\vert z \vert < 2$.

**Step 2: Combine.**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: ROC is the intersection.**

$$\text{ROC: } 0.5 < \vert z \vert < 2$$

This is the **annular ring** between circles of radius $0.5$ and $2$. The unit circle is included, so the system is **stable**.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Z-Plane ROC Visualization (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Z Transform Xz exists only for some z values"] --> B["Identify all poles of Xz from denominator"]
    B --> C{"Signal Type?"}
    C -->|Right sided xn=0 for n less than n0| D["ROC is OUTSIDE outermost pole: mod z greater than r_max"]
    C -->|Left sided xn=0 for n greater than n0| E["ROC is INSIDE innermost pole: mod z less than r_min"]
    C -->|Two sided signal| F["ROC is RING between poles: r1 less than mod z less than r2"]
    C -->|Finite duration| G["ROC is ENTIRE z plane possibly excluding z equals 0 and z equals infinity"]
    D --> H["Check stability: is unit circle in ROC?"]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Unit circle included?"}
    I -->|Yes| J["SYSTEM IS STABLE"]
    I -->|No| K["SYSTEM IS UNSTABLE"]
    J --> L["END: ROC analysis complete"]
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Inverse Z-Transform Method Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given Xz and ROC"] --> B{"Form of Xz?"}
    B -->|Rational proper fraction| C{"ROC given?"}
    B -->|Improper fraction| D["Use LONG DIVISION Power Series"]
    B -->|Non rational or transcendental| E["Use RESIDUE Contour Integration"]
    C -->|Yes causal ROC outside| F["PARTIAL FRACTION with causal pairs"]
    C -->|Yes anticausal ROC inside| G["PARTIAL FRACTION with anticausal pairs"]
    C -->|No ROC given| H["STOP: ROC is required for inverse ZT"]
    F --> I["Write xn in time domain"]
    G --> I
    D --> I
    E --> I
    I --> J["END: Inverse Z Transform computed"]
```

## 4.3 ROC vs Pole Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Z Plane Diagram"] --> B["Pole at z equals 0 point 5"]
    A --> C["Pole at z equals 2"]
    A --> D["ROC: Annular Ring 0 point 5 less than mod z less than 2"]
    A --> E["Unit Circle mod z equals 1 is INSIDE ROC"]
    E --> F["System is STABLE"]
    B --> G["Pole OUTSIDE Unit Circle equals 2"]
    G --> H["Would be UNSTABLE if right sided"]
    C --> G
    C --> I["Pole INSIDE Unit Circle equals 0 point 5"]
    I --> J["Stable decay"]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: Time Domain to Z-Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Discrete Signal xn"] -->|Forward Z Transform| B["Algebraic Xz plus ROC"]
    B -->|ROC selection| C{"Three ROC Options"}
    C -->|Right sided mod z greater than 2| D["Right sided sequence a to n un"]
    C -->|Left sided mod z less than 0 point 5| E["Left sided sequence negative a to n u negative n minus 1"]
    C -->|Two sided 0 point 5 less than mod z less than 2| F["Two sided sequence sum of both"]
    B -->|Inverse Z Transform| G["Recover xn uniquely"]
    G --> A
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform. State any three properties. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of the Z-transform $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ is the set of all complex values of $z$ for which the summation converges to a finite value, i.e., $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert \vert z \vert^{-n} < \infty$.

**Three Properties:**
1. The ROC is a **ring or disk** in the z-plane centered at the origin.
2. The ROC **cannot contain any poles** of $X(z)$.
3. For a **causal** sequence, the ROC is the region **outside the outermost pole** ($\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$).
4. (Bonus) A causal LTI system is **stable** iff the ROC **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

---

### Question 2: Determine the ROC of the Z-transform of $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^{n} z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^{n}$$

For convergence of this geometric series, we need $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

Therefore, $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z - a}$ with **ROC**: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{3 - 2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ for $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the ROC and comment on stability for $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 2 z^{-1})}$ with $x[n]$ right-sided. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Perform long division.**

$X(z)$ is improper (numerator degree 1 = denominator degree 1), so we first do polynomial division.

$$X(z) = \frac{3 - 2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

Divide: $(3 - 2z^{-1}) \div (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$:

* First term: $3 \cdot (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) = 3 - 1.5 z^{-1}$
* Remainder: $(3 - 2z^{-1}) - (3 - 1.5 z^{-1}) = -0.5 z^{-1}$
* Next term: $-0.5 z^{-1} / (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) \approx -0.5 z^{-1} (1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + \cdots)$

$$X(z) = 3 + \frac{-0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2: Simplify the remainder term.**

$$\frac{-0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = -1 + \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

Therefore:

$$X(z) = 3 - 1 + \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = 2 + \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the inverse Z-transform using the standard pair.**

$$x[n] = 2 \delta[n] + (0.5)^{n} u[n]$$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the poles.**

Poles at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 2$ (from the denominator roots).

**Step 2: Apply the right-sided rule.**

For a right-sided sequence, ROC is outside the **outermost** pole:

$$\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2$$

**Step 3: Comment on stability.**

A causal (right-sided) LTI system is **stable** if and only if all poles lie **inside** the unit circle. Here, $z = 2$ is **outside** the unit circle, and the ROC ($\vert z \vert > 2$) does **not** include the unit circle.

**Conclusion: The system is UNSTABLE.** The output will contain the term $(2)^{n} u[n]$, which grows without bound as $n \to \infty$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Using partial fraction expansion, find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{2z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}$ for $\vert z \vert > 0.8$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the five properties of the ROC. Is the system with $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 0.7}$, ROC $= \vert z \vert < 0.7$, stable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1: Set up PFE in $z^{-1}$ form.**

$$\frac{2z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})} = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2: Solve for A and B.**

$$2 z^{-1} = A(1 - 0.8 z^{-1}) + B(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$$

Setting $z^{-1} = 2$ (root of $1 - 0.5 z^{-1} = 0$): $A = \dfrac{2 \cdot 2}{1 - 0.8 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{4}{-0.6} = -\dfrac{20}{3}$

Setting $z^{-1} = 1.25$ (root of $1 - 0.8 z^{-1} = 0$): $B = \dfrac{2 \cdot 1.25}{1 - 0.5 \cdot 1.25} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.375} = \dfrac{20}{3}$

**Step 3: Write $X(z)$ with coefficients.**

$$X(z) = -\frac{20/3}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{20/3}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4: Apply inverse Z-transform (ROC $= \vert z \vert > 0.8$ means right-sided, causal).**

$$x[n] = -\frac{20}{3} (0.5)^{n} u[n] + \frac{20}{3} (0.8)^{n} u[n]$$

$$x[n] = \frac{20}{3}\left[(0.8)^{n} - (0.5)^{n}\right] u[n]$$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Five Properties of ROC:**

1. **Ring/Disk Shape:** ROC is always a ring or disk in the z-plane centered at the origin. Possible forms: $\vert z \vert > r_1$, $\vert z \vert < r_2$, or $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$.

2. **No Poles Inside:** The ROC cannot contain any pole of $X(z)$, because at a pole $X(z) \to \infty$ (diverges).

3. **Bounded by Poles:** For rational $X(z)$, the ROC boundary coincides with pole locations.

4. **Sequence Type Determines ROC:**
   * Right-sided → ROC is outside outermost pole
   * Left-sided → ROC is inside innermost pole
   * Two-sided → ROC is a ring between poles

5. **Stability Implication:** A causal LTI system is stable iff all poles are strictly inside the unit circle, equivalently, the ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$.

**Stability Analysis of $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 0.7}$, ROC $= \vert z \vert < 0.7$:**

The pole is at $z = 0.7$, which lies **inside** the unit circle. However, the ROC is $\vert z \vert < 0.7$, which corresponds to a **left-sided (anti-causal)** sequence, given by the pair:

$$-a^{n} u[-n-1] \quad \leftrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$$

So $x[n] = -(0.7)^{n} u[-n-1]$ (left-sided, growing backward in time).

**Stability Test:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{-1} (0.7)^{n}$. This is a convergent geometric series, so the signal is **absolutely summable**, hence **stable**. The ROC ($\vert z \vert < 0.7$) does include the unit circle (the entire unit disk $\vert z \vert \leq 1$ is inside ROC), confirming stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC:** Many students write $x[n] = a^{n} u[n]$ without specifying the ROC. **Always include the ROC** — it is mandatory for a complete answer.
> 2. **Wrong Pair Choice:** The expression $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ corresponds to **two different signals** depending on ROC: $a^{n} u[n]$ for $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ and $-a^{n} u[-n-1]$ for $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$. Mixing these up is the most common error.
> 3. **Stability Confusion:** A causal system with poles outside the unit circle is **unstable**, but an **anti-causal** system with the same pole location can be **stable** (because the ROC still includes the unit circle).
> 4. **Arithmetic in PFE:** When computing residues using the cover-up method, students often forget to evaluate $z^{-1}$ at the correct pole value (e.g., for $z = 0.5$, you must plug in $z^{-1} = 2$, not $z = 0.5$).
> 5. **No Final Verification:** Always verify $x[0]$ using $X(\infty)$ for causal sequences: $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$. If the values don't match, your PFE has an error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Z-Transform ROC: Quick-Fire Revision Points**

- [x] **Z-transform definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ (bilateral)
- [x] **ROC definition:** Set of $z$ values where the sum converges absolutely.
- [x] **ROC Uniqueness:** Same $X(z)$ with different ROC = different signal.
- [x] **Pole Exclusion Rule:** Poles can never lie inside the ROC.
- [x] **Right-sided signal ROC:** $\vert z \vert > \vert p \vert_{\max}$ (outside outermost pole)
- [x] **Left-sided signal ROC:** $\vert z \vert < \vert p \vert_{\min}$ (inside innermost pole)
- [x] **Two-sided signal ROC:** $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ (annular ring)
- [x] **Finite duration signal ROC:** Entire z-plane (except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$)
- [x] **Stability Criterion:** Causal system stable ⇔ all poles inside unit circle ⇔ ROC includes $\vert z \vert = 1$
- [x] **Standard pairs to memorize:** $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$; $u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-1}$; $a^{n} u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{z}{z-a}$
- [x] **PFE Method:** Best for proper rational $X(z)$ with known poles.
- [x] **Long Division Method:** Best for first few samples or improper fractions.
- [x] **Residue Method:** Best for non-rational or transcendental $X(z)$.
- [x] **Time reversal:** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z)$, ROC: $1/r_2 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_1$
- [x] **Initial value theorem:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ (causal signals only)
- [x] **Final value theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ (if limit exists)
- [x] **Convolution property:** $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z) X_2(z)$, ROC $\supseteq R_1 \cap R_2$
- [x] **Differentiation property:** $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In a 14-mark KTU question, allocate marks as follows: 2 marks for identifying poles, 2 marks for stating the ROC, 4 marks for PFE computation, 4 marks for inverse transform using standard pairs, 2 marks for stability comment. This is the typical KTU valuation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties (Proof Not Needed)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties (Proof Not Needed)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Z-Transform** (the central topic of Module 3 in PCECT402), the term **"Properties"** refers to the collection of mathematical theorems and operational rules that govern how the Z-Transform behaves when specific operations are applied either to the discrete-time signal in the time domain or to the algebraic expression in the Z-domain. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly classifies these under the category **"Proof Not Needed"**, meaning the student is required to **state the property, identify the conditions of validity, and apply it to solve problems** — but formal mathematical proofs are excluded from the assessment scope.

The Z-Transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

The **properties** are essentially **shortcut rules** that allow us to compute $X(z)$ for modified versions of $x[n]$ (such as shifted, scaled, convolved, or differentiated sequences) without having to apply the summation definition from scratch every single time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Under the Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, students must demonstrate **CO3 (Apply)** by using these properties in numerical problems, particularly for **finding the Z-Transform of complex sequences** and for **solving difference equations**. Proofs are NOT a part of the evaluation rubric.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are working in a **currency exchange counter**. A customer hands you **$100 in US Dollars**, and you must convert it to Euros. Now consider three scenarios:
