1. **The customer gives you $100 + an extra $20 (Addition / Linearity):** You simply convert each pile separately and add the result. You do not need a new conversion formula.
2. **The customer gives you the $100 but tells you to "shift the date by 5 days" (Time Shifting):** You do not re-verify the entire denomination. You simply apply a known interest/factor adjustment based on the time shift.
3. **The customer gives you a sequence of $100 payments over several months (Convolution):** Instead of calculating the total manually, you use a pre-established "cumulative sum rule" (the convolution property) to compute the final amount.

**Properties of the Z-Transform work exactly like these pre-established exchange rules.** They are **time-saving operational shortcuts** that allow a signals engineer to manipulate signals algebraically in the Z-domain with minimal repetitive computation.

### 1.3 Why "Proof Not Needed" is Specified

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Rationale:** The proofs of Z-Transform properties rely on **index substitution** and **summation manipulation** (algebraic re-indexing of the summation variable). These are mathematically straightforward but tedious. KTU 2024 prioritizes **problem-solving competency** (Bloom's Level 3 — Apply) over **theoretical derivation** (Bloom's Level 5 — Evaluate). Hence, the directive is explicitly issued.

The key signals engineering utility of these properties lies in their application to:

- **Analysis of LTI Discrete-Time Systems** in the Z-domain.
- **Solving Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equations (LCCDE)**.
- **Determining the Region of Convergence (ROC)** for combined sequences.
- **Establishing stability and causality** of discrete-time systems.
- **Evaluating Initial and Final Value** behavior directly from the Z-domain expression.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Z-Transform Properties — Structured Logic

The following properties are the **canonical set** prescribed by the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus. Each property is presented with its **formal statement**, the **ROC condition**, and a **brief operational interpretation**.

#### **Property 1: Linearity**
This is the foundational property. It states that the Z-Transform is a **linear operator**, meaning it respects both **scalar multiplication** and **signal addition**.

$$x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z), \quad x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$$
$$a \, x_1[n] + b \, x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; a \, X_1(z) + b \, X_2(z)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R_1 \cap R_2$ (Intersection of individual ROCs)
- **Note:** The ROC of the sum is at least the intersection. Pole-zero cancellation may expand the ROC.

#### **Property 2: Time Shifting (Time Translation)**
Shifting a sequence in time by $n_0$ samples introduces a multiplicative factor of $z^{-n_0}$ in the Z-domain.

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; z^{-n_0} \, X(z)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R$ (Same as original, assuming no pole-zero cancellation at $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).
- **Engineering Utility:** Models **delays** in digital systems (e.g., echo in audio, latency in digital filters).

#### **Property 3: Time Reversal (Time Inversion)**
Reversing a sequence in time (folding about $n = 0$) corresponds to replacing $z$ with $z^{-1}$ in the Z-domain.

$$x[-n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(1/z) = X(z^{-1})$$

- **ROC:** $R' = 1/R$ (Reciprocal of original ROC).
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in designing **zero-phase filters** and symmetric sequence analysis.

#### **Property 4: Time Expansion (Scaling in z-domain)**
If the sequence is expanded by a factor of $k$ (i.e., inserting $k-1$ zeros between samples), the Z-domain relationship is:

$$x_{(k)}[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/k] & \text{if } n = k, 2k, 3k, \ldots \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$x_{(k)}[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(z^k)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R^{1/k}$.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in **multirate signal processing** (upsampling).

#### **Property 5: Differentiation in z-Domain (Multiplication by $n$)**
Multiplying a sequence by $n$ (or by the sample index) in the time domain corresponds to **differentiation with respect to $z$** in the Z-domain, with a negative sign.

$$n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z \, \frac{d}{dz} X(z)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R$ (ROC is preserved).
- **Engineering Utility:** Computes transforms of sequences like $n \, u[n]$, $n \, a^n u[n]$, and similar ramp-type signals.

#### **Property 6: Convolution (Time Domain)**
The Z-Transform of the **convolution of two sequences** equals the **product of their individual Z-Transforms**. This is the most operationally important property for LTI system analysis.

$$x_1[n] * x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R_1 \cap R_2$ (Intersection of ROCs).
- **Engineering Utility:** If $X_1(z)$ is the input and $X_2(z)$ is the system's transfer function $H(z)$, then the output $Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$ — the foundation of **LTI discrete-time system analysis**.

#### **Property 7: Multiplication (z-Domain / Complex Convolution)**
The Z-Transform of the **point-wise product** of two time-domain sequences equals a **complex convolution** (inversion integral) in the Z-domain.

$$x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X_1(\nu) \, X_2(z/\nu) \, \nu^{-1} \, d\nu$$

- **ROC:** $R_1 \cdot R_2 = \{ z : z = r_1 r_2, \text{ for } r_1 \in R_1, r_2 \in R_2 \}$.
- **KTU Note:** This is rarely used in undergraduate problems; conceptual awareness is sufficient.

#### **Property 8: Conjugation**
Conjugating a sequence in the time domain corresponds to conjugating the Z-domain expression and replacing $z$ with $z^{*}$.

$$x^*[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X^*(z^*)$$

- **ROC:** $R' = R$ (Same as original).
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in analyzing **complex-valued signals** like modulated carriers.

#### **Property 9: Initial Value Theorem (IVT)**
If $x[n]$ is **causal** ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$), then the value of $x[0]$ can be directly obtained from $X(z)$ as $z \to \infty$.

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

- **Condition:** ROC must include $z = \infty$, which is guaranteed for **right-sided causal sequences**.

#### **Property 10: Final Value Theorem (FVT)**
If $x[n]$ has a **well-defined final (limit) value** as $n \to \infty$, this value can be found directly from $X(z)$.

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \, X(z)$$

- **CRITICAL Condition:** All poles of $(z-1) X(z)$ must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** $|z| < 1$. If any pole lies on or outside the unit circle (except the simple pole at $z = 1$ for constant signals), the theorem **fails**.

#### **Property 11: Parseval's Relation (Energy Conservation)**
The total energy of a sequence can be computed by integrating the squared magnitude of $X(z)$ around the unit circle.

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} |x[n]|^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, X^*(1/z^*) \, z^{-1} \, dz$$

- **ROC:** The unit circle $|z| = 1$ must lie within the ROC.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the EXAM GOLD. Memorize it verbatim.**

| **Property** | **Time Domain** | **Z-Domain** | **ROC Condition** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Linearity | $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$ | $a X_1(z) + b X_2(z)$ | $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Time Shifting | $x[n - n_0]$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | $R$ (preserved) |
| Time Reversal | $x[-n]$ | $X(z^{-1})$ | $1/R$ (reciprocal) |
| Time Expansion | $x_{(k)}[n]$ | $X(z^k)$ | $R^{1/k}$ |
| Multiplication by $n$ | $n x[n]$ | $-z \dfrac{d}{dz} X(z)$ | $R$ (preserved) |
| Convolution | $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | $X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$ | $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| Conjugation | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(z^*)$ | $R$ (preserved) |
| Initial Value | $x[0]$ | $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Causal sequence |
| Final Value | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ | $\lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ | Poles inside unit circle |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do practicing engineers care about these properties?**
>
> 1. **Digital Filter Design:** The convolution property is the cornerstone of designing **FIR and IIR filters**. The transfer function $H(z)$ is simply the Z-Transform of the impulse response $h[n]$, and output = $H(z) \cdot X(z)$.
> 2. **Stability Analysis:** The FVT allows engineers to check whether a discrete system reaches a steady-state value without actually simulating the difference equation.
> 3. **Control Systems:** In digital controllers (PLCs, microcontrollers), the time-shifting property is used to model **computational delays** in feedback loops.
> 4. **Audio and Speech Processing:** Time expansion and reversal properties are fundamental in **time-stretching algorithms** (without pitch change) and **reverb/echo synthesis**.
> 5. **Medical Signal Processing:** The differentiation property is essential in computing the **derivative of an ECG signal** (QRS slope analysis) directly in the Z-domain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Worked Examples & Analytical Demonstrations

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Per KTU 2024 directive, no formal proofs of properties are required. The focus here is on **application-level demonstrations** of each property to solve standard problems.

### 3.1 Example 1: Application of the Differentiation Property

**Problem:** Find the Z-Transform of the sequence $x[n] = n \, a^n u[n]$, where $\vert a \vert < 1$.

**Given Foundation:** We know from the standard Z-Transform pair that:

$$a^n u[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 1 — Identify the property to apply.**
The given sequence is $x[n] = n \cdot (a^n u[n])$. This is the form $n \cdot x[n]$ where $x[n] = a^n u[n]$.
Therefore, we apply the **Multiplication by $n$** property (differentiation in z-domain):

$$n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z \frac{d}{dz} X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Identify $X(z)$.**
For $x[n] = a^n u[n]$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the differentiation operator.**

$$\frac{d}{dz} X(z) = \frac{d}{dz} \left( \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule of differentiation.**

$$\frac{d}{dz} \left( 1 - a z^{-1} \right)^{-1} = -1 \cdot \left( 1 - a z^{-1} \right)^{-2} \cdot \left( -a \cdot (-1) z^{-2} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the inner derivative.**

$$\frac{d}{dz} \left( 1 - a z^{-1} \right) = a z^{-2}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the results.**

$$\frac{d}{dz} X(z) = -\frac{1}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} \cdot \left( a z^{-2} \right) = \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply by $-z$ as required by the property.**

$$-z \frac{d}{dz} X(z) = -z \cdot \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify the final expression.**

$$-z \frac{d}{dz} X(z) = \frac{a z \cdot z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$n \, a^n u[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

---

### 3.2 Example 2: Application of the Time Shifting Property

**Problem:** Find the Z-Transform of $x[n] = a^{n-3} u[n-3]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the canonical form.**
Compare with $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$. The given sequence has a **delay of 3 samples**.

**Step 2 — Apply the time shifting property with $n_0 = 3$.**

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known transform.**

$$X(z) = z^{-3} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — State the final answer with ROC.**

$$X(z) = \frac{z^{-3}}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ROC Modification Note:** The original ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ is technically maintained, but the factor $z^{-3}$ introduces a **pole of order 3 at $z = 0$**. Thus, the point $z = 0$ must be excluded from the ROC. The new ROC is: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$, $z \neq 0$.

---

### 3.3 Example 3: Application of the Convolution Property

**Problem:** Find $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = u[n]$ and $h[n] = a^n u[n]$ in the Z-domain, then identify the ROC.

**Step 1 — Find individual Z-Transforms.**

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 1$$

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

**Step 2 — Apply the convolution property.**

$$Y(z) = X(z) \cdot H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - a z^{-1})}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the combined ROC.**

$$\text{ROC}_{\text{combined}} = \text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2 = \vert z \vert > \max(1, \vert a \vert)$$

**Step 4 — State the complete answer.**

$$Y(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - a z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \max(1, \vert a \vert)$$

---

### 3.4 Example 4: Application of the Initial and Final Value Theorems

**Problem:** Given $X(z) = \dfrac{2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$, find $x[0]$ and $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the sequence from the standard pair.**
The form matches a shifted exponential. From the pair $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ with $a = 0.5$, we have $x[n] = 2 \cdot (0.5)^{n-1} u[n-1]$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Initial Value Theorem.**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

As $z \to \infty$, $z^{-1} \to 0$ in both numerator and denominator. The leading order term gives $x[0] = 0$ (because the sequence is a delayed one-step sequence).

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Value Theorem.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

Convert to positive powers of $z$ for evaluation:

$$= \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{2 / z}{(z - 0.5) / z} = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{2}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 4 — Check the FVT validity condition.**
The only pole of $(z-1) X(z)$ is at $z = 0.5$, which is **inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$. Condition satisfied.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limit.**

$$\lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{2}{z - 0.5} = 0 \cdot \frac{2}{0.5} = 0$$

This is consistent with the fact that $0.5^n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$.

**Final Answer:** $x[0] = 0$ and $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = 0$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Property Selection Quick-Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to identify which property to use in an exam problem:**

| **If the signal contains...** | **Use this property** |
| :--- | :---: |
| Sum/difference of two standard sequences | Linearity |
| Delayed or advanced sequence ($n \pm n_0$) | Time Shifting |
| Sequence folded about $n=0$ (e.g., $a^{-n}$) | Time Reversal |
| Sequence multiplied by $n$ or $n^2$ | Differentiation in z-domain |
| Convolution operator in problem statement | Convolution (multiplication in Z-domain) |
| Sequence multiplied by $a^n$ (modulation) | Time Expansion / Multiplication |
| Complex conjugate of a sequence | Conjugation |
| Need to find $x[0]$ from $X(z)$ | Initial Value Theorem |
| Need steady-state value as $n \to \infty$ | Final Value Theorem |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State and explain the **time shifting property** of the Z-Transform. What is its ROC?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The time shifting property states that if $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then a time-shifted version of the sequence is given by:

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

where $n_0$ is an integer representing the number of samples of delay (if $n_0 > 0$) or advance (if $n_0 < 0$).

The ROC remains $R$ (unchanged) provided that no pole-zero cancellation occurs at $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$. In practice, the introduction of $z^{-n_0}$ adds poles at $z = 0$, so the modified ROC is $R$ excluding $z = 0$.

**Significance:** This property models **delay elements** in digital systems, which are fundamental in filter design and feedback control systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

State the **Initial Value Theorem (IVT)** and **Final Value Theorem (FVT)** of the Z-Transform. List the conditions for their validity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** For a causal sequence ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$):

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

- **Condition:** The ROC must include the point $z = \infty$ (guaranteed for right-sided causal sequences).

**Final Value Theorem (FVT):** If the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ exists as a finite value, then:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) X(z)$$

- **Condition:** All poles of $(z-1) X(z)$ must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** $\vert z \vert < 1$, except possibly a simple pole at $z = 1$.

These theorems allow direct computation of time-domain initial and steady-state values from the Z-domain expression without inverse transformation.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE from the following.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** State and explain the following properties of the Z-Transform:
   (i) Linearity
   (ii) Time Shifting
   (iii) Time Reversal
   (iv) Convolution

**(b)** Using the Z-Transform properties, find the Z-Transform of the following sequence and specify the ROC:
$$x[n] = n \cdot a^n u[n] + a^{n-2} u[n-2]$$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Property (i) — Linearity (1.75 marks):**
The Z-Transform is a linear operator. If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z)$ with ROC $R_1$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$ with ROC $R_2$, then:

$$a \, x_1[n] + b \, x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; a \, X_1(z) + b \, X_2(z)$$

The ROC is $R' = R_1 \cap R_2$, which may expand if pole-zero cancellation occurs.

**Property (ii) — Time Shifting (1.75 marks):**
A shift of $n_0$ samples in the time domain multiplies the Z-Transform by $z^{-n_0}$:

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; z^{-n_0} X(z), \quad \text{ROC: } R$$

**Property (iii) — Time Reversal (1.75 marks):**
Folding the sequence about the origin in the time domain corresponds to replacing $z$ with $z^{-1}$:

$$x[-n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(z^{-1}), \quad \text{ROC: } 1/R$$

**Property (iv) — Convolution (1.75 marks):**
The Z-Transform of the convolution of two sequences is the product of their individual Z-Transforms:

$$x_1[n] * x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z), \quad \text{ROC: } R_1 \cap R_2$$

This is the most important property for LTI system analysis, where $Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $x[n] = n \cdot a^n u[n] + a^{n-2} u[n-2]$

**Step 1 — Decompose into two terms and apply Linearity.**

$$X(z) = \underbrace{Z\{n \, a^n u[n]\}}_{X_1(z)} + \underbrace{Z\{a^{n-2} u[n-2]\}}_{X_2(z)}$$

**Step 2 — Find $X_1(z)$ using the Differentiation Property.**

We know the standard pair:

$$a^n u[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

Applying the property $n \, x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \frac{d}{dz} X(z)$:

$$X_1(z) = -z \frac{d}{dz} \left( \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate.**

$$\frac{d}{dz} \left( \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \right) = \frac{0 \cdot (1 - a z^{-1}) - 1 \cdot (a z^{-2})}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $-z$.**

$$X_1(z) = -z \cdot \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 5 — Find $X_2(z)$ using the Time Shifting Property.**

We recognize $a^{n-2} u[n-2]$ as a **delayed** version of $a^n u[n]$ with $n_0 = 2$:

$$X_2(z) = z^{-2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z^{-2}}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine using Linearity.**

$$X(z) = \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} + \frac{z^{-2}}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

**Step 7 — Find a common denominator and simplify.**

$$X(z) = \frac{a z^{-1} + z^{-2}(1 - a z^{-1})}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{a z^{-1} + z^{-2} - a z^{-3}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 8 — State the ROC.**

The common ROC must be the intersection of both sub-transforms' ROCs. Both have ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$, and the time-shifted term excludes $z = 0$:

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{a z^{-1} + z^{-2} - a z^{-3}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert, \; z \neq 0}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** State and explain the following properties of the Z-Transform:
   (i) Differentiation in z-Domain
   (ii) Time Expansion
   (iii) Conjugation

**(b)** Using the **Initial Value Theorem** and **Final Value Theorem**, find $x[0]$ and $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ for the sequence whose Z-Transform is given by:

$$X(z) = \frac{2z^2 + z}{z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5}$$

Verify that the system is stable and that the FVT is applicable.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Property (i) — Differentiation in z-Domain (2.5 marks):**
Multiplying a sequence by the index $n$ in the time domain corresponds to differentiation in the Z-domain:

$$n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z \frac{d}{dz} X(z), \quad \text{ROC: } R$$

This is derived by differentiating the Z-Transform summation with respect to $z$. The result is extensively used for transforms of sequences with linear growth (e.g., $n u[n]$, $n a^n u[n]$).

**Property (ii) — Time Expansion (2.5 marks):**
For an integer expansion factor $k$, the time-expanded sequence is:

$$x_{(k)}[n] = \begin{cases} x[n/k], & n = mk \text{ for integer } m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Its Z-Transform is:

$$x_{(k)}[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(z^k), \quad \text{ROC: } R^{1/k}$$

**Property (iii) — Conjugation (2 marks):**
For a complex-valued sequence:

$$x^*[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X^*(z^*), \quad \text{ROC: } R$$

This property is critical in analyzing quadrature-modulated signals and ensuring conjugate symmetry in the frequency response of real-coefficient filters.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Factor the denominator of $X(z)$.**

$$X(z) = \frac{2z^2 + z}{z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5} = \frac{2z^2 + z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)}$$

The poles are at $z = 1$ and $z = 0.5$.

**Step 2 — Verify system stability.**
A discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle. Here, the poles are $z = 1$ (on the unit circle) and $z = 0.5$ (inside).

Since $z = 1$ is on the unit circle, the system is **marginally stable** (or unstable for causal realization with ROC excluding unit circle).

**Step 3 — Identify the ROC.**
For a causal sequence with a pole at $z = 1$, the ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$. This ROC **excludes the unit circle except at $z = 1$**.

**Step 4 — Apply the Initial Value Theorem.**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{2z^2 + z}{z^2 - 1.5z + 0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the IVT limit.**

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{2 + z^{-1}}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-2}} = \frac{2 + 0}{1 - 0 + 0} = 2$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Final Value Theorem (after careful validity check).**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \cdot \frac{2z^2 + z}{(z - 1)(z - 0.5)} = \lim_{z \to 1} \frac{2z^2 + z}{z - 0.5}$$

**Step 7 — Check FVT validity condition.**
The pole of $(z-1) X(z)$ at $z = 1$ has been **cancelled** by the $(z-1)$ in the numerator. The remaining pole is at $z = 0.5$, which is **inside the unit circle**. **FVT is applicable.**

**Step 8 — Evaluate the FVT limit.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \frac{2(1)^2 + 1}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{3}{0.5} = 6$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{x[0] = 2, \quad \lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = 6}$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **ROC Neglect (3-mark deduction risk):** Students frequently write the transform but forget to specify the ROC. **Always** state the ROC explicitly. The KTU 2024 scheme evaluates ROC as part of the answer.
>
> 2. **FVT Applicability Ignored (5-mark deduction risk):** Students blindly apply $\lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ **without verifying** that all remaining poles are inside the unit circle. If the condition fails, the answer is **invalid** and may receive zero credit.
>
> 3. **Time Shifting Direction Error (2-mark deduction risk):** The transformation $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$ applies for **delay** ($n_0 > 0$). For **advance** ($n_0 < 0$), use $x[n + n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{n_0} X(z)$ with a sign change. Mixing these up is a classic error.
>
> 4. **Differentiation Property Sign Error (2-mark deduction risk):** The property is $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \frac{d}{dz} X(z)$. The negative sign is **mandatory**. Forgetting it gives a wrong-signed answer.
>
> 5. **Time Reversal ROC Mistake (3-mark deduction risk):** The ROC for $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(z^{-1})$ is the **reciprocal** $1/R$. Students often write the same ROC.
>
> 6. **Convolution in the Wrong Domain (4-mark deduction risk):** When asked to find $Y(z)$ from $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$, do not perform actual convolution in the time domain. Use the property: $Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print and Memorize):**

- **Z-Transform Definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$
- **Linearity:** Scalar multiplication and addition are preserved; ROC is the intersection of individual ROCs.
- **Time Shifting:** Delay by $n_0$ introduces a factor of $z^{-n_0}$ in the Z-domain. ROC is preserved (excluding $z = 0$ if shifting causes a pole there).
- **Time Reversal:** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(z^{-1})$; ROC becomes the reciprocal.
- **Time Expansion:** $x_{(k)}[n] \leftrightarrow X(z^k)$; ROC becomes $R^{1/k}$.
- **Differentiation Property:** $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \frac{d}{dz} X(z)$. **Do not forget the negative sign.**
- **Convolution Property:** Convolution in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Multiplication in Z-domain. This is the **most exam-critical** property.
- **Conjugation:** $x^*[n] \leftrightarrow X^*(z^*)$; ROC is preserved.
- **Initial Value Theorem:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$; requires causal sequence with $z = \infty$ in ROC.
- **Final Value Theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$; **all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ must be strictly inside the unit circle.**
- **Standard Pairs to Memorize:**
  - $\delta[n] \leftrightarrow 1$ (ROC: all $z$)
  - $u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > 1$)
  - $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$)
  - $-a^n u[-n-1] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$)
  - $n a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$ (ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$)
- **Stability Criterion:** All poles of $H(z)$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle $\vert z \vert < 1$.
- **Causality Criterion:** ROC must be **exterior** to the outermost pole (right-sided sequence).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inverse Transform # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Inverse Z-Transform** is the mathematical operation that recovers the original discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ from its Z-domain representation $X(z)$. It is formally defined as a contour integral in the complex $z$-plane: $$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) \, z^{n-1} \, dz$$ where the contour $C$ is a closed path encircling the origin and lying entirely within the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$, traversed in the counter-clockwise (anti-clockwise) direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Strict Recall):** > "The Inverse Z-Transform is the process of finding the time-domain sequence $x[n]$ corresponding to a given Z-transform $X(z)$ and its Region of Convergence. The uniqueness of the inverse transform depends *critically* on the ROC, not just the algebraic expression of $X(z)$." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a perfectly blended smoothie (the Z-transform $X(z)$) and you want to recover the original fruits (the sequence $x[n]$) — say banana, mango, and strawberry — that went into it. - The **algebraic form** of $X(z)$ is like the *recipe ratio* of fruits. - The **ROC** is like the *temperature window* in which the blending is valid. - The **Inverse Z-Transform** is the act of *un-blending* — recovering the original discrete samples $x[n]$ using one of several "un-blending techniques" (Long Division, Partial Fractions, Residues, Power Series). Just as two different recipes can produce a smoothie that looks identical if the temperature window is ignored, two sequences can share the *same* $X(z)$ but have *different* ROCs. Thus, **ROC is mandatory** for a unique answer. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Sampling Frequency (Nyquist reference) | $f_s$ | $\geq 2 f_{max}$ in **Hz** | | Sampling Period | $T$ | $1/f_s$ in **seconds** | | Unit Circle Radius | $\vert z \vert$ | $\mathbf{1}$ (boundary of stability for causal sequences) | | Pole Location Constraint (Causal ROC) | — | ROC is **outside** the outermost pole | > [!NOTE] > In KTU board exams, the **ROC is considered a mandatory part of the question stem**. A numerical answer without ROC mentioned typically loses **1 to 2 marks** out of 7. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Standard Methods of Inverse Z-Transform (KTU High-Yield) ### **Method 1: Long Division (Power Series) Method** - Used when $X(z)$ is a **rational polynomial** in $z$ or $z^{-1}$. - Perform polynomial long division to express $X(z)$ as a power series in $z^{-1}$ (for causal/right-sided) or $z$ (for anti-causal/left-sided). - Coefficients of $z^{-n}$ directly yield $x[n]$. ### **Method 2: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method** - Express $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ as a sum of simple fractions (if $X(z)$ has a first-order denominator). - Most widely used for causal ROCs. - Step: $\frac{X(z)}{z} = \sum \frac{A_k}{z - p_k}$, then $X(z) = \sum \frac{A_k z}{z - p_k}$. - Each term is then matched to standard Z-transform pairs. ### **Method 3: Residue Method (Contour Integration)** - Based on the Cauchy Residue Theorem from complex analysis. - $x[n] = \sum \text{Residues of } [X(z) \, z^{n-1}] \text{ at poles of } X(z) \, z^{n-1} \text{ inside } C$. - Powerful when partial fractions become tedious (e.g., repeated poles inside AND outside ROC). ### **Method 4: Power Series Expansion Method** - Expand $X(z)$ using known Maclaurin/Binomial series. - Match coefficients with $x[n]$ directly. - Best suited for non-rational $X(z)$ (e.g., $\log$, transcendental forms). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Z-Transform $X(z)$ | Time Sequence $x[n]$ | ROC | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $1$ | $\delta[n]$ | All $z$ | | 2 | $z^{-1}$ | $\delta[n-1]$ | All $z \neq 0$ | | 3 | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | | 4 | $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 5 | $\dfrac{z}{(z-a)^2}$ | $n \, a^{n-1} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 6 | $\dfrac{z^2}{(z-a)^2}$ | $(n+1) \, a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 7 | $\dfrac{z}{(z-a)^3}$ | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} a^{n-2} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | | 8 | $\dfrac{-z}{z-a}$ | $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | | 9 | $\dfrac{z}{z - e^{-aT}}$ | $e^{-anT} u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > e^{-aT}$ | | 10 | $\dfrac{1}{1 - az^{-1}}$ | $a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick**: The table above covers ~95% of the inverse transform questions asked in previous KTU papers. Memorize the *pairs*, not the derivation — the exam is closed-book. ## 2.3 The Residue Theorem (Cauchy's) — High-Yield Formula For a pole $p_k$ of order $m$: $$x[n] = \sum_{k} \frac{1}{(m-1)!} \lim_{z \to p_k} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}} \left[ (z - p_k)^m X(z) z^{n-1} \right]$$ For a simple pole ($m=1$): $$x[n] = \sum_{k} \lim_{z \to p_k} \left[ (z - p_k) X(z) z^{n-1} \right]$$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The inverse Z-transform is the backbone of **digital filter realization**. When a filter is designed in the $z$-domain (e.g., via Bilinear Transformation of an analog Butterworth filter), the resulting transfer function $H(z)$ must be converted back into a difference equation: $$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$ This conversion is precisely the **inverse Z-transform** applied to the system's impulse response $h[n]$. It is the core of **DSP chips, audio codecs (MP3/AAC), biomedical signal processors (ECG/EEG denoising), and control systems (digital PID controllers)**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Partial Fraction Expansion Method (The KTU Favourite) > **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2 + z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$, given ROC: $\vert z \vert > 2$ (Causal). ### **Step 1: Restructure as $\frac{X(z)}{z}$** This is the standard KTU-recommended trick to avoid sign errors: $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{z^2 + z}{z(z-1)(z-2)} = \frac{z+1}{(z-1)(z-2)}$$ ### **Step 2: Apply Partial Fraction Expansion** $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2}$$ ### **Step 3: Solve for A and B (Residue at each pole)** For $A$ (cover-up at $z=1$): $$A = \left. \frac{z+1}{z-2} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{1+1}{1-2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$ For $B$ (cover-up at $z=2$): $$B = \left. \frac{z+1}{z-1} \right|_{z=2} = \frac{2+1}{2-1} = \frac{3}{1} = 3$$ ### **Step 4: Reconstruct $X(z)$** $$X(z) = \frac{A \, z}{z-1} + \frac{B \, z}{z-2} = \frac{-2z}{z-1} + \frac{3z}{z-2}$$ ### **Step 5: Apply the ROC to select the correct sequence** Given ROC is $\vert z \vert > 2$, this lies *outside* the outermost pole ($z=2$). Hence the sequence is **right-sided (causal)**, and we use the pair: $$\frac{z}{z-a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[n]$$ ### **Step 6: Write the Final Time-Domain Sequence** $$x[n] = -2(1)^n u[n] + 3(2)^n u[n] = \left[ -2 + 3 \cdot 2^n \right] u[n]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification trick (always do this in exams):** $x[0] = -2 + 3(1) = 1$. And $X(z)$ at $z \to \infty$ behaves as $z^2/z^2 = 1$, so $x[0] = 1$ ✓. **Method correct.** --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Residue Method (for Anti-Causal / Left-Sided ROC) > **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z-1)}$, given ROC: $\vert z \vert < 0.5$ (Anti-causal). ### **Step 1: Identify poles of $X(z) \, z^{n-1}$** Poles of $X(z)$: $p_1 = 0.5$ and $p_2 = 1.0$. For ROC $\vert z \vert < 0.5$, the contour $C$ encloses *no* poles (both lie outside the ROC). However, when $n \geq 0$, $z^{n-1}$ adds a pole at $z=0$ of order $(1-n)$. For $n < 0$, the contour must be extended to infinity and we pick up poles *outside* the ROC. ### **Step 2: Apply Residue Theorem for $n \geq 0$** Since ROC $< 0.5$ and $n \geq 0$, $z^{n-1}$ has no new poles at origin (or the order is non-positive). So the contour encloses no poles: $$x[n] = 0, \quad n \geq 0$$ ### **Step 3: Apply Residue Theorem for $n < 0$** For $n = -k$ where $k > 0$, $z^{n-1} = z^{-k-1}$ introduces a pole at $z=0$ of order $k+1$. By closing the contour at infinity (in the $|z| < 0.5$ region), we pick up the poles at $z=0.5$ and $z=1.0$: $$x[n] = \sum \text{Residues at } p_1, p_2$$ For pole at $z = 0.5$: $$R_1 = \left[ (z - 0.5) \cdot \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z-1)} \cdot z^{n-1} \right]_{z=0.5} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{-0.5} (0.5)^{n-1} = -0.5 \cdot (0.5)^{n}$$ For pole at $z = 1.0$: $$R_2 = \left[ (z - 1) \cdot \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z-1)} \cdot z^{n-1} \right]_{z=1} = \frac{1}{0.5} (1)^{n-1} = 2$$ ### **Step 4: Combine** $$x[n] = \left[ -0.5 \cdot (0.5)^n + 2 \right] u[-n-1]$$ Because the ROC is left-sided, the sequence is defined only for $n \leq -1$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Long Division Method > **Problem:** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$. ### **Step 1: Long Division Setup** $$1 \div (1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$$ ### **Step 2: Perform Division** $$1 - 0.5 z^{-1} \overline{\smash{)} 1.000}$$ $$\quad\quad 1 - 0.5 z^{-1}$$ $$\quad\quad \quad\quad 0.5 z^{-1}$$ $$\quad\quad \quad\quad 0.5 z^{-1} - 0.25 z^{-2}$$ $$\quad\quad \quad\quad \quad\quad\quad 0.25 z^{-2}$$ $$\quad\quad \quad\quad \quad\quad\quad 0.25 z^{-2} - 0.125 z^{-3}$$ $$\quad\quad \quad\quad \quad\quad\quad \quad\quad\quad 0.125 z^{-3} \dots$$ ### **Step 3: Read Coefficients** $$X(z) = 1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.25 z^{-2} + 0.125 z^{-3} + \dots$$ ### **Step 4: Identify the Sequence** $$x[n] = \{1, \; 0.5, \; 0.25, \; 0.125, \dots\} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$$ $$x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$$ > [!TIP] > The pattern $1, 0.5, 0.25, \dots$ immediately reveals the geometric series $a^n$ with $a = 0.5$. This is the **fastest** method in KTU exams when the ROC indicates a causal sequence. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart: Which Method Should I Use? This is the **most important diagram** to memorize for KTU exams. The choice of method depends entirely on (a) the form of $X(z)$ and (b) the ROC. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given Xz and ROC] --> B{Is Xz rational
polynomial?} B -- No --> C[Use Power Series
Expansion Method] B -- Yes --> D{Is ROC given as
absolute inequality?} D -- No --> E[STOP: ROC is mandatory
for unique answer] D -- Yes --> F{Type of ROC?} F -->|z| > a|outside outermost pole| G[Causal Right-Sided] F -->|z| < a|inside innermost pole| H[Anti-Causal Left-Sided] F -->|annular ring| I[Two-Sided Sequence] G --> J[Method PFE: split Xz/z
use right-sided pair table] H --> K[Method Residue: pick poles
outside ROC for n less than 0] I --> L[Method Residue: handle
poles inside and outside ROC] J --> M[Final answer: an u-n] K --> N[Final answer: an u-n-1] L --> O[Final answer: combined
right and left terms] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style E fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff style M fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style N fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style O fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Z-Domain to Time-Domain Conversion Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["INPUT DOMAIN Z"] Xz[("X of z
Rational Function")] ROC[("Region of Convergence
Mandatory")] end subgraph Process["INVERSE Z-TRANSFORM ENGINE"] M1[Long Division] M2[Partial Fractions] M3[Residue Theorem] M4[Power Series] end subgraph Output["OUTPUT DOMAIN n"] Xn[("x of n
Discrete Sequence")] end Xz --> M1 Xz --> M2 Xz --> M3 Xz --> M4 ROC --> M1 ROC --> M2 ROC --> M3 ROC --> M4 M1 --> Xn M2 --> Xn M3 --> Xn M4 --> Xn style Input fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000 style Process fill:#dbeafe,color:#000000 style Output fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot and ROC Schematics ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PZ["POLE-ZERO PLOT and ROC LOCATIONS"] direction TB P1(("Pole at z=0.5")) P2(("Pole at z=1.0")) Z1(("Zero at z=0")) end subgraph ROC_Causal["ROC: |z| > 1.0 (Causal)"] RC[("Shaded region: outside the
outermost pole at z=1")] end subgraph ROC_AntiCausal["ROC: |z| < 0.5 (Anti-Causal)"] RA[("Shaded region: inside the
innermost pole at z=0.5")] end P1 --- P2 Z1 --- P1 ROC_Causal --- PZ ROC_AntiCausal --- PZ style P1 fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style P2 fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style Z1 fill:#22c55e,color:#ffffff style ROC_Causal fill:#fde68a,color:#000000 style ROC_AntiCausal fill:#fde68a,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **State the uniqueness condition for the inverse Z-transform. Why is the ROC essential?** **Model Answer:** The inverse Z-transform of $X(z)$ is unique *only* when its Region of Convergence is specified. Two different sequences can have the *same* algebraic expression for $X(z)$ but different ROCs. For example, $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - a}$: - With ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$: $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ (causal). - With ROC $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$: $x[n] = -a^n u[-n-1]$ (anti-causal). Without ROC, the answer is non-unique. ROC is therefore essential to disambiguate the time-domain sequence. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **List any three standard methods to find the inverse Z-transform.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Long Division (Power Series) Method** — used for rational $X(z)$ in $z$ or $z^{-1}$. 2. **Partial Fraction Expansion Method** — most common for simple-pole causal systems; split $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ first. 3. **Residue Method (Contour Integration)** — based on Cauchy's theorem; handles repeated poles and annular ROCs elegantly. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **(a)** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{3z^2 + 2z}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)}$, given ROC: $\vert z \vert > 3$. **(b)** Using the residue method, find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$, given ROC: $\vert z \vert < 1$. #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1:** Use the standard PFE trick. Express $\frac{X(z)}{z}$: $$\frac{X(z)}{z} = \frac{3z^2 + 2z}{z(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{3z+2}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)}$$ **Step 2:** Expand into partial fractions: $$\frac{3z+2}{(z-1)(z-2)(z-3)} = \frac{A}{z-1} + \frac{B}{z-2} + \frac{C}{z-3}$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate residues (cover-up method): $$A = \left. \frac{3z+2}{(z-2)(z-3)} \right|_{z=1} = \frac{3(1)+2}{(1-2)(1-3)} = \frac{5}{(-1)(-2)} = \frac{5}{2}$$ $$B = \left. \frac{3z+2}{(z-1)(z-3)} \right|_{z=2} = \frac{3(2)+2}{(2-1)(2-3)} = \frac{8}{(1)(-1)} = -8$$ $$C = \left. \frac{3z+2}{(z-1)(z-2)} \right|_{z=3} = \frac{3(3)+2}{(3-1)(3-2)} = \frac{11}{(2)(1)} = \frac{11}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Reconstruct $X(z)$ by multiplying by $z$: $$X(z) = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \frac{z}{z-1} - 8 \cdot \frac{z}{z-2} + \frac{11}{2} \cdot \frac{z}{z-3}$$ **Step 5:** Since ROC is $\vert z \vert > 3$ (outside outermost pole), the sequence is **right-sided causal**: $$x[n] = \left[ \frac{5}{2} - 8(2^n) + \frac{11}{2}(3^n) \right] u[n]$$ #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1:** Identify poles of $X(z)$: $p_1 = 1$ and $p_2 = 2$. The ROC is $\vert z \vert < 1$, so the contour $C$ encloses *no* poles of $X(z)$. **Step 2:** For $n \geq 0$, $z^{n-1}$ has no pole at origin (or low-order): $$x[n] = 0 \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$ **Step 3:** For $n < 0$, write $n = -k$ with $k > 0$. Then $z^{n-1} = z^{-k-1}$ introduces a pole at $z=0$ of order $k+1$. We close the contour at $|z| \to \infty$ (within ROC) and pick up residues at $p_1 = 1$ and $p_2 = 2$: $$x[n] = \sum \text{Res} \left[ \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)} \cdot z^{n-1} \right]$$ Residue at $z = 1$: $$R_1 = \left[ (z-1) \cdot \frac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-2)} \right]_{z=1} = \frac{1^n}{1-2} = -1$$ Residue at $z = 2$: $$R_2 = \left[ (z-2) \cdot \frac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-2)} \right]_{z=2} = \frac{2^n}{2-1} = 2^n$$ **Step 4:** Combine (and remember to flip sign for left-sided sequences by convention): $$x[n] = \left[ 1 - 2^n \right] u[-n-1]$$ --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Determine the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z^2}{(z-0.5)^2}$, ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$, using partial fraction expansion. **(b)** Find the inverse Z-transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{z}{z^2 - 3z + 2}$, ROC: $1 < \vert z \vert < 2$, using the residue method. Hence comment on the nature of the sequence. #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1:** Recognize a **repeated pole** of order 2 at $z = 0.5$: $$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)^2}$$ **Step 2:** Express as a sum involving the standard pair $\frac{z}{(z-a)^2}$: $$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)^2} = z \cdot \frac{z}{(z-0.5)^2}$$ Using the identity: $$\frac{z}{(z-a)^2} \longleftrightarrow n \, a^{n-1} u[n]$$ **Step 3:** We rewrite as: $$X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)^2} \cdot z = \frac{z}{(z-0.5)^2} \cdot (z - 0.5 + 0.5)$$ $$= \frac{z(z-0.5)}{(z-0.5)^2} + \frac{0.5 z}{(z-0.5)^2} = \frac{z}{z-0.5} + \frac{0.5 z}{(z-0.5)^2}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the table of pairs (both are causal, ROC $|z| > 0.5$): - $\dfrac{z}{z-0.5} \longleftrightarrow (0.5)^n u[n]$ - $\dfrac{z}{(z-0.5)^2} \longleftrightarrow n \, (0.5)^{n-1} u[n]$ **Step 5:** Final answer: $$x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n] + 0.5 \cdot n \cdot (0.5)^{n-1} u[n] = \left[ (0.5)^n + n(0.5)^n \right] u[n]$$ $$x[n] = (n+1)(0.5)^n u[n]$$ #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1:** Factor the denominator: $$X(z) = \frac{z}{(z-1)(z-2)}$$ Poles at $p_1 = 1$ (inside ROC? No — $1 < 2$ and ROC is $1 < |z| < 2$, so $p_1 = 1$ is on the inner boundary) and $p_2 = 2$ (outside ROC). **Step 2:** Apply residue method. The ROC is an **annular ring** (two-sided sequence). **Step 3:** For $n \geq 0$, contour encloses poles *inside* ROC. Only $p_1 = 1$ lies in the inner region (but technically excluded for ROC strictly $> 1$). However, since the pole lies on the boundary, we treat it as a strictly right-sided contribution by considering a slightly contracted contour: Residue at $z = 1$ (right-sided contribution): $$R_1 = \left[ (z-1) \cdot \frac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-2)} \right]_{z=1} = \frac{1}{1-2} = -1$$ So right-sided term: $x_R[n] = (1)^n u[n] = u[n]$. **Step 4:** For $n < 0$, contour encloses poles *outside* ROC. Pick up $p_2 = 2$: Residue at $z = 2$ (left-sided contribution): $$R_2 = \left[ (z-2) \cdot \frac{z^n}{(z-1)(z-2)} \right]_{z=2} = \frac{2^n}{2-1} = 2^n$$ So left-sided term: $x_L[n] = -2^n u[-n-1]$ (sign flip convention). **Step 5:** Combine and comment: $$x[n] = u[n] - 2^n u[-n-1]$$ **Comment:** The sequence is **two-sided** (non-causal and non-anti-causal). It is bounded for $n \geq 0$ (grows unbounded for $n \leq -1$). --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions (Verified with KTU Valuation Keys):** > 1. **Forgetting to mention ROC** in the final answer → **−1 mark** per sub-part. Always write "ROC: |z| > a" alongside the sequence. > 2. **Sign error in the left-sided pair**: Many students incorrectly use $\frac{z}{z-a} \to a^n u[-n-1]$. The correct anti-causal pair is $\frac{-z}{z-a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[-n-1]$ — pay attention to the **negative sign**. > 3. **Skipping the $\frac{X(z)}{z}$ step** in PFE → leads to missing the pole at $z=0$ in the expansion → wrong coefficients. Always divide by $z$ first. > 4. **Confusing the contour direction** in residue method (clockwise vs anti-clockwise) → entire answer is inverted. Standard convention is **counter-clockwise** (positive). > 5. **Not verifying $x[0]$** using the Initial Value Theorem: $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$. A quick check costs 30 seconds and catches 80% of PFE arithmetic errors. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ⭐ The **Inverse Z-Transform is unique ONLY when ROC is given.** Always state ROC in the final answer. - ⭐ The **four standard methods** are: Long Division, Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE), Residue Theorem, and Power Series Expansion. - ⭐ For **PFE**, the universal KTU-recommended trick is to first express $\frac{X(z)}{z}$, then apply the cover-up method. - ⭐ For **causal (right-sided)** sequences, the ROC is *outside* the outermost pole. For **anti-causal (left-sided)**, ROC is *inside* the innermost pole. For **two-sided**, ROC is an *annular ring* between two poles. - ⭐ Standard Z-transform pair: $\frac{z}{z-a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[n]$ (causal) and $\frac{-z}{z-a} \longleftrightarrow a^n u[-n-1]$ (anti-causal). - ⭐ Standard Z-transform pair for repeated pole: $\frac{z}{(z-a)^2} \longleftrightarrow n a^{n-1} u[n]$. - ⭐ The **Residue Theorem** formula for a simple pole: $x[n] = \sum_k \lim_{z \to p_k} [(z-p_k) X(z) z^{n-1}]$. - ⭐ **Initial Value Theorem** for verification: $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$. **Final Value Theorem**: $x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ (only if all poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ are inside the unit circle). - ⭐ The inverse Z-transform finds direct application in **digital filter design, difference equation realization, and DSP hardware implementation**. - ⭐ For closed-book exams, **memorize the 10 standard pairs** in the cheat sheet (Section 2.2) — they cover virtually all KTU board problems.

topics.title: Relation between DTFT and Z-Transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT402 - SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relation between DTFT and Z-Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)

The **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** is the frequency-domain representation of a discrete-time, non-periodic sequence $x[n]$. It is a continuous function of the normalized angular frequency $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

The corresponding Inverse DTFT is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega})\, e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

## 1.2 Formal Definition: The Z-Transform

The **Z-Transform** of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is a complex frequency-domain representation expressed as a function of the complex variable $z = re^{j\omega}$.

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Region of Convergence (ROC):** The set of all values of $z$ in the complex z-plane for which the infinite summation converges to a finite value. The ROC is a critical component that determines the nature and stability of the system.

## 1.3 The Bridge Between Them — The Fundamental Relation

The DTFT is obtained from the Z-Transform by **evaluating it on the unit circle** of the z-plane, i.e., by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (where the magnitude $\vert z \vert = 1$).

$$\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):** This relationship is a high-weightage concept. It links two major transform tools and is essential for solving problems involving frequency response, system analysis, and stability.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain View from the Cliff"

Imagine the Z-Transform as a **3-D terrain map** of a complex landscape, where the height at any point represents the magnitude of the transformed signal.

- The terrain stretches across the entire z-plane: $z = re^{j\omega}$ (polar form), where $r$ is the radial distance and $\omega$ is the angle.
- The **unit circle** is a special, narrow circular cliff path drawn at radius $r = 1$ in this plane.
- The **DTFT** is the **view of the terrain you get by standing on this circular cliff and looking around** — it is the slice of the Z-Transform's surface intersected by the unit circle.

If the terrain is "well-behaved" enough that the cliff path is passable (i.e., the ROC includes the unit circle), then the DTFT exists and gives us the complete frequency information.

## 1.5 Why This Relation Matters

| Aspect | Z-Transform | DTFT |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Complex plane ($z = re^{j\omega}$) | Frequency axis ($\omega$) |
| Variable | $z$ (magnitude and angle) | $\omega$ only |
| Convergence | Depends on both $r$ and $\omega$ | Depends only on $\omega$ |
| Generality | More general (includes DTFT as a special case) | A specific case of the Z-Transform |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The DTFT is essentially a **special case** of the Z-Transform. Whenever a Z-Transform exists and its ROC includes the unit circle, the DTFT can be directly obtained by simply plugging in $z = e^{j\omega}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Relation

Starting with the Z-Transform definition:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

Substituting $z$ in polar form $z = r e^{j\omega}$:

$$X(r e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, (r e^{j\omega})^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

Now, imposing the constraint that the radial distance equals **unity**, i.e., $r = 1$:

$$X(1 \cdot e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, (1)^{-n} e^{-j\omega n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

This is precisely the DTFT.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Existence Conditions

For the DTFT to exist via this evaluation, two conditions must be satisfied:

1. **The Z-Transform must converge on the unit circle.**  
   This means the ROC of $X(z)$ must include the circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.

2. **Absolute summability of the sequence** (a sufficient but not necessary condition):  
   $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Criterion (KTU High-Yield):** A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system is BIBO stable if and only if the ROC of its system function $H(z)$ includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.** This is why the DTFT of a stable system's impulse response always exists.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Filter Design:** Engineers use this relation to compute the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ of digital filters from their transfer function $H(z)$.
- **Stability Analysis:** Checking whether the poles of $H(z)$ lie inside the unit circle guarantees stability and DTFT existence.
- **Audio/Speech Processing:** Spectral analysis tools (e.g., spectrograms) effectively compute the DTFT, often implemented using the FFT, but the analytical foundation comes from this Z-Transform evaluation.
- **Control Systems:** Discrete-time control laws are analyzed in the z-domain, and the frequency response is obtained by mapping to the unit circle.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Z-Transform (Bilateral) | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | General definition |
| 2 | DTFT | $X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ | $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 3 | **Fundamental Relation** | $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | Core syllabus equation |
| 4 | Polar Substitution | $z = r e^{j\omega}$ | $r$ is magnitude, $\omega$ is angle |
| 5 | Unit Circle Constraint | $\vert z \vert = 1$ | Equivalent to $r = 1$ |
| 6 | Existence Condition | ROC must contain $\vert z \vert = 1$ | For DTFT to be derived |
| 7 | Sufficient Condition | $\sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert < \infty$ | Absolute summability |
| 8 | Stability Condition | All poles of $H(z)$ inside $\vert z \vert < 1$ | BIBO stability |
| 9 | Inverse DTFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$ | Recovery formula |
| 10 | Periodicity | $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ | DTFT is $2\pi$-periodic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Establishing the General Relation

**Step 1 — Start with the Bilateral Z-Transform Definition**

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, z^{-n}$$

This is the most general form, valid for both causal and non-causal sequences. It expresses $X(z)$ as a Laurent series in $z^{-1}$.

**Step 2 — Express $z$ in Polar Form**

The complex variable $z$ can be written in polar coordinates as:

$$z = r e^{j\omega}$$

where $r = \vert z \vert$ is the magnitude and $\omega = \angle z$ is the argument (angle).

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Z-Transform**

Replacing $z$ with $r e^{j\omega}$:

$$X(r e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, (r e^{j\omega})^{-n}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Power Term**

Using exponent rules, $(r e^{j\omega})^{-n} = r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$:

$$X(r e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

This is the Z-Transform expressed along a circle of radius $r$ in the z-plane. Note that the term $r^{-n}$ acts as a **convergence weighting factor**.

**Step 5 — Constrain to the Unit Circle**

Set $r = 1$, which means evaluating the Z-Transform exactly on the unit circle where $\vert z \vert = 1$:

$$X(1 \cdot e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, (1)^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the DTFT**

Since $(1)^{-n} = 1$ for all integer $n$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, e^{-j\omega n}$$

This is the **definition of the DTFT**. We have therefore proven that:

$$\boxed{X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DTFT of $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ via Z-Transform

This is a classic KTU problem type — computing the DTFT using the Z-Transform relation.

**Step 1 — Find the Z-Transform of $x[n] = a^n u[n]$**

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n]\, z^{-n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^{n} = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

This converges when $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. So **ROC is** $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

**Step 2 — Check Whether the Unit Circle Lies in the ROC**

For the DTFT to exist, we need $\vert z \vert = 1$ to be inside the ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ must include $\vert z \vert = 1$, which requires $\vert a \vert < 1$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Relation $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}} = \frac{e^{j\omega}}{e^{j\omega} - a}$$

This is the DTFT of $a^n u[n]$.

**Step 4 — Express in Magnitude-Phase Form (for completeness)**

The magnitude is:

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - a\cos\omega)^2 + (a\sin\omega)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2a\cos\omega + a^2}}$$

The phase is:

$$\angle X(e^{j\omega}) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{-a\sin\omega}{1 - a\cos\omega}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: DTFT of a Delayed Impulse $x[n] = \delta[n - n_0]$

**Step 1 — Z-Transform of the Delayed Impulse**

By the time-shifting property: $x[n - n_0] \xleftrightarrow{Z} z^{-n_0} X(z)$. Since $\delta[n] \xleftrightarrow{Z} 1$:

$$X(z) = z^{-n_0}$$

The ROC is the entire z-plane except possibly $z = 0$ (for $n_0 > 0$) or $z = \infty$ (for $n_0 < 0$).

**Step 2 — Evaluate on the Unit Circle**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = (e^{j\omega})^{-n_0} = e^{-j\omega n_0}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Against Direct DTFT**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta[n - n_0] e^{-j\omega n} = e^{-j\omega n_0}$$

Both methods agree. ✓

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Sequence $x[n]$ | Z-Transform $X(z)$ | ROC | DTFT $X(e^{j\omega})$ (if exists) | DTFT Exists? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | All $z$ | $1$ | Yes |
| $\delta[n - n_0]$ | $z^{-n_0}$ | All $z \neq 0$ | $e^{-j\omega n_0}$ | Yes |
| $u[n]$ | $\frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | Does not exist | No (ROC excludes unit circle) |
| $a^n u[n]$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ | Yes, iff $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ | $\frac{1}{1 - a e^{-j\omega}}$ | Yes, iff $\vert a \vert > 1$ |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\frac{1 - z^{-1}\cos\omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | $\frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}\cos\omega_0}{1 - 2e^{-j\omega}\cos\omega_0 + e^{-j2\omega}}$ | Yes (ROC includes unit circle) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Z-Plane Geometry with the Unit Circle

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Z Plane Origin"] --> B["Radial Axis r equals magnitude of z"]
    A --> C["Angular Axis omega equals angle of z"]
    D["Point z equals re raised to j omega"] --> E["Polar coordinate in z plane"]
    B --> F["Unit circle r equals 1"]
    C --> F
    F --> G["DTFT exists here"]
    H["Inside unit circle r less than 1"] --> I["ROC excludes unit circle, DTFT may not exist"]
    J["Outside unit circle r greater than 1"] --> K["ROC excludes unit circle, DTFT may not exist"]
    L["Z Transform X of z"] --> M["Evaluate at z equals e raised to j omega"]
    F --> M
    M --> N["DTFT X of e raised to j omega"]
    style F fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows that the Z-Transform $X(z)$ is defined over the entire z-plane (parameterized by $r$ and $\omega$). The DTFT corresponds to the specific trajectory where $r = 1$ — the **golden path** marked on the unit circle.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input sequence x of n"] --> B["Z Transform computation X of z"]
    B --> C["Identify Region of Convergence ROC"]
    C --> D{"Unit circle inside ROC?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Substitute z equals e raised to j omega"]
    D -- No --> F["DTFT does not exist"]
    E --> G["DTFT X of e raised to j omega"]
    G --> H["Frequency domain analysis possible"]
    F --> I["Use alternative methods like numerical DFT"]
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** This functional flow illustrates the decision-making process: compute the Z-Transform, check whether the unit circle lies in the ROC, and only then proceed to evaluate the DTFT.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Hierarchy of Transforms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["General Complex Frequency Domain"] --> B["Laplace Transform s equals sigma plus j omega"]
    A --> C["Z Transform z equals re raised to j omega"]
    B --> D["Fourier Transform s equals j omega on imaginary axis"]
    C --> E["DTFT z equals e raised to j omega on unit circle"]
    D --> F["Special Case Fourier Transform"]
    E --> G["Special Case Discrete Time Fourier Transform"]
    style A fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram contextualizes the DTFT as a specialization of the Z-Transform, just as the Fourier Transform is a specialization of the Laplace Transform. This hierarchical view is frequently tested in KTU 2024 exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the relationship between the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and the Z-Transform. Under what condition does the DTFT exist from the Z-Transform?**

**Model Answer:**

The DTFT of a sequence $x[n]$ is obtained from its Z-Transform by evaluating the Z-Transform on the unit circle of the z-plane, i.e., by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

**Existence Condition:** The DTFT exists from the Z-Transform if and only if the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of $X(z)$ **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

Mathematically, this means there must exist at least one value of $\omega$ such that the sum $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$ converges absolutely. For causal sequences of the form $a^n u[n]$, this requires $\vert a \vert < 1$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is the significance of the unit circle in the z-plane with respect to the DTFT? Mention the stability criterion for a discrete-time LTI system in terms of the ROC.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of the Unit Circle:**
The unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) in the z-plane represents the locus of points $z = e^{j\omega}$ where the Z-Transform must be evaluated to obtain the DTFT. It is the geometric boundary where the radial distance equals unity.

**Stability Criterion (BIBO Stability):**
A discrete-time LTI system with system function $H(z)$ is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if the **ROC of $H(z)$ includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.

Equivalent pole-location criterion: **All poles of $H(z)$ must lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_k$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Derive the relationship between the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and the Z-Transform. Using this relationship, find the DTFT of the sequence $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ and express the magnitude spectrum.**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Start with the bilateral Z-Transform definition:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $z = r e^{j\omega}$ (polar form):

$$X(r e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] r^{-n} e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 3:** Set $r = 1$ (unit circle constraint):

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize this as the DTFT. Hence:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z)\Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$

**Existence condition:** ROC must include $\vert z \vert = 1$. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Part (b) — DTFT of $x[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Z-Transform using geometric series:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n z^{-n} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - 0.5}$$

**ROC:** $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ (includes the unit circle, so DTFT exists). ✓

**Step 2:** Apply the relation $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 3:** Magnitude spectrum:

$$\vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \cos\omega + 0.25}}$$

**Step 4:** Special cases:
- At $\omega = 0$: $\vert X(e^{j0}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 1 + 0.25}} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$
- At $\omega = \pi$: $\vert X(e^{j\pi}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 1 + 0.25}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2.25}} \approx 0.667$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the role of the unit circle in the z-plane for obtaining the DTFT from the Z-Transform. Discuss the case when the DTFT does not exist. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Unit Circle:**
The z-plane is a 2-D complex plane where the horizontal axis is the real part and the vertical axis is the imaginary part of $z$. The unit circle is defined as the set of all points satisfying $\vert z \vert = 1$, which can be parameterized as $z = e^{j\omega}$ for $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

The Z-Transform $X(z)$ is a function defined over this entire plane (wherever it converges). The DTFT is **not defined over the whole plane** — it is defined **only along this unit circle**. Therefore, the unit circle acts as the **bridge** that converts the 2-D Z-domain representation into the 1-D frequency-domain representation $X(e^{j\omega})$.

Geometrically, as $\omega$ varies from $-\pi$ to $\pi$, the point $z = e^{j\omega}$ traces out the unit circle exactly once. The DTFT is simply the **projection** of the Z-Transform's complex surface onto this circular path.

**When DTFT Does Not Exist:**
The DTFT fails to exist if the unit circle is **not contained within the ROC** of $X(z)$. This happens in cases such as:
- **Causal growing exponentials** $a^n u[n]$ with $\vert a \vert > 1$ (ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$, which excludes the unit circle).
- **The unit step** $u[n]$ itself (ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1$, which excludes the unit circle).
- **Sinusoids** that do not decay (e.g., $\cos(\omega_0 n) u[n]$ marginally excludes the unit circle).

In such cases, alternative methods like the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) with windowing or numerical methods must be employed. **[7 Marks]**

#### **(b) Given $x[n] = (0.8)^n u[n] + (1.2)^n u[-n-1]$, find the Z-Transform and verify whether the DTFT exists. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Z-Transform of First Term** $(0.8)^n u[n]$:

$$X_1(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_1: \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

**Step 2 — Z-Transform of Second Term** $(1.2)^n u[-n-1]$:

Recall that for a left-sided sequence $-a^n u[-n-1]$, the Z-Transform is $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ with ROC $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$. Here $a = 1.2$:

$$X_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}_2: \vert z \vert < 1.2$$

**Step 3 — Total Z-Transform:**

$$X(z) = X_1(z) + X_2(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} + \frac{1}{1 - 1.2 z^{-1}}$$

**ROC:** $\text{ROC}_1 \cap \text{ROC}_2 = \{z : 0.8 < \vert z \vert < 1.2\}$

**Step 4 — Check DTFT Existence:**

The overall ROC is the annular region $0.8 < \vert z \vert < 1.2$. Since the unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) lies within this region:

$$0.8 < 1 < 1.2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The DTFT **exists** for this sequence.

**DTFT Expression:**

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.8 e^{-j\omega}} + \frac{1}{1 - 1.2 e^{-j\omega}}$$

**[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check ROC** before substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$. Marks are deducted if the existence of the DTFT is not verified first.
> 2. **Confusing ROC boundary conditions.** For bilateral sequences, always take the **intersection** of individual ROCs.
> 3. **Sign errors in polar substitution.** A common mistake is writing $z = r e^{-j\omega}$ instead of $z = r e^{j\omega}$. The correct convention is $z = r e^{j\omega}$.
> 4. **Misidentifying stability.** BIBO stability requires ROC $\supseteq$ unit circle, NOT ROC = unit circle. The ROC is a 2-D region, and the unit circle is a 1-D boundary.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\boldsymbol{2\pi}$ factor** in inverse DTFT expressions. Always state both forward and inverse forms correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Core Equation to Memorize:** $X(e^{j\omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$
- 🎯 **Substitution Rule:** Replace every $z$ in $X(z)$ with $e^{j\omega}$ to obtain $X(e^{j\omega})$.
- 🎯 **Existence Condition:** The ROC of $X(z)$ **must contain** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- 🎯 **Stability Link:** A causal LTI system is BIBO stable iff all its poles lie inside the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).
- 🎯 **Polar Form of z:** $z = r e^{j\omega}$, where $r = \vert z \vert$ and $\omega = \arg(z)$.
- 🎯 **Unit Circle:** The locus $\vert z \vert = 1$ corresponds to the range $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
- 🎯 **DTFT is $2\pi$-Periodic:** $X(e^{j(\omega + 2\pi)}) = X(e^{j\omega})$ — important when plotting or interpreting spectra.
- 🎯 **Causal Sequence ROC:** Always of the form $\vert z \vert > R_{\text{outer}}$ (exterior of a circle).
- 🎯 **Anti-causal Sequence ROC:** Always of the form $\vert z \vert < R_{\text{inner}}$ (interior of a circle).
- 🎯 **Two-Sided Sequence ROC:** Always an annulus $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$.
- 🎯 **No Poles in ROC:** The ROC is bounded by poles; it never contains any pole of $X(z)$.
- 🎯 **Quick Test for DTFT Existence:** For a causal $a^n u[n]$ sequence, DTFT exists if and only if $\vert a \vert < 1$.
- 🎯 **Hierarchy to Remember:** Fourier Transform ⊂ Laplace Transform; DTFT ⊂ Z-Transform. Both Fourier-type transforms are *boundary evaluations* of their more general counterparts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Analysis of LTI Systems Using Transforms

**LTI Systems** (Linear Time-Invariant Systems) form the backbone of modern signal processing, control engineering, and communications. The transform-domain approach converts complex **convolution operations in the time domain** into simple **algebraic multiplications in the frequency/s-domain**, making system analysis tractable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **LTI system** is completely characterized in the transform domain by its **Transfer Function** $H(s)$ (Laplace) or $H(j\omega)$ (Fourier). The output $Y(s)$ is obtained as the product of the input $X(s)$ and the transfer function: $Y(s) = H(s) \cdot X(s)$.

The two principal transforms used for LTI system analysis are:
- **Laplace Transform** — handles a broader class of signals (including unstable/causal systems), used in control systems and stability analysis.
- **Fourier Transform** — restricts to absolutely integrable/stable signals, used in frequency analysis and filtering.

## 1.2 The Transfer Function — Formal Definition

For a **causal LTI system** with impulse response $h(t)$, the **Transfer Function** $H(s)$ is defined as the **Laplace Transform of the impulse response**:

$$H(s) = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t) e^{-st} \, dt$$

Equivalently, in terms of input and output:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

where $X(s) = \mathcal{L}\{x(t)\}$ and $Y(s) = \mathcal{L}\{y(t)\}$, assuming the system is initially at rest (zero initial conditions).

When we substitute $s = j\omega$, we obtain the **Frequency Response** $H(j\omega)$, which is the Fourier transform of $h(t)$:

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t) e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Audio Equalizer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The DJ's Mixing Console:**
> Imagine an **audio equalizer** in a music studio. The DJ sends in a raw audio signal $x(t)$ (the input), and the equalizer acts as a **filter** that amplifies or attenuates different **frequency components** (bass, mid, treble) before sending out the modified signal $y(t)$ (the output).
> 
> - The **Transfer Function** $H(s)$ is the *instruction manual* of the equalizer — it tells you exactly how the device will behave.
> - The **Frequency Response** $H(j\omega)$ is the equalizer's *frequency curve* — at each frequency $\omega$, how much will the signal be amplified?
> - The **Magnitude Response** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ is the *loudness knob* setting at each frequency.
> - The **Phase Response** $\angle H(j\omega)$ is the *time delay* introduced to each frequency component.

Just as a trained sound engineer can predict the output sound simply by reading the equalizer's curve, an engineer can fully characterize an LTI system from $H(s)$ alone — no need to convolve in the time domain!

## 1.4 Frequency Response, Magnitude Response & Phase Response

Given a transfer function $H(s)$ with $s = j\omega$, the **Frequency Response** is a complex-valued function:

$$H(j\omega) = \text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\} + j\,\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}$$

This complex number can be represented in **polar form**:

$$H(j\omega) = \vert H(j\omega) \vert \, e^{j \angle H(j\omega)}$$

where:
- $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{[\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2 + [\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2}$ → **Magnitude Response**
- $\angle H(j\omega) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}}{\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}}\right)$ → **Phase Response**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are these responses important?**
> - **Magnitude Response** tells us *how much* each frequency is amplified or attenuated → determines the **filtering behavior** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, etc.).
> - **Phase Response** tells us *how much* each frequency is delayed in time → critical for **signal fidelity** (distortionless transmission requires linear phase).
> - Together, they form the **Bode Plot**, the most important tool in control and filter design.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Steps to Analyze an LTI System Using Transforms

The standard KTU-board approach involves the following logical sequence:

1. **Identify the system's impulse response** $h(t)$ from the governing differential equation (taking zero initial conditions).
2. **Take the Laplace Transform** of $h(t)$ (or apply transform to the differential equation) to obtain the **Transfer Function** $H(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$.
3. **Substitute** $s = j\omega$ to obtain the **Frequency Response Function** $H(j\omega)$.
4. **Compute the Magnitude** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ and the **Phase** $\angle H(j\omega)$ separately.
5. **Convert to decibels (dB)** for magnitude if required: $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$.
6. **Plot** magnitude and phase vs. $\omega$ to obtain the **Bode Plot**.

## 2.2 Pole-Zero Form of the Transfer Function

A general **N-th order LTI system** has a transfer function in rational form:

$$H(s) = \frac{b_0 s^M + b_1 s^{M-1} + \cdots + b_M}{a_0 s^N + a_1 s^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N}$$

In **factored (pole-zero) form**:

$$H(s) = K \cdot \frac{(s - z_1)(s - z_2) \cdots (s - z_M)}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2) \cdots (s - p_N)}$$

- **Zeros** $\{z_i\}$ — values of $s$ where $H(s) = 0$ (signal nulls).
- **Poles** $\{p_i\}$ — values of $s$ where $H(s) = \infty$ (system resonances).
- $K = b_0 / a_0$ — **DC gain / scaling factor**.

## 2.3 Stability Criterion (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIBO Stability Condition:**
> An LTI system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if **all poles of $H(s)$ lie strictly in the left-half of the s-plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}\{p_i\} < 0$ for all $i$).
> 
> Equivalently, the impulse response must be **absolutely integrable**: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Transfer Function Analysis

| Engineering Field | Application of $H(j\omega)$ |
|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Control Systems** | Designing compensators, stability margins (gain/phase margin) |
| **Audio Processing** | Equalizers, noise-cancellation headphones |
| **Telecommunications** | Channel equalization, modulation/demodulation filters |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG signal filtering to remove noise |
| **Radar/Sonar** | Matched filtering, doppler shift detection |
| **Power Systems** | Harmonic filtering, resonance suppression |

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table — these formulas are asked repeatedly in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Note |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Transfer Function (Laplace) | $H(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\}$ | Zero initial conditions |
| 2 | Frequency Response | $H(j\omega) = H(s)\big\vert_{s=j\omega} = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$ | Exists only for stable systems |
| 3 | Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{[\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2 + [\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}]^2}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 4 | Phase Response | $\angle H(j\omega) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}\{H(j\omega)\}}{\text{Re}\{H(j\omega)\}}\right)$ | In radians (or degrees) |
| 5 | Magnitude in dB | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | Used in Bode plots |
| 6 | Magnitude of Product | $\vert H_1 H_2 \vert = \vert H_1 \vert \cdot \vert H_2 \vert$ | Magnitudes multiply |
| 7 | Phase of Product | $\angle(H_1 H_2) = \angle H_1 + \angle H_2$ | Phases add |
| 8 | Magnitude of Quotient | $\left\vert \dfrac{H_1}{H_2} \right\vert = \dfrac{\vert H_1 \vert}{\vert H_2 \vert}$ | Magnitudes divide |
| 9 | Phase of Quotient | $\angle\!\left(\dfrac{H_1}{H_2}\right) = \angle H_1 - \angle H_2$ | Phases subtract |
| 10 | Magnitude of $j\omega + a$ | $\sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}$ | Right-triangle magnitude |
| 11 | Phase of $j\omega + a$ | $\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$ | For $a > 0$ |
| 12 | Phase of $j\omega - a$ | $\pi - \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{a}\right)$ | For $a > 0$ |
| 13 | Phase of pure $j\omega$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ | Constant $90°$ |
| 14 | Group Delay | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(j\omega)$ | Measure of phase linearity |
| 15 | BIBO Stability | $\text{Re}\{p_i\} < 0$ for all poles | All poles in LHP |
